personal manual · programming
Cherry Code
— A Code Diary —
A personal reference manual for learning programming — core concepts, multi-language examples, zero fluff.
NameKaylla Dearo
StartedApril 23
GoalCybersecurity Expert
9 blocks · 98 terms · 3 languages
First Quiz Block 1 Mastered 50 Studied 10 Favorites
cherry code › how to use
How to Use
A guide to getting the most out of this notebook.
01
Read the concept first, code second
Each term explains the concept in plain language before showing code. Understanding what something IS comes before knowing how to write it.
02
Compare languages side by side
Each term shows the same concept in Python, JavaScript, and Java. Notice the patterns — most languages share core concepts but differ in syntax.
03
Try every snippet
Open python3, your browser console (F12), or an online compiler. Reading code is passive — typing and seeing output is when learning sticks.
04
Use tags to track progress
Mark each term as Studied, Review, Hard, or Favorite. Use Review Mode for spaced repetition of difficult concepts.
05
Make mistakes, read errors
Error messages are not failures — they are information. Every error you read teaches the language better than working code does. Embrace bugs.
cherry code › study paths
Study Paths
Curated sequences through the notebook. Pick a path and follow the terms in order.
Total Beginner
Never written code before. A gentle, ordered route through the absolute essentials — fundamentals, basic types, operators, control flow, your first functions and data structures.
★☆☆☆☆19 terms·~3 hours
F·01F·02F·06F·07T·01T·02T·03T·04T·06O·01O·02C·01C·02C·05N·01N·02N·04S·01S·02
Python Focus
A complete front-to-back run through every concept block, ideal if Python is your first language. Cover fundamentals, types, operators, flow, functions, structures and error handling in sequence.
★★☆☆☆full blocks·~8 hours
Fund›Types›Ops›Flow›Func›Structs›Errors
Interview Prep
Focused on what technical interviews probe: data structures, object-oriented design, error handling, and complexity analysis. Emphasis on the structures block plus OOP and Big-O.
★★★★☆~30 terms·~6 hours
Structs›Big-O›OOP›Errors
Speedrun
One key term from each of the nine blocks — a fast orientation tour that touches every major area without the full depth. Use it to map the territory before diving in.
★★☆☆☆9 terms·~1 hour
F·01›T·01›O·01›C·01›N·01›S·01›P·01›E·01›B·01
cherry code › install app
Install App
Cherry Code works offline as an installable app. Add it to your home screen or desktop for a full-screen, no-browser experience.
iOS — Safari
1
Open this page in Safari (not Chrome — only Safari can install on iOS).
2
Tap the Share button (the square with an upward arrow) in the toolbar.
3
Scroll down and tap Add to Home Screen.
4
Confirm the name "Cherry Code" and tap Add. The icon appears on your home screen.
Android — Chrome
1
Open this page in Chrome.
2
Tap the ⋮ menu (three dots) in the top-right corner.
3
Tap Install app or Add to Home screen.
4
Confirm. Cherry Code installs as a standalone app and runs offline.
Desktop — Chrome / Edge
1
Open this page in Chrome or Edge.
2
Look for the install icon (a monitor with a downward arrow) at the right end of the address bar.
3
Click it, then click Install. Cherry Code opens in its own window and is available offline.

Block 2 · Types & Variables
T·01VariableA named container for data
T·02Data TypeThe kind of value
T·03StringText data

T·05FloatDecimal numbers
T·06BooleanTrue or false

Block 3 · Operators & Expressions
O·01Arithmetic OperatorsMath on numbers
O·02Comparison OperatorsCompare two values
O·03Logical OperatorsCombine boolean conditions
O·04Assignment OperatorsAssign or update a variable

O·07Increment / DecrementAdd or subtract 1
O·08Operator PrecedenceOrder of operations in code

Block 4 · Control Flow
C·01If StatementRun code only when condition is true
C·02Else / ElifAlternative branches

C·04While LoopRepeat while condition holds
C·05For LoopRepeat a counted number of times
C·06For-Each LoopIterate over a collection
C·07BreakExit a loop early
C·08ContinueSkip to the next iteration
C·09Ternary OperatorInline if-else expression
C·10Nested LoopsA loop inside a loop

N·01FunctionNamed, reusable block of code

N·06RecursionA function that calls itself

N·08Lambda / ArrowAnonymous inline function
N·09Default ArgumentsFallback values for parameters
N·10*args / **kwargsVariable-length arguments

P·04AttributeData stored on an object

P·06ConstructorInitializes a new object
P·07InheritanceClass inherits from another class
P·08PolymorphismSame interface, different behavior
P·09EncapsulationHiding internal state

E·05BreakpointPause execution for inspection

B·04DRY PrincipleDon't Repeat Yourself
B·05SOLID PrinciplesFive OOP design guidelines

cherry code › glossary
Glossary
Quick definitions for every term in the notebook.
A
AbstractionExposing only the essential parts of a system while hiding the rest.
ArgumentThe concrete value passed to a function when calling it.
Arithmetic OperatorsSymbols that perform math: + - * / % **.
ArrayOrdered collection of values, accessed by numeric index starting at 0.
Assignment OperatorsSymbols that store a value in a variable, like = += -=.
AttributeA named piece of data belonging to an object.
B
Big O NotationA way to describe how an algorithm scales with input size.
Bitwise OperatorsOperators that work on the individual bits of integers.
BooleanA type with only two possible values: true or false.
BreakA statement that exits a loop immediately.
BreakpointA marker telling the debugger to pause at a specific line.
BugAny mistake in code causing wrong behavior or crashes.
C
ClassA blueprint describing the shape and behavior of objects.
ClosureA function bundled with references to its surrounding state.
CodeText written in a programming language, instructions for a computer.
Code ReviewPeers reading each other's code before it is merged.
CommentsNotes inside source code that explain intent to humans.
Comparison OperatorsOperators that compare two values and return a boolean.
CompilerConverts source code to machine code before running.
ConditionalA statement that runs code only when a condition is true.
ConstantA variable whose value cannot be changed after declaration.
ConstructorSpecial method that runs when a new object is created.
ContinueA statement that skips the rest of the current loop iteration.
D
DebugThe process of finding and fixing bugs in code.
DebuggerA tool to step through code one line at a time.
Default ArgumentsPre-set parameter values used when no argument is passed.
DictCollection of key-value pairs; called dict (Python), object (JS), map (Java).
Dictionary / MapA collection of key-to-value pairs with fast lookup.
DocumentationWriting aimed at the next person who reads or uses the code.
Do-While LoopA loop that always runs once and then checks its condition.
DRY PrincipleDon't Repeat Yourself — every fact lives in one authoritative place.
E
Edge CaseAn unusual input that breaks code which handles common cases fine.
ElseThe branch taken when the if-condition is false.
EncapsulationHiding internal state behind a controlled interface.
ExceptionAn unexpected error that disrupts normal execution.
F
FloatA number with a decimal point.
For LoopA loop driven by a counter or by iteration over a collection.
FunctionA reusable named block of code that performs a task.
Function ScopeVariables declared in a function exist only inside it.
G
GraphNodes connected by edges, representing arbitrary relationships.
H
Hash TableArray-based structure that maps keys to slots via a hash function.
Higher-Order FunctionsFunctions that take or return other functions.
I
IDEIntegrated Development Environment — software that bundles coding tools.
If StatementA conditional that runs a block when its expression is true.
IndentationLeading whitespace that shows the nesting level of code.
InheritanceA subclass reusing and extending a superclass's members.
InstanceA specific object created from a class.
IntegerA whole number, no decimals.
InterfaceA contract listing methods a class promises to implement.
InterpreterReads and executes code line by line at runtime.
L
Lambda / Arrow FunctionA short, anonymous function expression.
Linked ListA chain of nodes where each points to the next.
LinterA static tool that flags errors and style issues before runtime.
ListA dynamic, resizable, ordered collection of items.
Logical OperatorsOperators that combine boolean conditions: AND, OR, NOT.
LoopA construct that repeats code while a condition holds.
M
Match / SwitchA control structure for branching by value against many cases.
MethodA function attached to a class, operating on its instances.
N
Naming ConventionsAgreed-upon rules for how to name identifiers in code.
Nested ConditionalsConditionals placed inside other conditionals.
NullRepresents the absence of a value. Also called None (Python), nil.
O
ObjectA concrete bundle of state and behavior built from a class.
Operator PrecedenceThe order in which operators are evaluated in an expression.
P
ParadigmA style of programming: imperative, OOP, functional.
ParameterA named placeholder for input listed in a function's definition.
PolymorphismSame method name behaving differently depending on the object.
print / console.logQuick debugging by inspecting variable values at runtime.
ProgrammingWriting instructions a computer can execute.
Pure FunctionA function with no side effects: same inputs always give same outputs.
Q
QueueFIFO collection — items leave in the order they were added.
R
RecursionA function that calls itself on a smaller subproblem.
RefactorImproving the structure of code without changing its behavior.
Return ValueThe value a function gives back to its caller.
RuntimeThe period when code is actually executing.
S
ScopeDefines where a variable is accessible in code.
self / thisReference to the current instance inside a method.
SetAn unordered collection of unique items.
SOLID PrinciplesFive OOP design principles for flexible, maintainable systems.
StackLIFO collection — last item added is the first removed.
Stack TraceThe chain of function calls active at the moment of an error.
StringText data — letters, words, sentences enclosed in quotes.
String OperatorsOperators that combine or repeat string values.
SyntaxThe grammar rules of a programming language.
Syntax vs Runtime ErrorParse-time errors versus errors raised while the program runs.
T
TerminalText-based interface for running commands.
Ternary OperatorA compact one-line conditional expression.
TestingAutomated checks that verify code behaves as expected.
Throw / RaiseDeliberately signal an error from your own code.
TreeA hierarchical structure with one root and child nodes.
Try / CatchWrap risky code so errors can be handled gracefully.
TupleAn immutable, ordered sequence of fixed length.
TypeThe kind of value a variable holds: int, string, boolean, etc.
Type CastingConverting a value from one type to another.
V
VariableA named container that holds a value.
Variadic Arguments (*args / **kwargs)Parameters that accept any number of inputs.
Version ControlA system that records every change made to a codebase.
W
While LoopA loop that repeats as long as a condition is true.

1
Block 1 · Fundamentals
The Core Concepts
Before syntax, the ideas. What is code? What is a bug? What is the difference between writing code and running it? Get these right and everything else clicks.
ProgrammingCodeBug IDETerminalSyntax
10 terms · F·01 → F·10 start here
cherry code › F·01
Programming
Writing instructions a computer can execute.
F·01programmingbasics
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:Start here — no prerequisites.
① Definition
"Programming is telling a computer what to do, step by step."
Code is a precise sequence of instructions in a language the computer can interpret. Every app, website, and game is fundamentally a series of programming instructions.
② Explanation

At its core, programming is the act of translating a human intention into a form a machine can carry out without ambiguity. Computers cannot guess or improvise — they follow instructions literally, which means the programmer must think through every step, every edge case, and every order of operations in advance.

This precision is what makes programming both powerful and unforgiving. A single misplaced character can change behavior entirely. Good programming is less about typing fast and more about decomposing a fuzzy real-world problem into small, clear, ordered steps that a CPU can execute reliably millions of times.

③ Where It Appears
Building a website or mobile app
Automating a repetitive spreadsheet task
Scripting data analysis in a notebook
Writing firmware for embedded devices
④ How It Works
You write code → computer reads it → executes the steps one by one. Programs solve problems by breaking them into small, ordered instructions.
⑤ Code Example
# Your first program
print("Hello, World!")
// Your first program
console.log("Hello, World!");
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.println("Hello, World!");
  }
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Vague# "make it work somehow" # no clear ordered steps
✓ Precise# 1. read input 2. validate # 3. compute 4. print result
Why: a computer follows instructions literally. Decompose the problem into explicit ordered steps before writing code.
❌ Assumingprint("done") # before the work runs
✓ Ordereddo_work() print("done")
Why: statements execute top to bottom. Order of operations is part of the logic, not a detail.
⑥ Remember
Code is communication — between you and the computer, AND between you and future you (or your team).
FAQ
Why does programming exist?
Computers are general-purpose machines that do nothing until instructed. Programming is the means by which we specify those instructions to automate work, solve problems, and build software.
When will I use it?
Any time you want a machine to perform a task repeatedly or precisely — from a one-line script to a full application. It underlies every app, website, and automated system.
Is it hard?
The core idea — giving ordered, unambiguous instructions — is simple. Difficulty comes from breaking fuzzy problems into precise steps, which improves steadily with practice.
Do I need to be good at math?
No. Programming relies more on logical decomposition and clear thinking than on advanced mathematics. Many domains require little math beyond basic arithmetic.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·02 CodeF·08 SyntaxT·01 Variable
cherry code › F·02
Code
The text we write in a programming language.
F·02codesource
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·01 Programming
① Definition
"Code is the text of a program — written in a specific language with strict rules."
Source code is the human-readable version of a program. Tools (interpreters/compilers) convert it into instructions the machine actually runs. Code lives in files with extensions like .py, .js, .java.
② Explanation

Code is the concrete written form of a program — the actual characters, keywords, and symbols a developer types into a file. While programming is the activity, code is the artifact it produces, and it is what gets stored, shared, versioned, and reviewed by teams.

Because code is read far more often than it is written, clarity matters as much as correctness. Naming, spacing, and structure all communicate intent to other humans, while the machine only cares about whether the code is valid. Source code is later transformed into something the computer can actually run.

③ Where It Appears
Files stored in a Git repository
Pull requests reviewed by teammates
Snippets shared on Stack Overflow
Open-source libraries on GitHub
④ How It Works
You write code in a text editor → save it as a file → run it with the right tool. Code can be a single line or millions of lines across thousands of files.
⑤ Code Example
# File: greet.py
name = "Cherry"
print(f"Hello, {name}!")
// File: greet.js
const name = "Cherry";
console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`);
// File: Greet.java
String name = "Cherry";
System.out.println("Hello, " + name);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Cryptic namesx = "a@b.com" y = f(x)
✓ Intent-revealingemail = "a@b.com" domain = extract_domain(email)
Why: code is read far more than written. Names are the cheapest documentation you can give the next reader.
❌ Wrong extension# script.txt — won't run as Python
✓ Correct file# script.py — interpreter recognizes it
Why: the file extension signals the language to tools. The text inside is still code, but the runner needs the right file.
⑥ Remember
Code is text. That is it. Files of text that follow strict rules.
FAQ
Why does source code exist as text?
Text is human-readable, easy to edit, version, and review. Tools then translate that text into the binary instructions the machine executes.
When will I use it?
Constantly — every file you write, store in version control, or open in an editor is source code.
Is it hard?
Writing valid code is a learnable skill. Writing clear, maintainable code is the longer-term craft.
What's the difference between code and a program?
Code is the written artifact. A program is what you get when that code is run by an interpreter or compiler.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·08 SyntaxF·03 Compiler vs InterpreterF·06 IDE
cherry code › F·03
Compiler vs Interpreter
Two ways to turn code into something the computer runs.
F·03compilerinterpreter
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·01 ProgrammingF·02 Code
① Definition
"Compilers translate everything before running. Interpreters translate line by line as they go."
A compiler converts your whole program into machine code once, then runs the result (fast, but slower to start). An interpreter reads and executes line by line (slower at runtime, but instant feedback). Java is compiled. Python and JavaScript are interpreted.
② Explanation

The fundamental difference is timing. A compiler translates the entire source file into machine code before the program runs, producing a standalone executable. An interpreter reads and executes the code line by line while the program runs, with no separate build step.

This trade-off shapes the developer experience. Compiled languages like C and Java tend to run faster and catch many errors up front, but require a build step. Interpreted languages like Python and JavaScript start instantly and are easier to experiment with, at the cost of slower execution and errors that only surface at runtime.

③ Where It Appears
Choosing Python (interpreted) for quick scripting
Compiling a C++ game for performance
Running JavaScript directly in a browser
Java's hybrid bytecode + JIT approach
④ How It Works
Compiler: source → executable file → runs.
Interpreter: source → translated AND run line by line.
Modern languages mix both (JIT compilation).
⑤ Code Example
# Interpreted — run directly
# $ python script.py
print("runs line by line")
// Interpreted by the browser/Node
// No compile step needed
console.log("runs immediately");
// Compiled FIRST: javac Main.java
// THEN run: java Main
System.out.println("compiled then run");
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Skipping the build// Java: editing Main.java then // running the OLD compiled class
✓ Recompile first// javac Main.java // java Main
Why: compiled languages run the build artifact, not the source. Forgetting to recompile runs stale code.
❌ Expecting compile errors# Python: typo only fails when # that line actually runs
✓ Test the path# run/exercise each branch so # interpreted errors surface
Why: interpreters report many errors only at runtime, so untested lines can hide bugs.
⑥ Remember
Python/JS = type and run. Java = compile, then run. Both end up doing the same thing eventually.
FAQ
Why do both approaches exist?
They trade off speed against flexibility. Compilation favors fast execution and early error detection; interpretation favors instant feedback and portability.
When will I use this knowledge?
When choosing a language for a project, diagnosing why an error appears late, or understanding why a build step is required.
Is it hard?
The concept is straightforward. The nuance — JIT, bytecode, hybrid models — comes later and is optional at first.
Is Python purely interpreted?
Not exactly. CPython compiles source to bytecode first, then interprets that bytecode. The line between the two is blurrier than the names suggest.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·09 RuntimeF·04 BugF·08 Syntax
cherry code › F·04
Bug
When code does not do what you intended.
F·04bugerror
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·01 ProgrammingF·02 Code
① Definition
"A bug is any mistake in code that causes wrong behavior or crashes."
Bugs are unavoidable — every programmer creates them. The skill is not avoiding bugs, but FINDING and FIXING them quickly. Types: syntax errors (won't even run), runtime errors (crashes mid-execution), logic bugs (runs but wrong output).
② Explanation

A bug is the gap between what the programmer intended and what the code actually does. Bugs are not random — every bug has a concrete cause in the logic, and the computer is always doing exactly what it was told, just not what the developer meant.

Bugs range from harmless cosmetic glitches to crashes and security vulnerabilities. The earlier a bug is caught — ideally while writing or testing — the cheaper it is to fix. This is why testing, code review, and careful reading are central to professional development.

③ Where It Appears
A button that does nothing when clicked
A calculation returning the wrong total
An app crashing on certain input
A security flaw letting attackers in
④ How It Works
You wrote something the way you meant — but the computer reads exactly what you typed. Bug = gap between what you typed and what you meant.
⑤ Code Example
# BUG: wrong operator
age = 18
if age = 18:  # SyntaxError: should be ==
    print("adult")
// BUG: typo causes ReferenceError
let name = "Cherry";
console.log(nme);  // nme is not defined
// BUG: array out of bounds
int[] nums = {1, 2, 3};
System.out.println(nums[5]);  // crashes!
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Assignment in conditionif age = 18:
✓ Comparisonif age == 18:
Why: = assigns, == compares. Mixing them is one of the most common beginner bugs.
❌ Out-of-range indexnums = [1, 2, 3] print(nums[5])
✓ Valid indexif 5 < len(nums): print(nums[5])
Why: indexing past the end crashes at runtime. Bounds-check or iterate instead of guessing positions.
⑥ Remember
Every senior dev still creates bugs daily. They just got faster at finding them.
FAQ
Why do bugs exist?
Because there is always a gap between what a programmer intends and what they literally wrote. The computer executes the literal version.
When will I deal with them?
Constantly. Most development time goes to finding and fixing bugs rather than writing new code.
Is avoiding bugs hard?
Eliminating them entirely is unrealistic. The practical skill is finding and fixing them quickly, which improves with experience.
What are the main kinds of bugs?
Syntax errors (won't run), runtime errors (crash mid-execution), and logic bugs (run but produce wrong output).
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·05 DebugF·08 SyntaxF·09 Runtime
cherry code › F·05
Debug
Finding and fixing bugs.
F·05debugfix
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·04 BugF·07 Terminal
① Definition
"Debugging is the process of investigating WHY code misbehaves and fixing it."
Steps: 1) Reproduce the bug consistently. 2) Read the error message (it usually tells you exactly what is wrong). 3) Print intermediate values to find where logic diverges. 4) Fix and test. Most time programming is debugging, not writing.
② Explanation

Debugging is the disciplined process of locating why code misbehaves and then correcting it. It is detective work: forming a hypothesis about the cause, testing it, and narrowing down the problem until the exact faulty line is found.

Effective debugging relies on tools and techniques — breakpoints, print statements, log inspection, and step-through execution. Developers often spend more time debugging than writing new code, so building strong debugging instincts is one of the most valuable programming skills.

③ Where It Appears
Setting breakpoints in an IDE
Reading a stack trace after a crash
Adding print/log statements to trace flow
Using browser dev tools on a web page
④ How It Works
Add prints/logs around suspected code → run → see what values actually flow through → spot the mismatch with what you expected → fix.
⑤ Code Example
# Add print to debug
result = total / count
print(f"DEBUG: total={total}, count={count}")
# Oh! count is 0 → ZeroDivisionError
// console.log() is your friend
const result = total / count;
console.log("DEBUG:", { total, count });
// → { total: 100, count: 0 }
// Use println for quick debug
System.out.println("DEBUG total: " + total);
System.out.println("DEBUG count: " + count);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Guessing blindly# change random lines # and re-run, hoping
✓ Inspect valuesprint("total =", total) # compare to expectation
Why: debugging is investigation, not trial and error. Confirm what the code actually does before changing it.
❌ Ignoring the trace# scroll past the error, # rewrite from scratch
✓ Read line + type# TypeError on line 12 — # go straight there
Why: the error message and stack trace usually name the file, line, and cause. Reading it first saves the most time.
⑥ Remember
Read the error message FIRST. It is the most important sentence on the screen.
FAQ
Why is debugging a distinct skill?
Writing code and diagnosing why it misbehaves are different activities. Debugging is systematic investigation: reproduce, observe, isolate, fix.
When will I use it?
Every time code does not behave as expected — which is often. It is among the most-used skills in daily development.
Is it hard?
It can be frustrating early on, but a disciplined approach — reproduce, read the error, inspect values — makes it methodical rather than mysterious.
Print statements or a debugger?
Both are valid. Prints are quick and universal; a step debugger is more powerful for complex state. Use whichever fits the situation.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·06 IDEF·09 RuntimeE·01 Errors
cherry code › F·06
IDE
Integrated Development Environment — your coding workshop.
F·06IDEeditor
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·02 Code
① Definition
"An IDE is software that bundles tools you need to code: editor, debugger, terminal, file explorer."
Popular IDEs: VS Code (most popular, free, all languages), PyCharm (Python), IntelliJ (Java), WebStorm (JS). They all share: code highlighting, autocomplete, error checking, debugger, integrated terminal.
② Explanation

An IDE bundles everything a developer needs into one application: a code editor, a way to run programs, a debugger, and tools to navigate large projects. It removes the friction of switching between separate tools and keeps focus on the code.

Beyond editing, modern IDEs offer autocomplete, error highlighting as you type, refactoring shortcuts, and integration with version control. These features catch mistakes early and speed up navigation, which is why most professionals work inside one rather than a plain text editor.

③ Where It Appears
Writing Python in VS Code or PyCharm
Java development in IntelliJ IDEA
Step-through debugging with breakpoints
Refactoring and autocomplete in a large codebase
④ How It Works
IDE highlights syntax, suggests completions as you type, marks errors in real time, and lets you run/debug without leaving the window. Optional but transformative for productivity.
⑤ Code Example
# In VS Code with Python extension:
# - Auto-complete as you type
# - Inline error: missing :
# - F5 to run, F9 for breakpoint
// VS Code shows:
// - red squiggle on typos
// - intellisense for methods
// - F12 to "go to definition"
// IntelliJ IDEA:
// - imports auto-added
// - refactor symbol everywhere
// - Shift+F10 to run
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Tool before fundamentals# memorize 50 IDE shortcuts # before writing any code
✓ Code first# learn the language; add # shortcuts as needs arise
Why: the IDE accelerates work but is not the work. Concepts transfer across tools; muscle memory does not.
❌ Trusting autocomplete blindly# accept a suggested method # without knowing what it does
✓ Verify the suggestion# read the signature/docs # before accepting
Why: autocomplete speeds typing, not understanding. Wrong-but-plausible completions introduce subtle bugs.
⑥ Remember
Pick VS Code if unsure. It does everything and is free.
FAQ
Why use an IDE instead of a plain editor?
An IDE integrates editing, running, debugging, and navigation in one place, catching errors as you type and reducing tool-switching friction.
When will I use one?
For most real projects. Plain editors suffice for quick edits, but an IDE pays off as soon as a project has multiple files.
Is it hard to learn?
Basic use is immediate. Advanced features — debugging, refactoring, extensions — are learned gradually as you need them.
Which IDE should a beginner pick?
VS Code is a strong default: free, cross-language, and widely supported. Language-specific IDEs like PyCharm are also excellent.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·07 TerminalF·05 DebugT·01 Variable
cherry code › F·07
Terminal
Text-based interface to run commands.
F·07terminalCLI
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·01 Programming
① Definition
"The terminal is where you type commands instead of clicking buttons."
Essential for developers: run scripts, install packages, use git, deploy apps. Looks intimidating but you only need ~10 commands daily. Windows: PowerShell/Terminal. Mac/Linux: Terminal.
② Explanation

The terminal is a text-based gateway to the computer, where commands are typed instead of clicked. It predates graphical interfaces and remains the fastest, most precise way to control a machine — especially servers, which often have no graphical interface at all.

Mastering the terminal unlocks automation: commands can be chained, scripted, and repeated exactly. Developers use it to run programs, manage files, install tools, and interact with version control, making it an indispensable everyday tool rather than a relic.

③ Where It Appears
Running build and test commands
Managing a remote Linux server via SSH
Installing packages with npm or pip
Navigating files with cd, ls, and grep
④ How It Works
Type a command → press Enter → terminal runs it → shows output. Like talking to your computer in text instead of clicking icons.
⑤ Code Example
# Run a Python file
$ python script.py
# Install a package
$ pip install requests
# Interactive Python
$ python
# Run a JS file with Node
$ node app.js
# Install package
$ npm install express
# Start a project
$ npm start
# Compile a Java file
$ javac Main.java
# Run compiled class
$ java Main
# With Maven
$ mvn run
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrong directory$ python script.py # run from the wrong folder
✓ Check location first$ pwd $ cd project $ python script.py
Why: commands run relative to the current directory. "File not found" usually means you are in the wrong place.
❌ Including the prompt$ $ pip install requests
✓ Command only$ pip install requests
Why: the leading $ in examples represents the prompt, not part of the command. Type only what follows it.
⑥ Remember
The terminal is intimidating for 2 weeks, then it becomes faster than the mouse forever.
FAQ
Why does the terminal still matter?
It is the fastest, most precise way to control a machine, scriptable and available even on servers that have no graphical interface.
When will I use it?
Daily — running scripts, installing packages, using version control, and managing remote machines.
Is it hard?
It looks intimidating at first, but a handful of commands cover most daily work. Comfort comes quickly with use.
Do I have to memorize every command?
No. A small core set handles most tasks, and built-in help plus search cover the rest as needed.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·06 IDEF·05 DebugT·01 Variable
cherry code › F·08
Syntax
The grammar rules of a programming language.
F·08syntaxgrammar
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·02 CodeF·04 Bug
① Definition
"Syntax is the exact way you must write code — wrong syntax = code does not run."
Like spoken languages, programming has rules. Python uses indentation. JavaScript uses { }. Java requires semicolons after every statement. Syntax errors are the easiest bugs to find — the compiler/interpreter literally tells you where.
② Explanation

Syntax is the set of structural rules that define what counts as valid code in a language — the placement of brackets, colons, quotes, and keywords. Just as a sentence needs correct grammar to be understood, code must follow its language's syntax exactly.

When syntax is violated, the compiler or interpreter refuses to run the code and reports a syntax error. These are the most common errors for beginners, but also the easiest to fix because the tool usually points to the exact line and character.

③ Where It Appears
Forgetting a closing bracket or semicolon
Indentation rules in Python
Matching quotes around a string
Syntax errors flagged by the IDE
④ How It Works
Computer parses your code character by character. If anything is wrong (missing bracket, wrong operator), it stops and shows an error pointing to the problem.
⑤ Code Example
# Indentation IS syntax
if age >= 18:
    print("adult")  # 4 spaces required
// Curly braces define blocks
if (age >= 18) {
  console.log("adult");  // ; ends statement
}
// Types declared, semicolons required
if (age >= 18) {
  System.out.println("adult");
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Missing colonif age >= 18 print("adult")
✓ Colon presentif age >= 18: print("adult")
Why: Python requires a colon to open a block. The interpreter reports the exact line, so these are quick to fix.
❌ Mixed indentationif ok: → do_a() do_b() # tab vs spaces
✓ Consistent spacesif ok: do_a() do_b()
Why: in Python, indentation is syntax. Mixing tabs and spaces breaks block structure even when it looks aligned.
⑥ Remember
Syntax errors are the EASY ones. The compiler points right at them.
FAQ
Why is syntax so strict?
Parsers must interpret code unambiguously. Strict rules let the tool determine structure precisely, with no guessing.
When will I encounter syntax errors?
Constantly while learning, and occasionally forever. They are the most common beginner error and the easiest to fix.
Is syntax hard?
No. It is memorized quickly with practice, and the IDE and compiler flag violations immediately, often pointing to the exact character.
Does every language use the same syntax?
No. Concepts are shared, but the rules differ — Python uses indentation, JavaScript uses braces, Java requires semicolons.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·09 RuntimeF·10 ParadigmT·01 Variable
cherry code › F·09
Runtime
When code is actually executing.
F·09runtimeexecution
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·03 Compiler vs InterpreterF·08 Syntax
① Definition
"Runtime is the time when your program is running — opposed to "compile time" when it is just being prepared."
Runtime errors happen during execution: dividing by zero, accessing missing data, network failures. Syntax errors happen BEFORE runtime (won't even start). Logic bugs happen DURING runtime.
② Explanation

Runtime refers to the period when a program is actually running, as opposed to when it is being written or compiled. Many things can only be known at runtime — user input, file contents, network responses — because they don't exist until execution begins.

This distinction matters because some errors and behaviors only appear at runtime, not while writing code. A bug like dividing by zero or accessing missing data may pass syntax checks yet crash the moment the offending line executes with real values.

③ Where It Appears
A crash that only happens on certain input
Reading user-entered data live
Runtime exceptions like null reference errors
Performance measured while the program runs
④ How It Works
Compile time: code is being checked/translated. Runtime: code is actively doing things — reading files, talking to network, calculating. Each has its own type of error.
⑤ Code Example
# Runtime error — found WHEN running
nums = [1, 2, 3]
print(nums[10])
# IndexError: list index out of range
// Runtime error happens at execution
const data = null;
console.log(data.name);
// TypeError: Cannot read properties of null
// Compiles fine, crashes at runtime
int[] nums = {1,2,3};
System.out.println(nums[10]);
// ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ No bounds checknums = [1, 2, 3] print(nums[5]) # crashes at runtime
✓ Guard firstif len(nums) > 5: print(nums[5])
Why: runtime errors surface only with real data. Validate sizes and inputs before you use them.
❌ Access on Nonedata = None print(data.name) # AttributeError
✓ Check before useif data is not None: print(data.name)
Why: the code parses fine, so syntax checks pass — but it breaks the moment a real value is missing.
⑥ Remember
Runtime errors are sneakier than syntax errors — code looks correct but breaks when run.
FAQ
What is the difference between compile time and runtime?
Compile time is when code is checked and translated; runtime is when it actually executes. Some errors only appear at runtime, with real values.
Why didn't my IDE catch this?
IDEs and compilers catch syntax and many type errors, but they cannot know runtime values like user input or file contents until the program runs.
Are runtime errors my fault?
Usually they signal an unhandled case — missing data, bad input, an empty list. They are normal; the fix is to anticipate and handle those cases.
How do I prevent runtime errors?
Validate inputs, check sizes and for None/null before using values, and wrap risky operations in try/except (covered in Block 8).
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:F·10 ParadigmE·01 ExceptionE·02 Try / Catch
cherry code › F·10
Paradigm
A style or approach to programming.
F·10paradigmstyle
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·01 ProgrammingF·02 Code
① Definition
"A paradigm is a way of thinking about code — what to organize around."
Main paradigms: Imperative (do this then this), Object-Oriented (organize around objects), Functional (everything is a function). Most modern languages support multiple paradigms — you choose per problem.
② Explanation

A paradigm is a fundamental way of organizing and thinking about code. Rather than dictating syntax, it shapes how a problem is modeled — whether as objects and their interactions, as a sequence of steps, or as pure functions transforming data.

Common paradigms include procedural, object-oriented, and functional programming. Many modern languages are multi-paradigm, letting developers mix styles. Understanding paradigms helps you choose the right mental model for a problem and read code written in unfamiliar styles.

③ Where It Appears
Object-oriented design with classes
Functional pipelines (map, filter, reduce)
Procedural scripts running top to bottom
Declarative UI frameworks like React
④ How It Works
Same problem can be solved many ways. Imperative: step-by-step instructions. OOP: create objects with state and methods. Functional: chain functions that transform data.
⑤ Code Example
# Same task, 3 paradigms

# Imperative
result = []
for n in nums:
    if n > 0: result.append(n*2)

# Functional
result = [n*2 for n in nums if n > 0]
// Imperative vs Functional
// Imperative
const result = [];
for (let n of nums) {
  if (n > 0) result.push(n*2);
}

// Functional
const result = nums.filter(n => n>0).map(n => n*2);
// OOP-first language
class Doubler {
  int[] doublePositives(int[] nums) {
    // imperative inside method
    return Arrays.stream(nums)
      .filter(n -> n>0)
      .map(n -> n*2).toArray();
  }
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ OOP for a trivial taskclass Adder: def add(self, a, b): return a + b Adder().add(2, 3)
✓ A plain functiondef add(a, b): return a + b add(2, 3)
Why: a paradigm is a tool, not a rule. Forcing OOP onto simple logic adds noise — pick the simplest fit.
❌ "Functional" but mutatesnums.sort() # mutates input result = nums
✓ No side effectsresult = sorted(nums) # new list
Why: functional style avoids side effects. sorted() returns a new list instead of changing the original.
⑥ Remember
No paradigm is "best". Use what fits the problem — most pros mix all three.
FAQ
Which paradigm should I learn first?
Start procedural/imperative (step-by-step) — it maps to how you already think. OOP and functional come naturally once the basics are solid.
Do I have to pick just one?
No. Most modern languages are multi-paradigm, and real code mixes them — objects for structure, functions for transformations.
Is OOP outdated?
No. It is still everywhere. Functional ideas have grown popular alongside it, but they coexist rather than replace each other.
Why do paradigms matter?
They give you mental models for organizing code and let you read unfamiliar codebases written in a different style.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·01 VariableP·01 ClassN·05 Pure Function
2
Block 2 · Types & Variables
The Building Blocks
Every program manipulates data. Variables hold it. Types categorize it. Get comfortable with these and you can read 80% of any program.
VariableTypeString IntArrayDictNull
12 terms · T·01 → T·12 data foundations
cherry code › T·01
Variable
A named container for data.
T·01variablestorage
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·02 CodeF·08 Syntax
① Definition
"A variable is a name that holds a value you can use later."
Variables let you store data and refer to it by name. You can change what they store. Naming matters — `x` tells nothing, `userAge` tells everything.
② Explanation

A variable is a named label attached to a location in memory that holds a value. Instead of working with raw memory addresses, programmers use readable names, and the language tracks where the actual data lives in RAM.

Variables make programs flexible: the same code can operate on different values depending on what's stored. They can be read, updated, and passed around, forming the basic units of state that programs manipulate as they run.

③ Where It Appears
Storing user input from a form
Counters inside loops
Config values like an API key
Accumulating a running total
④ How It Works
Create variable with a name → assign a value to it → use the name later to access the value. You can reassign at any time.
⑤ Code Example
name = "Cherry"
age = 25
active = True

print(name, age)  # Cherry 25
let name = "Cherry";
let age = 25;
let active = true;

console.log(name, age);
String name = "Cherry";
int age = 25;
boolean active = true;

System.out.println(name);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Cryptic namex = 25
✓ Descriptive nameuser_age = 25
Why: names are documentation. A reader (including future you) should know what a value means without comments.
❌ Use before assignprint(total)
✓ Initialize firsttotal = 0 print(total)
Why: referencing a name that was never assigned raises NameError (Python) or ReferenceError (JS).
⑥ Remember
Good variable names are documentation. Use them liberally.
FAQ
Can a variable change type?
In dynamically typed languages (Python, JavaScript) yes — reassign any value. In statically typed ones (Java) the type is fixed at declaration.
Variable vs constant?
A constant is meant never to be reassigned. See T·10 Constant.
Where does the value live?
In RAM while the program runs. It disappears when the program exits unless saved to disk.
Are names case-sensitive?
In most languages yes — age and Age are different variables.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·02 Data TypeT·10 ConstantT·11 Scope
cherry code › T·02
Data Type
The kind of value a variable holds.
T·02typekind
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·01 Variable
① Definition
"A type tells you what kind of data: number, text, true/false, list, etc."
Types determine what operations make sense. Adding numbers makes sense. Adding two booleans is weird. Python is "dynamic" (figures out types automatically). Java is "static" (you declare types).
② Explanation

A data type defines what kind of value a variable holds and what operations are allowed on it. Numbers can be added, strings can be concatenated, and booleans can be combined — the type tells the language how to interpret the raw bits in memory.

Types prevent meaningless operations and help catch bugs early. Some languages enforce types strictly at compile time, while others check them at runtime. Either way, understanding types is essential to predicting how code behaves.

③ Where It Appears
Validating a numeric form field
Choosing a column type in a database
Preventing 'string + number' mistakes
API responses with typed JSON fields
④ How It Works
Each value has a type. Operations on types follow rules. "5" + 3 behavior differs by language — some error, some concatenate, some convert.
⑤ Code Example
# Dynamic typing — auto-detected
age = 25           # int
name = "Cherry"      # str
price = 9.99        # float
active = True       # bool

type(age)             # 
// Dynamic typing
let age = 25;          // number
let name = "Cherry";    // string
let active = true;     // boolean

typeof age;             // "number"
// Static typing — declare types
int age = 25;
String name = "Cherry";
double price = 9.99;
boolean active = true;

// Type is FIXED at declaration
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Mixing types"3" + 4
✓ Cast firstint("3") + 4
Why: adding a string and a number either errors (Python) or concatenates unexpectedly (JS gives "34").
❌ Cross-type compare"5" == 5
✓ Same typeint("5") == 5
Why: comparing different types gives surprising results; convert to a common type first.
⑥ Remember
Type errors are some of the most common bugs. Knowing types prevents many.
FAQ
Why do types matter?
They define which operations are valid and how the value is stored in memory.
Static vs dynamic typing?
Static checks types before running (Java); dynamic checks while running (Python, JavaScript).
How do I check a value's type?
type(x) in Python, typeof x in JavaScript.
Can I convert between types?
Yes — see T·12 Type Casting.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·03 StringT·04 IntegerT·12 Type Casting
cherry code › T·03
String
Text data — letters, words, sentences.
T·03stringtext
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·02 Data Type
① Definition
"A string is text — anything between quotes."
Strings hold any text: a single letter, a word, a paragraph, JSON, HTML. Operations: concatenation (+), length, indexing, slicing, search, replace, upper/lower.
② Explanation

A string is a sequence of characters used to represent text — anything from a single letter to an entire document. Internally it's stored as an ordered collection of character codes, which is why strings can be indexed, sliced, and searched.

Strings are everywhere in software because most human-facing data is text. Languages provide rich operations — concatenation, formatting, splitting, replacing — and care must be taken with encoding and with user-supplied text, which is a common source of security issues.

③ Where It Appears
Displaying messages and labels in a UI
Reading names and emails from a form
Parsing text from a file or API
Building SQL or URLs (watch for injection)
④ How It Works
Wrap text in quotes (single or double) to make a string. Join strings with + or templates (cleaner). Access characters by index starting at 0.
⑤ Code Example
greeting = "Hello"
name = "Cherry"

# Concatenation
msg = greeting + ", " + name

# f-string (best)
msg = f"{greeting}, {name}!"

# Methods
print(name.upper())  # CHERRY
print(len(name))     # 6
const greeting = "Hello";
const name = "Cherry";

// Concatenation
const msg = greeting + ", " + name;

// Template literal (best)
const msg2 = `${greeting}, ${name}!`;

// Methods
name.toUpperCase();  // CHERRY
name.length;            // 6
String greeting = "Hello";
String name = "Cherry";

// Concatenation
String msg = greeting + ", " + name;

// String.format
String msg2 = String.format("%s, %s!", greeting, name);

// Methods
name.toUpperCase();
name.length();
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Mismatched quotesname = "Cherry'
✓ Matched quotesname = "Cherry"
Why: the opening and closing quote characters must match, or you get a syntax error.
❌ Concatenate a number"age: " + 25
✓ Use an f-stringf"age: {25}"
Why: Python will not join a string and an int. f-strings insert values cleanly.
⑥ Remember
Template strings (f-strings, backticks) are way cleaner than + + +. Use them.
FAQ
Are strings mutable?
In Python and Java, no — operations return a new string instead of changing the original.
How do I insert variables?
f-strings in Python (f"hi {name}"), template literals in JS (`hi ${name}`).
Is the first character index 0?
Yes — strings are zero-indexed.
Single or double quotes?
Usually interchangeable. Pick one style and stay consistent.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·04 IntegerT·08 Array / ListT·12 Type Casting
cherry code › T·04
Integer
Whole numbers — no decimals.
T·04intnumber
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·02 Data Type
① Definition
"An integer is a number without a decimal point: 0, 1, -5, 9999."
Used for counting, indexing, IDs, ages — anything discrete. Math operations: +, -, *, /, % (modulo). Integer division varies by language — Python uses // for floor division.
② Explanation

An integer represents a whole number with no fractional part, used for anything that is counted rather than measured. Integers are stored exactly in memory, so arithmetic on them is precise — there's no rounding error.

Because integers have a fixed size in many languages, they can overflow if a value grows beyond the maximum. Choosing integer versus float depends on whether the quantity is discrete (counts, indices, ages) or continuous (prices, measurements).

③ Where It Appears
Loop counters and array indices
Counting items in a cart
User IDs and database keys
Quantities like 'number of attempts'
④ How It Works
Store whole numbers, do math. Be aware: integer overflow exists in some languages (Java int max is ~2.1 billion). Python handles huge integers natively.
⑤ Code Example
age = 25
count = -10
big = 999999999999   # No overflow

# Arithmetic
10 + 3    # 13
10 / 3    # 3.333 (float)
10 // 3   # 3 (floor div)
10 % 3    # 1 (remainder)
let age = 25;
let count = -10;

// JS has only "number" type
// Use Math.floor for int div
10 + 3;            // 13
10 / 3;            // 3.333
Math.floor(10/3); // 3
10 % 3;            // 1
int age = 25;
int count = -10;
long big = 999999999999L;

10 + 3;   // 13
10 / 3;   // 3 (int div!)
10.0 / 3; // 3.333 (double)
10 % 3;   // 1
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Expecting integer7 / 2 # 3.5
✓ Floor division7 // 2 # 3
Why: in Python / always returns a float. Use // for integer (floor) division.
❌ Assuming no limitint x = 2_000_000_000 + 2_000_000_000
✓ Use a wider typelong x = 2_000_000_000L + 2_000_000_000L
Why: fixed-width integers (Java int) wrap around on overflow. Python ints grow unbounded.
⑥ Remember
Integer math: simple but watch for division behavior — Java integer division truncates!
FAQ
Is there a maximum integer?
Python integers are unbounded. Java int is 32-bit (max ~2.1 billion).
Integer vs float?
Integers have no fractional part; floats do. See T·05 Float.
How do I turn a float into an int?
int(3.9) truncates to 3; round(3.9) rounds to 4.
Why does 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3?
That is float behavior, not integer. See T·05 Float.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·05 FloatT·06 BooleanT·12 Type Casting
cherry code › T·05
Float
Numbers with decimals.
T·05floatdecimal
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·04 Integer
① Definition
"A float represents a number with a decimal point: 3.14, -0.5, 99.99."
Used for prices, measurements, scientific calculations. Floats have precision issues — 0.1 + 0.2 often equals 0.30000000000000004. Never use floats for money — use specialized decimal types.
② Explanation

A float (floating-point number) represents values with a fractional part, like 3.14 or -0.5. It stores numbers in a binary scientific-notation form, which lets it cover a huge range of magnitudes with limited memory.

The catch is that floats are approximate: many decimal values cannot be represented exactly in binary, leading to tiny rounding errors. This is why money is often handled with integers (cents) or decimal types rather than raw floats.

③ Where It Appears
Prices and measurements
Scientific and engineering calculations
Percentages and averages
Coordinates in graphics or maps
④ How It Works
Internally, floats use binary scientific notation, which can't represent some decimals exactly. Real-world impact: comparisons fail for "obviously equal" numbers.
⑤ Code Example
price = 9.99
pi = 3.14159

# Precision issue!
print(0.1 + 0.2)
# 0.30000000000000004

# For money, use Decimal
from decimal import Decimal
price = Decimal("9.99")
const price = 9.99;
const pi = 3.14159;

// Same precision issue
console.log(0.1 + 0.2);
// 0.30000000000000004

// Workaround for money
(0.1 + 0.2).toFixed(2);
// "0.30"
double price = 9.99;
double pi = 3.14159;

// Same precision issue
System.out.println(0.1 + 0.2);
// 0.30000000000000004

// For money use BigDecimal
BigDecimal price = new BigDecimal("9.99");
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Exact equality0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3
✓ Compare with toleranceabs((0.1+0.2) - 0.3) < 1e-9
Why: floats store binary approximations, so two "equal" values can differ slightly. Compare within a small tolerance.
❌ Float for moneyprice = 0.10 * 3
✓ Integer cents or Decimalfrom decimal import Decimal price = Decimal("0.10") * 3
Why: rounding errors accumulate. Use integer cents or Decimal for currency.
⑥ Remember
NEVER use float/double for money. Always use Decimal/BigDecimal or store cents as integers.
FAQ
Why are floats imprecise?
They store decimal numbers as binary approximations, so some values cannot be represented exactly.
When should I use Decimal?
For money and any exact decimal math.
float vs double?
double has more precision. Python's float is a double under the hood.
How do I format decimals?
f"{x:.2f}" shows two decimal places.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·06 BooleanT·04 IntegerT·12 Type Casting
cherry code › T·06
Boolean
True or false — only two values.
T·06boollogic
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·02 Data Type
① Definition
"A boolean represents truth: either true or false. That is it."
Booleans drive all decisions in code: if statements, while loops, conditions. They come from comparisons (a > b), checks (list.isEmpty()), or direct assignment.
② Explanation

A boolean holds one of exactly two values — true or false — making it the foundation of all decision-making in code. Every condition in an if statement or loop ultimately reduces to a boolean.

Booleans are produced by comparisons and combined with logical operators (and, or, not) to express complex conditions. Many languages also treat other values as 'truthy' or 'falsy', a convenient shortcut that can also be a subtle source of bugs.

③ Where It Appears
Conditions in if statements
Feature flags turning behavior on/off
Form validation (isValid)
Loop control (keepRunning)
④ How It Works
Comparisons produce booleans. Logical operators combine them: AND (both must be true), OR (at least one true), NOT (flip).
⑤ Code Example
active = True
logged_in = False

# From comparison
is_adult = age >= 18

# Combine
can_enter = active and is_adult
block = not active or banned

# Truthy/Falsy
if name:  # empty string = False
    print("has name")
let active = true;
let loggedIn = false;

// From comparison
const isAdult = age >= 18;

// Combine
const canEnter = active && isAdult;
const block = !active || banned;

// Truthy/Falsy
if (name) {  // "" is falsy
  console.log("has name");
}
boolean active = true;
boolean loggedIn = false;

// From comparison
boolean isAdult = age >= 18;

// Combine
boolean canEnter = active && isAdult;
boolean block = !active || banned;

// Java: strict boolean only
// no truthy/falsy
if (active) { ... }
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Assign in conditionif x = 5:
✓ Compare with ==if x == 5:
Why: = assigns a value; == compares. Python raises a syntax error here; some languages silently assign.
❌ Redundant compareif is_active == True:
✓ Use truthinessif is_active:
Why: a boolean is already true or false; comparing it to True is noise.
⑥ Remember
Booleans are the foundation of all logic. Every if reduces to a boolean.
FAQ
What is truthy and falsy?
Non-empty, non-zero values are truthy. 0, "", empty collections and None are falsy.
What are and / or / not?
Logical operators that combine or invert boolean values.
What is short-circuiting?
and / or stop evaluating as soon as the result is known.
What do comparisons return?
A boolean — 3 > 2 evaluates to True.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·07 Null / NoneT·02 Data Type
cherry code › T·07
Null / None
Representation of "no value".
T·07nullnone
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·06 Boolean
① Definition
"Null/None means: this variable intentionally has NO value."
Different from 0, empty string, or false — those ARE values. Null means absence. Famously called "the billion dollar mistake" because misusing it causes null pointer exceptions.
② Explanation

Null (or None, nil) represents the deliberate absence of a value — a variable that points to nothing. It is distinct from zero or an empty string: those are real values, while null means 'there is no value here at all'.

Null is useful for modeling missing or optional data, but it is also a famous source of crashes — the 'null reference' error happens when code tries to use something that isn't there. Modern languages add safeguards like optional types to tame it.

③ Where It Appears
A database field with no entry yet
An optional function argument left out
API responses with missing fields
Initializing a variable before it has data
④ How It Works
Use it to signal: data not provided, lookup failed, optional field absent. ALWAYS check before using — accessing null.something crashes.
⑤ Code Example
data = None

if data is None:
    print("No data")

# Crash if not checked!
# data.upper()  → AttributeError

# Safe access
name = data or "default"
let data = null;
let missing;  // undefined

if (data === null) {
  console.log("No data");
}

// Optional chaining
const name = data?.name;
// undefined instead of crash

// Nullish coalescing
const v = data ?? "default";
String data = null;

if (data == null) {
  System.out.println("No data");
}

// Crash without check!
// data.length() → NullPointerException

// Better: Optional
Optional<String> opt = Optional.ofNullable(data);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Compare with ==if value == None:
✓ Identity checkif value is None:
Why: is None checks identity, is the idiomatic Python form, and is faster.
❌ Use without guardingname = user.name
✓ Guard firstif user: name = user.name
Why: calling an attribute or method on None raises AttributeError. Check for None before using it.
⑥ Remember
ALWAYS check for null before using. NullPointerException is the #1 runtime crash.
FAQ
null vs undefined in JS?
null is an intentional empty value; undefined means a variable was never set.
Is None falsy?
Yes — if x: is false when x is None.
Why does None cause crashes?
Accessing attributes, indexes, or methods on None raises an error.
How do I provide a default?
x or default in Python, x ?? default in JavaScript.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·08 Array / ListT·09 Dict / Object / Map
cherry code › T·08
Array / List
An ordered collection of values.
T·08arraylist
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·01 VariableT·02 Data Type
① Definition
"An array (or list) holds multiple values in order, accessed by index."
Index starts at 0. Operations: add, remove, get by index, length, iterate. Python lists and JS arrays can hold mixed types. Java arrays are fixed-size with single type — use ArrayList for dynamic.
② Explanation

An array or list stores multiple values in a single, ordered structure, accessed by numeric index. Instead of dozens of separate variables, related data is grouped together and can be processed uniformly with loops.

Order is preserved, so the first element is at index 0 in most languages. Lists are central to almost every program because real data usually comes in collections — and iterating, filtering, and transforming them is a daily task.

③ Where It Appears
A list of search results
Items in a shopping cart
Rows returned from a database query
Collecting values inside a loop
④ How It Works
Each item has a position (index) starting at 0. arr[0] is first, arr[1] is second. Length is total count. Out-of-bounds = crash.
⑤ Code Example
# List = dynamic array
fruits = ["cherry", "apple", "plum"]

fruits[0]              # "cherry"
len(fruits)            # 3

fruits.append("pear")  # add
fruits.remove("apple")# remove

for f in fruits:
    print(f)
const fruits = ["cherry", "apple", "plum"];

fruits[0];               // "cherry"
fruits.length;          // 3

fruits.push("pear");     // add end
fruits.pop();             // remove end
fruits.indexOf("apple");// find

fruits.forEach(f => console.log(f));
// Fixed array
String[] fruits = {"cherry", "apple", "plum"};
fruits[0];               // "cherry"
fruits.length;          // 3

// Dynamic: ArrayList
List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("cherry");
list.remove(0);
list.size();
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Off-by-one indexarr[len(arr)]
✓ Last valid indexarr[len(arr) - 1]
Why: indices run from 0 to length-1. Reading arr[len(arr)] is out of range.
❌ Mutate while iteratingfor x in items: items.remove(x)
✓ Iterate a copyfor x in items[:]: items.remove(x)
Why: changing a list while looping over it skips or repeats elements.
⑥ Remember
Index starts at 0. ALWAYS. Memorize: array of length 3 has indices 0, 1, 2.
FAQ
Are lists zero-indexed?
Yes — the first element is at index 0.
Are they a fixed size?
Python lists grow and shrink. Java arrays have a fixed length.
How do I add an item?
append in Python, push in JavaScript.
Do they keep order?
Yes — lists preserve the order items were added.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·09 Dict / Object / MapT·03 String
cherry code › T·09
Dict / Object / Map
Key-value pairs.
T·09dictobject
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·08 Array / List
① Definition
"A dict (or object, or map) stores values associated with named keys."
Unlike arrays (indexed by number), dicts use any key — usually strings. Perfect for representing structured data: a user has name, email, age. Order may or may not matter depending on language/version.
② Explanation

A dictionary (also called object, map, or hash) stores data as key-value pairs, letting you look up a value by a meaningful name rather than a numeric position. This makes data self-describing and fast to access.

Where arrays answer 'what's at position 3?', dictionaries answer 'what's the value for this key?'. They are the natural way to model records with named fields and underpin formats like JSON used across the web.

③ Where It Appears
Representing a user record (name, email)
Parsing JSON from an API
Counting word frequencies
Config settings keyed by name
④ How It Works
Access by key: user["name"] or user.name. Add new keys anytime. Missing key = error or default depending on access method.
⑤ Code Example
user = {
    "name": "Cherry",
    "age": 25,
    "active": True
}

user["name"]         # "Cherry"
user.get("email")    # None (safe)
user["email"] = "c@x.com"

for key, val in user.items():
    print(key, val)
const user = {
  name: "Cherry",
  age: 25,
  active: true
};

user.name              // "Cherry"
user["name"]           // "Cherry"
user.email             // undefined
user.email = "c@x.com";

for (const [k, v] of Object.entries(user)) {
  console.log(k, v);
}
// HashMap is the typical choice
Map<String, Object> user = new HashMap<>();
user.put("name", "Cherry");
user.put("age", 25);

user.get("name");    // "Cherry"
user.getOrDefault("email", "");

for (var e : user.entrySet()) {
  System.out.println(e.getKey());
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Access a missing keyuser["age"]
✓ Use get with defaultuser.get("age", 0)
Why: bracket access on a missing key raises KeyError. get returns a default instead of crashing.
❌ Assume key existstotal += scores["math"]
✓ Check firstif "math" in scores: total += scores["math"]
Why: verify a key is present before reading it, or supply a default.
⑥ Remember
Dicts are everywhere — JSON, configs, API responses. Master them early.
FAQ
What can be a key?
Usually strings or other hashable, immutable values.
Can keys repeat?
No — assigning to an existing key overwrites its value.
How do I check a key exists?
"key" in d returns true or false.
Dict vs list?
A dict maps keys to values; a list is ordered by numeric index.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·08 Array / ListT·11 Scope
cherry code › T·10
Constant
A variable that cannot change.
T·10constfinal
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·01 Variable
① Definition
"A constant is a value that stays fixed after declaration."
Used for values that should never change: PI, max retries, API URLs, configuration. Trying to reassign = error. Convention: name them in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.
② Explanation

A constant is a named value that is fixed once set and cannot be reassigned. Declaring something constant signals intent — to other developers and to the compiler — that this value should never change during execution.

Constants make code safer and clearer: they prevent accidental reassignment, give meaning to 'magic numbers', and let the language optimize. Using a named constant like MAX_RETRIES is far clearer than scattering the number 5 throughout the code.

③ Where It Appears
Mathematical values like PI
Configuration limits (MAX_USERS)
Fixed API endpoints or keys
Replacing 'magic numbers' with names
④ How It Works
Declare with const (JS), final (Java), or use ALL_CAPS convention (Python). Compiler/interpreter prevents reassignment, catching bugs early.
⑤ Code Example
# Convention only — uppercase
MAX_RETRIES = 3
API_URL = "https://api.cherry.com"
PI = 3.14159

# Python doesn't prevent reassign
# But programmers respect ALL_CAPS

# For real constants: Final from typing
from typing import Final
MAX: Final = 100
const MAX_RETRIES = 3;
const API_URL = "https://api.cherry.com";
const PI = 3.14159;

// Reassignment = error
MAX_RETRIES = 5;  
// TypeError: Assignment to constant

// NOTE: const objects can mutate
// inside, just can't reassign whole
final int MAX_RETRIES = 3;
final String API_URL = "https://api.cherry.com";
final double PI = 3.14159;

// Reassignment = compile error
MAX_RETRIES = 5;
// Cannot assign value to final variable

// static final for true constants
public static final int MAX = 100;
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Reassign a constconst MAX = 10; MAX = 20;
✓ Leave it fixedconst MAX = 10;
Why: reassigning a JS const throws a TypeError. A constant is set once.
❌ Expect enforcementPI = 3.14 # then PI = 3 later
✓ Treat as conventionPI = 3.14 # UPPER_CASE = do not change
Why: Python does not enforce constants. UPPER_CASE naming signals intent to other developers.
⑥ Remember
Use const by default. Switch to let/variable only when value MUST change.
FAQ
Does Python have real constants?
No — it is convention only, written in UPPER_CASE.
Why use constants?
They remove magic numbers and centralize values you might change later.
Does const freeze the object in JS?
No — only the binding is fixed. Use Object.freeze to lock the value.
Where should I define them?
Near the top of the file or module, grouped together.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·11 ScopeT·01 Variable
cherry code › T·11
Scope
Where a variable is accessible.
T·11scopevisibility
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·01 Variable
① Definition
"Scope defines where you can use a variable: global, local to a function, local to a block."
A variable inside a function does not exist outside it. Inside a block { } may also limit scope (in JS/Java). This isolation prevents naming conflicts and bugs.
② Explanation

Scope defines the region of code where a variable can be seen and used. A variable declared inside a function typically lives only there, while one declared at the top level may be visible everywhere.

Scope prevents naming collisions and keeps state contained, which makes large programs manageable. Misunderstanding scope is a frequent source of bugs — using a variable outside where it exists, or accidentally shadowing one with the same name.

③ Where It Appears
Local variables inside a function
Block scope in if/for blocks
Global config visible everywhere
Closures capturing outer variables
④ How It Works
When code asks for a variable, the language looks in current scope → enclosing scope → global. If not found anywhere = error.
⑤ Code Example
x = 10          # global

def my_func():
    y = 20      # local to function
    print(x)   # can read global: 10
    print(y)   # 20

my_func()
print(y)       # NameError!
let x = 10;       // global/module

function myFunc() {
  let y = 20;     // function scope
  if (true) {
    let z = 30;   // block scope
  }
  // z does NOT exist here
}

myFunc();
console.log(y);    // ReferenceError
public class Demo {
  static int x = 10;  // class field
  
  public static void myFunc() {
    int y = 20;       // method scope
    if (true) {
      int z = 30;     // block scope
    }
    // z is gone here
  }
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Read a local outsidedef f(): result = 1 print(result)
✓ Return itdef f(): return 1 print(f())
Why: a local variable does not exist outside its function. Return the value to use it.
❌ Mutate a globaldef add(): global total total += 1
✓ Pass and returndef add(total): return total + 1
Why: hidden global state is hard to trace and test. Prefer passing values in and out.
⑥ Remember
Smaller scope = safer code. Keep variables as local as possible.
FAQ
Local vs global?
A local variable lives inside a function or block; a global is visible across the whole module.
What is shadowing?
A local name hiding an outer variable with the same name.
What is block scope?
In JS, let and const are block-scoped; var is function-scoped.
Why prefer local scope?
It keeps code predictable and easy to test.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·01 VariableT·09 Dict / Object / Map
cherry code › T·12
Type Casting
Converting one type to another.
T·12castconvert
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·02 Data Type
① Definition
"Casting = converting a value from one type to another, like string to integer."
Common: user input is always a string — cast to int/float to do math. Float to int truncates decimals. Some conversions can fail ("abc" to int crashes). Two types: explicit (you ask) and implicit (auto, sometimes surprising).
② Explanation

Type casting is the act of converting a value from one data type to another — turning the string '42' into the integer 42, or a number into text for display. It bridges the gap between types when data arrives in the wrong form.

Casting can be explicit (the programmer requests it) or implicit (the language does it automatically). Implicit conversions are convenient but can hide bugs, such as '5' + 3 producing '53' instead of 8 when a string sneaks into arithmetic.

③ Where It Appears
Converting form input (string) to a number
Formatting a number for display
Parsing JSON values into typed objects
Reading numeric data from a CSV file
④ How It Works
Use built-in functions: int(), str(), float() (Python), Number(), String() (JS), Integer.parseInt() (Java). Validate input first to avoid crashes.
⑤ Code Example
# String to int
age_str = "25"
age = int(age_str)        # 25

# Int to string
num = 42
text = str(num)            # "42"

# Float to int (truncates)
int(3.7)                  # 3

# Fails:
int("abc")
# ValueError: invalid literal
// String to number
const age = Number("25");   // 25
const age2 = parseInt("25"); // 25

// Number to string
const text = String(42);  // "42"

// Implicit coercion (sneaky!)
"5" + 3;                // "53" (string!)
"5" - 3;                // 2 (number!)

Number("abc");              // NaN
// String to int
int age = Integer.parseInt("25");

// Int to string
String text = String.valueOf(42);

// Double to int
double d = 3.7;
int i = (int) d;          // 3

// Throws if invalid:
Integer.parseInt("abc");
// NumberFormatException
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Cast unchecked inputn = int("abc")
✓ Validate or guardtry: n = int(value) except ValueError: n = 0
Why: casting a non-numeric string raises ValueError. Wrap risky casts in try/except.
❌ Rely on implicitmsg = "score: " + 5
✓ Convert explicitlymsg = "score: " + str(5)
Why: explicit conversion is predictable across languages and avoids surprises.
⑥ Remember
User input is ALWAYS string. Cast carefully — wrap in try/catch when source isn't trusted.
FAQ
Implicit vs explicit casting?
Implicit happens automatically; explicit is when you call int(), str(), etc.
Does casting lose data?
It can — int(3.9) truncates to 3.
How do I cast user input safely?
Wrap the cast in try/except and handle the failure.
Does str then int round-trip?
Yes, as long as the string holds a valid number.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·02 Data TypeT·04 IntegerT·05 Float
3
Block 3 · Operators & Expressions
The Logic of Math
Operators are the verbs of programming — they act on values and produce results. Understanding precedence and expression evaluation is the foundation of every calculation your code ever makes.
ArithmeticComparisonLogicalAssignmentPrecedence
10 terms · O·01 → O·10 compute it
cherry code › O·01
Arithmetic Operators
+ − × ÷ — math on numbers.
O·01operatorsmath
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·04 IntegerT·05 Float
① Definition
"Arithmetic operators perform math — addition, subtraction, multiplication, division."
They take numeric operands and return a result. Every language supports the four basic operators: + - * / and usually also exponentiation and modulo.
② Explanation

Arithmetic operators perform the basic mathematical operations — addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division — on numeric values. They are the building blocks of any calculation a program performs.

Their behavior can subtly depend on types: dividing two integers may truncate the result in some languages, while mixing integers and floats promotes to float. Knowing these rules avoids surprising results in calculations.

③ Where It Appears
Computing a shopping cart total
Calculating averages and percentages
Converting units (km to miles)
Positioning elements in graphics
④ How It Works
An operator sits between two operands and produces a result. 5 + 3 evaluates to 8. Integer division truncates the decimal in statically typed languages. Division by zero raises an error in all languages.
⑤ Code Example
a = 10
b = 3
print(a + b)   # 13
print(a - b)   # 7
print(a * b)   # 30
print(a / b)   # 3.333...
print(a ** b)  # 1000 (exponent)
print(a % b)   # 1 (remainder)
const a = 10, b = 3;
console.log(a + b);  // 13
console.log(a - b);  // 7
console.log(a * b);  // 30
console.log(a / b);  // 3.333...
console.log(a ** b); // 1000
console.log(a % b);  // 1
int a = 10, b = 3;
System.out.println(a + b); // 13
System.out.println(a - b); // 7
System.out.println(a * b); // 30
System.out.println(a / b); // 3 (int division)
System.out.println(Math.pow(a, b)); // 1000.0
System.out.println(a % b); // 1
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrong division7 / 2 # 3.5, wanted 3
✓ Floor division7 // 2 # 3
Why: in Python / always yields a float. Use // when you want an integer result.
❌ Precedence guess2 + 3 * 4 # not 20
✓ Group explicitly(2 + 3) * 4 # 20
Why: multiplication binds tighter than addition. Parentheses make intent unambiguous.
⑥ Remember
Integer division truncates — 7 / 2 in Java is 3, not 3.5. Cast to float first when you need decimals.
FAQ
What does ** do?
Exponentiation in Python: 2 ** 3 is 8.
Difference between / and //?
/ is true division (float); // is floor division (integer).
What is %?
The remainder operator. See O·06 Modulo Operator.
Same in every language?
The basics match, but // and ** are Python-specific spellings.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·02 Comparison OperatorsO·06 Modulo OperatorO·08 Operator Precedence
cherry code › O·02
Comparison Operators
Compare two values — the result is always true or false.
O·02operatorsboolean
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·06 Boolean
① Definition
"Comparison operators evaluate a relationship between two values and return a boolean."
They are the building blocks of every conditional. Common operators: == (equal), != (not equal), >, <, >=, <=.
② Explanation

Comparison operators test the relationship between two values — equal, greater than, less than — and always produce a boolean result. They are how programs ask questions about data.

A classic pitfall is confusing assignment (=) with equality (== or ===). Languages also distinguish loose from strict equality, where strict checks both value and type, avoiding surprises like 0 equalling false.

③ Where It Appears
Conditions in if statements
Sorting items by a key
Validating a password length
Checking if a score beats a record
④ How It Works
Each comparison evaluates to true or false. In JavaScript, == does type coercion ("3" == 3 is true), while === checks type and value strictly. Python uses == for equality and is for identity.
⑤ Code Example
x = 5
print(x == 5)  # True
print(x != 3)  # True
print(x > 4)   # True
print(x < 10)  # True
print(x >= 5)  # True
print(x <= 4)  # False
let x = 5;
console.log(x === 5);  // true  (strict)
console.log(x !== 3);  // true
console.log(x > 4);    // true
console.log("5" == 5);  // true  (loose, coercion)
console.log("5" === 5); // false (strict)
int x = 5;
System.out.println(x == 5); // true
System.out.println(x != 3); // true
System.out.println(x > 4);  // true
System.out.println(x <= 5); // true
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Assign in conditionif x = 5:
✓ Compareif x == 5:
Why: = assigns; == compares. A classic source of silent bugs in C-like languages.
❌ Compare as strings"10" < "9" # True!
✓ Compare as numbers10 < 9 # False
Why: string comparison is lexicographic ("1" comes before "9"). Convert to numbers first.
⑥ Remember
Always prefer === over == in JavaScript. Type coercion causes subtle bugs that are hard to trace.
FAQ
What do they return?
A boolean — True or False.
== vs === in JS?
=== also checks the type, so "5" === 5 is false.
Can I chain comparisons?
Python allows 0 < x < 10. Most languages do not.
How are strings compared?
Lexicographically, character by character (dictionary order).
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·03 Logical OperatorsT·06 Boolean
cherry code › O·03
Logical Operators
Combine or invert boolean conditions: and, or, not.
O·03operatorsboolean
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:O·02 Comparison Operators
① Definition
"Logical operators combine boolean expressions into a single true/false result."
The three core logical operators are AND (both must be true), OR (at least one must be true), and NOT (inverts the value). They are essential for building complex conditions.
② Explanation

Logical operators combine or invert boolean values: and requires both sides true, or requires at least one, and not flips the value. They let programs express compound conditions.

Most languages use short-circuit evaluation: and stops at the first false, or stops at the first true. This is both a performance optimization and a common idiom for guarding against errors like checking a value exists before using it.

③ Where It Appears
Combining multiple form validations
Access control (isAdmin and isLoggedIn)
Guard clauses (if user and user.name)
Filtering data with several conditions
④ How It Works
