Logical operators use short-circuit evaluation — with AND, if the first operand is false the second is never checked. With OR, if the first is true the second is skipped. This can prevent errors and is used for default-value patterns in Python and JS.
⑤ Code Example
age = 20
has_id = True

print(age >= 18 and has_id)  # True
print(age < 18 or has_id)   # True
print(not has_id)             # False
const age = 20, hasId = true;

console.log(age >= 18 && hasId);  // true
console.log(age < 18 || hasId);   // true
console.log(!hasId);               // false
// Short-circuit default:
const name = null || "Guest";     // "Guest"
int age = 20;
boolean hasId = true;

System.out.println(age >= 18 && hasId); // true
System.out.println(age < 18 || hasId);  // true
System.out.println(!hasId);              // false
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Bitwise instead of logicalif a & b:
✓ Logical operatorif a and b:
Why: & is bitwise AND on numbers. Use and (Python) or && (JS) for boolean logic.
❌ Awkward negationif not a == b:
✓ Direct inequalityif a != b:
Why: != reads clearly and avoids precedence confusion around not.
⑥ Remember
Short-circuit evaluation means the right side may never run. Use this to guard against null errors: obj && obj.name.
FAQ
Do they short-circuit?
Yes — and / or stop as soon as the result is determined.
What do and / or return in Python?
One of the operands, not always a strict True/False — 0 or 5 is 5.
What is the precedence?
not binds tightest, then and, then or.
JavaScript equivalents?
&&, ||, and !.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·08 Operator PrecedenceO·02 Comparison Operators
cherry code › O·04
Assignment Operators
Store or update a value in a variable.
O·04operatorsvariables
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·01 Variable
① Definition
"Assignment operators store a value into a variable, sometimes combining with arithmetic."
The basic operator is =. Compound operators like +=, -=, *=, /= combine assignment with an operation — they are shorthand that modifies a variable in place.
② Explanation

Assignment operators store a value into a variable. The basic = sets a value, while compound forms like += or *= update a variable based on its current value, combining an operation with assignment.

These shortcuts make code shorter and clearer — total += price reads better than total = total + price. Note that = is assignment, not equality; mixing the two up is one of the most common beginner bugs.

③ Where It Appears
Initializing a variable
Accumulating a sum with +=
Building a string with +=
Toggling or updating counters
④ How It Works
The right side is evaluated first, then the result is stored in the variable on the left. x += 5 is exactly equivalent to x = x + 5. Compound operators are not just shorthand — they also make the intent clearer: "add 5 to x".
⑤ Code Example
x = 10
x += 5   # x = 15
x -= 3   # x = 12
x *= 2   # x = 24
x /= 4   # x = 6.0
x %= 4   # x = 2.0
print(x)  # 2.0
let x = 10;
x += 5;   // 15
x -= 3;   // 12
x *= 2;   // 24
x /= 4;   // 6
x %= 4;   // 2
console.log(x); // 2
int x = 10;
x += 5;   // 15
x -= 3;   // 12
x *= 2;   // 24
x /= 4;   // 6
x %= 4;   // 2
System.out.println(x); // 2
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Compare by mistaketotal == total + 1
✓ Assigntotal = total + 1
Why: == only tests equality; nothing is stored. Use = to assign.
❌ Reversed operatorx =+ 1 # sets x to +1
✓ Compound assignx += 1 # adds 1 to x
Why: =+ is "assign positive 1", not "add 1". The order of characters matters.
⑥ Remember
= is assignment; == is comparison. Confusing them is one of the most common beginner errors.
FAQ
What does += do?
Adds the right side to the variable and stores the result back.
Is = an expression?
In some languages it returns a value; relying on that is error-prone, so avoid it.
What compound operators exist?
+=, -=, *=, /=, and more.
Can I chain assignment?
a = b = 0 sets both to 0.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·01 Arithmetic OperatorsT·01 Variable
cherry code › O·05
String Concatenation
Joining two or more strings end-to-end.
O·05stringsoperators
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·03 String
① Definition
"String concatenation joins two or more strings into one longer string."
In most languages, + is overloaded to concatenate strings. Modern languages also offer interpolation (template literals, f-strings) as a cleaner alternative for embedding values inside strings.
② Explanation

String concatenation joins two or more pieces of text into one. It's how programs assemble dynamic messages, combining fixed text with variable data like a user's name or a result value.

While the + operator works in many languages, modern code often prefers templates or interpolation (like f-strings or template literals) for readability. Naive concatenation when building queries or HTML can also open security holes such as injection.

③ Where It Appears
Building a greeting from a username
Assembling a full file path
Constructing a URL with parameters
Composing log messages
④ How It Works
The + operator appends the right string to the end of the left. For dynamic content, interpolation is preferred: Python's f"Hello {name}" and JavaScript's `Hello ${name}` are more readable than chaining +. Java uses String.format() or StringBuilder for performance.
⑤ Code Example
first = "Cherry"
last = "Code"
print(first + " " + last)  # Cherry Code
print(f"Hello, {first}!")   # Hello, Cherry!
const first = "Cherry", last = "Code";
console.log(first + " " + last);   // Cherry Code
console.log(`Hello, ${first}!`);  // Hello, Cherry!
String first = "Cherry", last = "Code";
System.out.println(first + " " + last);
System.out.println(String.format("Hello, %s!", first));
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Join string and number"age " + 25
✓ Use an f-stringf"age {25}"
Why: Python will not concatenate a string and an int. f-strings convert and insert in one step.
❌ Build with + in a loops = "" for w in words: s += w
✓ Use joins = "".join(words)
Why: strings are immutable, so each += copies the whole string. join builds it once.
⑥ Remember
Prefer f-strings/template literals over + for readability. Concatenating many strings in a loop creates many temporary objects — use join() or StringBuilder instead.
FAQ
Best way to build strings?
f-strings for formatting, join for combining many pieces.
Why is += in a loop slow?
Each concatenation creates a new string and copies the old contents.
JavaScript equivalent?
Template literals: `age ${25}`.
Concatenate a list of strings?
"".join(list) in Python, arr.join("") in JS.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·03 StringO·01 Arithmetic Operators
cherry code › O·06
Modulo Operator
The remainder after division — written as %.
O·06operatorsmath
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:O·01 Arithmetic Operators
① Definition
"The modulo operator returns the remainder left over after integer division."
10 % 3 is 1 because 10 divided by 3 is 3 remainder 1. It is used for cycling through ranges, checking even/odd, and wrapping indices.
② Explanation

The modulo operator (%) returns the remainder left over after dividing one number by another. While simple, it is surprisingly powerful for problems involving cycles, grouping, and divisibility.

A key use is testing divisibility: n % 2 == 0 checks if a number is even. It also wraps values around a range, such as cycling through colors or computing positions on a clock, making it a staple of everyday logic.

③ Where It Appears
Checking if a number is even or odd
Wrapping an index around an array
Formatting time (seconds to mm:ss)
Distributing items into buckets
④ How It Works
If a % b == 0, then a is divisible by b. This is the standard even/odd check: n % 2 == 0 means even. Modulo also wraps values — index % length keeps an index within bounds of an array when cycling.
⑤ Code Example
print(10 % 3)   # 1
print(9 % 3)    # 0 (divisible)

for i in range(6):
    if i % 2 == 0:
        print(i, "is even")
console.log(10 % 3);  // 1
console.log(9 % 3);   // 0

for (let i = 0; i < 6; i++) {
  if (i % 2 === 0) console.log(i, "is even");
}
System.out.println(10 % 3); // 1
for (int i = 0; i < 6; i++) {
    if (i % 2 == 0)
        System.out.println(i + " is even");
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrong even testif n % 2 == 1: # "is even"?
✓ Remainder zeroif n % 2 == 0: # is even
Why: a number is even when the remainder after dividing by 2 is 0, not 1.
❌ Assume negative result-7 % 3 # depends on language
✓ Know your language-7 % 3 # Python: 2, C: -1
Why: the sign of a modulo result with negatives differs between languages. Verify before relying on it.
⑥ Remember
n % 2 == 0 checks for even numbers. n % 2 != 0 checks for odd. This pattern appears constantly in interview problems.
FAQ
What is modulo?
The remainder left after integer division.
Common uses?
Even/odd checks, wrapping values around a range, cycling through items.
What about negative operands?
The result sign depends on the language — Python and C differ.
Does % work with floats?
Yes in Python — 5.5 % 2 is 1.5.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·01 Arithmetic OperatorsO·08 Operator Precedence
cherry code › O·07
Increment / Decrement
Add or subtract 1 from a variable — ++ and --.
O·07operatorsloops
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:O·04 Assignment Operators
① Definition
"Increment adds 1 to a variable; decrement subtracts 1."
++ and -- are shorthand operators in Java and JavaScript. Python does not have ++; instead you use += 1. These operators are most common in loop counters.
② Explanation

Increment (++) and decrement (--) add or subtract one from a variable, a pattern so common that many languages give it a dedicated shorthand. They are most often seen driving loop counters.

The pre- versus post- form (++i vs i++) differs in whether the change happens before or after the value is used — a subtle distinction that causes confusion. Python notably omits these operators in favor of += 1.

③ Where It Appears
Advancing a for-loop counter
Tracking how many times something happened
Stepping through array indices
Countdown timers
④ How It Works
Post-increment (x++) returns the current value then increments. Pre-increment (++x) increments first then returns the new value. This distinction matters when the expression is used inside another expression: y = x++ gives y the old value of x.
⑤ Code Example
# Python has no ++ operator
x = 5
x += 1  # x = 6 (increment)
x -= 1  # x = 5 (decrement)
print(x)  # 5
let x = 5;
x++;          // x = 6
x--;          // x = 5
let y = x++; // y = 5, then x = 6
let z = ++x; // x = 7, then z = 7
console.log(y, z); // 5, 7
int x = 5;
x++;                           // x = 6
x--;                           // x = 5
int y = x++;                   // y=5, x=6
int z = ++x;                   // x=7, z=7
System.out.println(y + " " + z); // 5 7
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ ++ in Pythonx++
✓ Compound assignx += 1
Why: Python has no ++ operator. Use += 1.
❌ Mix pre and posty = i++ + ++i;
✓ Keep it simplei += 1; y = i + i;
Why: combining pre- and post-increment in one expression is hard to read and order-dependent.
⑥ Remember
Avoid using x++ inside larger expressions. Keep increment on its own line to avoid subtle pre/post bugs that are hard to spot in code review.
FAQ
Does Python have ++?
No — use += 1 and -= 1.
Pre vs post increment?
++i increments then returns the new value; i++ returns the old value then increments.
Why avoid them in complex expressions?
Evaluation order makes the result confusing and bug-prone.
Where are they common?
C, Java, and JavaScript loops.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·04 Assignment OperatorsO·01 Arithmetic Operators
cherry code › O·08
Operator Precedence
The order in which operators are evaluated in an expression.
O·08operatorsevaluation
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:O·01 Arithmetic OperatorsO·03 Logical Operators
① Definition
"Operator precedence defines which operators are evaluated first when multiple appear in one expression."
Just like PEMDAS/BODMAS in math, programming languages have a precedence table. Multiplication before addition, comparisons before logical operators, and so on.
② Explanation

Operator precedence is the set of rules that decides which operations are performed first in an expression without explicit parentheses — much like multiplication before addition in math.

Relying on memory of precedence rules is error-prone, so experienced developers add parentheses to make intent explicit. This prevents subtle bugs where an expression evaluates in an unexpected order.

③ Where It Appears
Math expressions mixing * and +
Combining comparisons and logical ops
Clarifying intent with parentheses
Reading unfamiliar code correctly
④ How It Works
Higher precedence operators bind tighter: 2 + 3 * 4 is 14, not 20, because * has higher precedence than +. Parentheses always override precedence. When operators have equal precedence, associativity determines direction — most operators are left-to-right.
⑤ Code Example
print(2 + 3 * 4)      # 14  (* first)
print((2 + 3) * 4)    # 20  (parens first)
print(10 - 3 - 2)     # 5   (left to right)
print(True or False and False) # True (and before or)
console.log(2 + 3 * 4);      // 14
console.log((2 + 3) * 4);    // 20
console.log(true || false && false); // true (&& before ||)
System.out.println(2 + 3 * 4);   // 14
System.out.println((2 + 3) * 4); // 20
System.out.println(true || false && false); // true
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Assume left to right2 + 3 * 4 # not 20
✓ Group what you mean(2 + 3) * 4 # 20
Why: * is evaluated before +. Parentheses force the order you intend.
❌ Trust logical precedencea and b or c
✓ Parenthesize(a and b) or c
Why: explicit grouping is clearer than memorizing precedence tables for every reader.
⑥ Remember
When in doubt, use parentheses. They cost nothing and make intent explicit. Code clarity always beats relying on implicit precedence rules.
FAQ
What binds tightest?
Typically **, then * and /, then + and -.
How do I stay safe?
When in doubt, add parentheses. They cost nothing and remove ambiguity.
What about logical precedence?
not > and > or.
Same across languages?
Mostly the same, but always verify for unusual operators.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·01 Arithmetic OperatorsO·03 Logical Operators
cherry code › O·09
Expression vs Statement
Expressions produce a value; statements perform an action.
O·09expressionsfundamentals
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·02 Code
① Definition
"An expression evaluates to a value; a statement executes an action."
2 + 3 is an expression — it produces 5. if x > 0: print(x) is a statement — it directs program flow but does not produce a value itself. The distinction matters in contexts where only one or the other is allowed.
② Explanation

An expression is any piece of code that evaluates to a value — like 2 + 3 or a function call that returns something. A statement is a complete instruction that performs an action, like assigning a variable or running a loop.

The distinction matters because expressions can be nested and combined, while statements form the steps of a program. Some constructs blur the line: in many languages an assignment or function call is both, used for its value or its effect.

③ Where It Appears
A calculation passed as a function argument
An if/for block (statement)
A ternary returning a value (expression)
Assigning the result of a computation
④ How It Works
Expressions can appear anywhere a value is expected — in assignments, as function arguments, inside other expressions. Statements form the structure of a program: loops, conditionals, declarations. In Python, the walrus operator := makes assignments into expressions.
⑤ Code Example
# Expressions — produce a value
result = 2 + 3      # 5
flag = result > 3  # True

# Statements — perform actions
if flag:
    print("big")    # statement
// Expressions
const result = 2 + 3;    // 5
const flag = result > 3; // true

// Statements
if (flag) {
  console.log("big");
}
// Expressions
int result = 2 + 3;     // 5
boolean flag = result > 3; // true

// Statement
if (flag) {
    System.out.println("big");
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Expect a statement to returnx = (if a: 1 else 0)
✓ Use the expression formx = 1 if a else 0
Why: in Python if is a statement, not an expression. The ternary form evaluates to a value.
❌ Use = as a testwhile (line = read()):
✓ Compare explicitlyline = read() while line is not None:
Why: mixing assignment into a condition hides intent and is invalid in Python.
⑥ Remember
If you can substitute it with a value, it is an expression. If it controls flow or declares something, it is a statement. Lambdas are expressions; function declarations are statements.
FAQ
Expression vs statement?
An expression evaluates to a value; a statement performs an action.
Is assignment an expression?
It depends on the language — in C yes, in Python no.
What is a ternary?
An expression form of if/else: a if cond else b.
Why does the difference matter?
It determines where the code can legally appear.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·10 Side EffectsF·02 Code
cherry code › O·10
Side Effects
Changes a function makes beyond returning a value.
O·10functionsstate
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:O·09 Expression vs Statement
① Definition
"A side effect is any observable change a function causes outside its own scope."
Side effects include modifying a global variable, writing to a file, printing to the screen, changing a passed-in list, or making a network request. Functions without side effects are called pure functions.
② Explanation

A side effect is any observable change a piece of code makes beyond computing and returning a value — writing to a file, modifying a global variable, printing to the screen, or making a network call.

Functions without side effects ('pure' functions) are easier to test, reason about, and reuse because they depend only on their inputs. Managing side effects carefully is a core idea in functional programming and in writing predictable, debuggable code.

③ Where It Appears
Writing data to a database
Logging to the console
Modifying a shared global variable
Sending an email or HTTP request
④ How It Works
Side effects are not inherently bad — printing, saving data, and updating UI all require them. The problem is unexpected side effects: a function that secretly modifies a list the caller passed in can lead to hard-to-trace bugs. Functional programming minimizes side effects to improve predictability.
⑤ Code Example
# Side effect: modifies the list in place
def add_item(lst, item):
    lst.append(item)  # side effect!

nums = [1, 2]
add_item(nums, 3)
print(nums)  # [1, 2, 3] — changed outside
// Side effect: modifies array in place
function addItem(arr, item) {
  arr.push(item);  // side effect!
}

const nums = [1, 2];
addItem(nums, 3);
console.log(nums); // [1, 2, 3]
import java.util.List;
void addItem(List<Integer> list, int item) {
    list.add(item);  // side effect!
}
// Caller's list is now modified
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Mutable default argdef add(x, acc=[]): acc.append(x)
✓ Default to Nonedef add(x, acc=None): acc = acc or [] acc.append(x)
Why: a default list is created once and shared across every call, so it keeps growing unexpectedly.
❌ Silently mutate inputdef sort_it(items): items.sort()
✓ Return a new listdef sort_it(items): return sorted(items)
Why: changing the caller's list surprises them. Returning a new value keeps the function predictable.
⑥ Remember
Make side effects explicit and intentional. A function that modifies its input should be clearly named — e.g., sort_in_place() — so callers know to expect mutation.
FAQ
What is a side effect?
Any change to state outside the function — I/O, globals, or mutating arguments.
What is a pure function?
One that returns a value and causes no side effects.
Why minimize side effects?
Pure code is easier to test, reuse, and reason about.
Are side effects bad?
No — I/O needs them. Just make them intentional and contained.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·09 Expression vs StatementF·02 Code
4
Block 4 · Control Flow
Directing the Path
Code runs top to bottom — unless you tell it otherwise. Control flow is how you make decisions, repeat actions, and skip steps. Mastering it means your program can react to any situation.
If / ElseLoopsBreakSwitchTernary
10 terms · C·01 → C·10 branch it
cherry code › C·01
If Statement
Execute a block of code only when a condition is true.
C·01control flowconditionals
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·06 BooleanO·02 Comparison Operators
① Definition
"An if statement runs a block of code only when its condition evaluates to true."
It is the most fundamental control structure. The condition is an expression that must evaluate to a boolean. If true, the body executes; if false, it is skipped entirely.
② Explanation

An if statement is the program's most basic decision point. The runtime evaluates the condition to a single boolean value, and that one yes-or-no answer decides whether the guarded block of code runs at all. Without conditionals, a program could only ever do exactly the same thing every time it ran.

Conditions are built from comparison and logical operators, so an if can ask rich questions like "is the user logged in and is their balance positive?". The block stays skipped entirely when the answer is false, which lets you protect code that should only run in specific situations.

③ Where It Appears
Validating a form before submitting it
Showing a discount only when a coupon is valid
Guarding against division by zero
Checking permissions before an action
④ How It Works
The condition in parentheses (or after if in Python) is evaluated first. If it is truthy, the indented block runs. In Python, indentation defines the block; in Java and JavaScript, curly braces {} define it. Always use braces in Java/JS even for single-line bodies to avoid bugs.
⑤ Code Example
score = 85

if score >= 60:
    print("Passed")   # runs

if score >= 90:
    print("Excellent") # skipped
const score = 85;

if (score >= 60) {
  console.log("Passed");    // runs
}

if (score >= 90) {
  console.log("Excellent"); // skipped
}
int score = 85;

if (score >= 60) {
    System.out.println("Passed");    // runs
}

if (score >= 90) {
    System.out.println("Excellent"); // skipped
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Assign in conditionif x = 5:
✓ Compareif x == 5:
Why: a condition tests a value; = assigns. Use == to compare.
❌ No colon or indentif x > 0 print(x)
✓ Colon + indented bodyif x > 0: print(x)
Why: Python opens a block with a colon and an indented body. Both are required.
⑥ Remember
Always use curly braces in Java and JavaScript even for one-line bodies. Omitting them is a common source of bugs when adding a second line later.
FAQ
What can the condition be?
Anything truthy or falsy — it does not have to be a literal boolean.
Can I have an if with no else?
Yes — else is optional.
How do I test multiple conditions?
Combine them with and / or.
Can ifs be nested?
Yes, but deep nesting hurts readability — prefer early returns.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·02 Else / ElifC·09 Ternary Operator
cherry code › C·02
Else / Elif
Alternative branches when the if condition is false.
C·02control flowconditionals
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·01 If Statement
① Definition
"else runs when the if condition is false; elif (else-if) adds additional conditions to check."
else provides a fallback path. elif (Python) / else if (Java, JS) chains additional conditions. Only one branch in the chain executes — the first one whose condition is true.
② Explanation

Else and elif extend an if into a complete decision tree. Where a lone if either acts or does nothing, an if/elif/else chain guarantees that exactly one branch runs, covering every possible outcome with a clear fallback.

The runtime tests each condition top to bottom and stops at the first one that is true, skipping the rest. This short-circuit ordering means you should place the most specific or most likely conditions first, and use the final else as a catch-all default.

③ Where It Appears
Grading a score into A, B, C, or F
Routing a request by user role
Choosing a message for empty, single, or many results
Picking a shipping rate by region
④ How It Works
The interpreter checks conditions top to bottom and executes the first matching branch, then skips the rest. The final else is a catch-all with no condition — it runs if nothing above was true. An if chain without else may execute nothing at all.
⑤ Code Example
score = 72

if score >= 90:
    print("A")
elif score >= 80:
    print("B")
elif score >= 70:
    print("C")  # this runs
else:
    print("F")
const score = 72;

if (score >= 90) {
  console.log("A");
} else if (score >= 80) {
  console.log("B");
} else if (score >= 70) {
  console.log("C"); // runs
} else {
  console.log("F");
}
int score = 72;

if (score >= 90) {
    System.out.println("A");
} else if (score >= 80) {
    System.out.println("B");
} else if (score >= 70) {
    System.out.println("C"); // runs
} else {
    System.out.println("F");
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Separate ifsif score > 90: grade = "A" if score > 80: grade = "B"
✓ elif chainif score > 90: grade = "A" elif score > 80: grade = "B"
Why: separate ifs all run; an elif chain stops at the first match.
❌ Wrong keywordelse if x > 0:
✓ Python keywordelif x > 0:
Why: Python spells it elif; else if belongs to JavaScript and Java.
⑥ Remember
Order matters — put the most specific conditions first. Checking >= 70 before >= 90 would catch everything at 70+ in the first branch.
FAQ
elif vs else if?
Python uses elif; JavaScript and Java use else if.
Do I need an else?
No — else is optional.
Does order matter?
Yes — the first matching branch runs and the rest are skipped.
Too many branches?
Consider a dictionary lookup or a match/switch.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·03 Switch / CaseC·01 If Statement
cherry code › C·03
Switch / Case
Branch on a single value against multiple possible matches.
C·03control flowconditionals
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·02 Else / Elif
① Definition
"A switch statement tests one value against several cases, running the matching branch."
It is a cleaner alternative to long if-elif-else chains when you are comparing one variable to many discrete values. Python added match/case in 3.10.
② Explanation

A switch (or match) statement compares one value against many possible cases, offering a flatter, more readable alternative to a long if/elif chain when every branch tests the same variable. Each case lists a literal to match, and the matching block runs.

Many languages "fall through" from one case to the next unless you add a break, which is a classic source of bugs; modern forms like Python's match or JavaScript's switch with break give cleaner control. A default case handles values that match nothing.

③ Where It Appears
Handling menu selections by command name
Mapping HTTP status codes to messages
Dispatching events by their type
Translating enum values to labels
④ How It Works
The expression is evaluated once, then matched against each case. In Java and JavaScript, execution falls through to the next case unless you add break. The default case is the catch-all, like else. Python's match does not fall through by default.
⑤ Code Example
# Python 3.10+ match/case
day = "Mon"
match day:
    case "Mon" | "Tue" | "Wed":
        print("Weekday")
    case "Sat" | "Sun":
        print("Weekend")
    case _:
        print("Unknown")
const day = "Mon";
switch (day) {
  case "Mon":
  case "Tue":
    console.log("Weekday"); break;
  case "Sat":
  case "Sun":
    console.log("Weekend"); break;
  default:
    console.log("Unknown");
}
String day = "Mon";
switch (day) {
  case "Mon": case "Tue":
    System.out.println("Weekday"); break;
  case "Sat": case "Sun":
    System.out.println("Weekend"); break;
  default:
    System.out.println("Unknown");
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Missing breakcase 1: doA(); case 2: doB();
✓ break each casecase 1: doA(); break;
Why: in C and Java, cases fall through to the next one unless you add break.
❌ Expect switch in old Pythonswitch x:
✓ match or dictmatch x: case 1: ...
Why: Python only added match in 3.10. Before that, use a dictionary dispatch.
⑥ Remember
Always add break in Java/JS switch cases unless fall-through is intentional. A missing break is one of the sneakiest bugs — execution silently continues into the next case.
FAQ
Does Python have switch?
match/case since 3.10; otherwise use dict dispatch.
What is fall-through?
Without break, execution continues into the next case.
When prefer switch over if/elif?
When branching on many discrete values of one variable.
What is the default case?
It runs when no other case matches.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·04 While LoopC·02 Else / Elif
cherry code › C·04
While Loop
Repeat a block as long as a condition stays true.
C·04loopscontrol flow
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·06 Boolean
① Definition
"A while loop repeats its body as long as the condition at the top remains true."
The condition is checked before each iteration. If it starts false, the body never runs. Use a while loop when you don't know in advance how many iterations are needed.
② Explanation

A while loop repeats its body as long as a condition remains true, checking that condition before every pass. It is the right tool when you do not know in advance how many iterations you will need — you loop until something changes.

Because the condition is re-tested each time, the body must eventually make it false, otherwise the loop runs forever. Beginners often forget to update the variable the condition depends on, producing an infinite loop that hangs the program.

③ Where It Appears
Reading input until the user types 'quit'
Polling a sensor until a value is ready
Game loops that run until the player loses
Retrying a network request until it succeeds
④ How It Works
The loop checks the condition; if true, runs the body, then checks again. You must update something inside the body that will eventually make the condition false, or you get an infinite loop. Use while True: with an explicit break for game loops or user input prompts.
⑤ Code Example
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1
# prints 0 1 2 3 4
let count = 0;
while (count < 5) {
  console.log(count);
  count++;
}
// prints 0 1 2 3 4
int count = 0;
while (count < 5) {
    System.out.println(count);
    count++;
}
// prints 0 1 2 3 4
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Never update conditioni = 0 while i < 5: print(i)
✓ Advance inside loopi = 0 while i < 5: print(i) i += 1
Why: if the condition variable never changes, the loop runs forever.
❌ Off-by-one boundwhile i <= len(arr):
✓ Stop before lengthwhile i < len(arr):
Why: valid indices go up to length-1, so use <, not <=.
⑥ Remember
Every while loop needs a clear exit condition. Always ask: "what changes so this condition becomes false?" If nothing changes, the loop runs forever and freezes the program.
FAQ
while vs for?
Use while when the number of iterations is unknown; for when it is known.
How do I stop early?
Use break.
Infinite loop on purpose?
while True: with a break inside is a common pattern.
What is while-else in Python?
The else block runs if the loop finished without hitting break.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·05 For LoopC·07 Break
cherry code › C·05
For Loop
Repeat a block a known number of times using a counter.
C·05loopscontrol flow
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·04 While Loop
① Definition
"A for loop iterates a set number of times, typically using a counter variable."
In Java and JavaScript the classic form uses init; condition; update. Python's for is always a for-each over an iterable — use range() to mimic a counter loop.
② Explanation

A for loop is built for counting: it bundles initialization, a condition, and an update step into one compact header, then repeats its body a known number of times. This makes it the natural choice when you already know how many iterations you need.

The loop counter is usually available inside the body, so you can use it as an index into a sequence or to label each pass. Keeping the start, end, and step explicit in one place makes for loops easier to read and less error-prone than equivalent while loops.

③ Where It Appears
Processing every row of a fixed-size dataset
Repeating an action a set number of times
Building a multiplication table
Iterating with an index to modify list positions
④ How It Works
Java/JS: initialize a counter, check the condition, run the body, execute the update, repeat. Python: for i in range(n) generates integers 0 to n-1. range(start, stop, step) gives full control over the sequence.
⑤ Code Example
for i in range(5):
    print(i)          # 0 1 2 3 4

for i in range(1, 10, 2):
    print(i)          # 1 3 5 7 9
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  console.log(i);  // 0 1 2 3 4
}

for (let i = 1; i < 10; i += 2) {
  console.log(i);  // 1 3 5 7 9
}
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    System.out.println(i); // 0 1 2 3 4
}

for (int i = 1; i < 10; i += 2) {
    System.out.println(i); // 1 3 5 7 9
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Expect end includedfor i in range(1, 10) # stops at 9
✓ Add one to reach itfor i in range(1, 11) # reaches 10
Why: range excludes its end value, so the last number is end-1.
❌ Remove while loopingfor x in items: items.remove(x)
✓ Loop over a copyfor x in items[:]: items.remove(x)
Why: changing the list you are looping over skips elements.
⑥ Remember
range(n) produces 0 to n-1, not 1 to n. Off-by-one errors are among the most common bugs — always check your range boundaries.
FAQ
Is the range end inclusive?
No — range(0, 5) yields 0,1,2,3,4.
Where is the C-style for?
Python uses for x in ...; C/Java use init/condition/step.
How do I get the index too?
Use enumerate(items).
How do I loop in reverse?
reversed(items) or range(n, 0, -1).
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·06 For-Each LoopC·10 Nested Loops
cherry code › C·06
For-Each Loop
Iterate over every element in a collection without a counter.
C·06loopscollections
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·05 For LoopT·08 Array / List
① Definition
"A for-each loop visits every element in a collection in order, without managing an index."
It is cleaner than a counter-based loop when you need the values but not the positions. Python's for is always for-each. Java uses for (Type x : collection). JavaScript uses for...of.
② Explanation

A for-each loop walks directly over the elements of a collection, handing you each item in turn without any index bookkeeping. It expresses intent more clearly than a counter-based loop: you say "do this to every item" rather than "count from zero to length minus one".

Because there is no manual index, for-each loops eliminate off-by-one errors and out-of-bounds mistakes. The tradeoff is that you usually cannot easily change the collection's structure while iterating, and in many languages you do not get the position of the current element for free.

③ Where It Appears
Printing every name in a guest list
Summing the values in an array
Rendering each item in a UI list
Applying a transform to every file in a folder
④ How It Works
On each iteration the loop variable is assigned the next element from the collection. In JavaScript, for...of gives values; for...in gives keys/indices — a common source of confusion. Use enumerate() in Python when you need both the index and value.
⑤ Code Example
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

# With index:
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(i, fruit)
const fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"];

for (const fruit of fruits) {
  console.log(fruit);
}

// With index:
fruits.forEach((fruit, i) =>
  console.log(i, fruit));
String[] fruits = {"apple", "banana", "cherry"};

for (String fruit : fruits) {
    System.out.println(fruit);
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Need index, lose itfor item in items: # which position?
✓ Use enumeratefor i, item in enumerate(items): print(i, item)
Why: for-each yields elements, not indices. enumerate gives both.
❌ Mutate while iteratingfor x in data: data.append(x)
✓ Iterate a snapshotfor x in list(data): data.append(x)
Why: adding or removing during iteration leads to skipped items or endless loops.
⑥ Remember
In JavaScript, always use for...of for arrays, not for...in. for...in iterates over property keys and will also iterate inherited properties — rarely what you want.
FAQ
for-each vs indexed for?
For-each hands you each element directly, no index bookkeeping.
How do I get the index?
enumerate in Python, .entries() in JavaScript.
Does it work on dicts?
Yes — it iterates the keys by default.
Can I modify during the loop?
Avoid it; iterate over a copy instead.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·07 BreakT·08 Array / List
cherry code › C·07
Break
Immediately exit the current loop.
C·07loopscontrol flow
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·04 While Loop
① Definition
"break exits the innermost loop immediately, skipping any remaining iterations."
It is used when you have found what you were looking for or when a condition makes continuing pointless. Execution resumes at the first statement after the loop.
② Explanation

Break gives you an emergency exit from a loop: the moment it runs, the loop stops immediately and execution jumps to the first statement after the loop, regardless of whether the loop condition is still true. It turns an open-ended loop into one that can stop early.

Break is most useful in search-style loops where you scan until you find what you want and then have no reason to keep going. Inside nested loops, break only exits the innermost loop, which is a common surprise for newcomers.

③ Where It Appears
Stopping a search once a match is found
Exiting a menu loop when the user picks 'exit'
Aborting a scan when an error is detected
Limiting how many results you collect
④ How It Works
break only exits the innermost loop. In nested loops, a break in the inner loop does not affect the outer loop. Use a flag variable or structured code to break out of multiple levels. In Python, a for loop's optional else clause runs only if no break was executed.
⑤ Code Example
numbers = [1, 3, 7, 4, 9]
for n in numbers:
    if n % 2 == 0:
        print(f"Found even: {n}")
        break
# Found even: 4
const numbers = [1, 3, 7, 4, 9];
for (const n of numbers) {
  if (n % 2 === 0) {
    console.log(`Found even: ${n}`);
    break;
  }
}
// Found even: 4
int[] numbers = {1, 3, 7, 4, 9};
for (int n : numbers) {
    if (n % 2 == 0) {
        System.out.println("Found even: " + n);
        break;
    }
}
// Found even: 4
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Expect it to exit all loopsfor a in xs: for b in ys: break # only inner stops
✓ Use a flag or functiondef search(): for a in xs: for b in ys: return (a, b)
Why: break exits only the innermost loop. To leave several, use a function with return or a flag.
⑥ Remember
break only exits the innermost loop. If you have nested loops and need to escape both, consider refactoring into a function and using return instead.
FAQ
What does break do?
It exits the nearest enclosing loop immediately.
Does it break all nested loops?
No — only the innermost one.
break vs continue?
break stops the loop; continue skips to the next iteration.
break inside a switch?
In C/Java it ends the current case.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·08 ContinueC·10 Nested Loops
cherry code › C·08
Continue
Skip the rest of the current iteration and move to the next.
C·08loopscontrol flow
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·07 Break
① Definition
"continue skips the remaining code in the current loop iteration and jumps to the next one."
Unlike break, the loop keeps running — only this iteration is cut short. It is useful for filtering: skip invalid values and process only the valid ones.
② Explanation

Continue skips the remainder of the current iteration and jumps straight to the loop's next pass. Unlike break, it does not end the loop — it simply abandons the work for the current item and moves on to the following one.

Continue is a clean way to filter inside a loop: you check a condition at the top, and if the item is not interesting you continue, keeping the rest of the body free of deep nesting. It makes the "happy path" of a loop easier to read.

③ Where It Appears
Skipping blank lines while reading a file
Ignoring invalid records during import
Processing only even numbers in a range
Bypassing items that fail a validation check
④ How It Works
When continue is hit, execution jumps immediately to the loop's update step (for-loop) or back to the condition check (while-loop). The loop does not exit — it just skips the code below continue for this pass.
⑤ Code Example
for i in range(10):
    if i % 2 == 0:
        continue      # skip evens
    print(i)          # 1 3 5 7 9
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
  if (i % 2 === 0) continue; // skip evens
  console.log(i);              // 1 3 5 7 9
}
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
    if (i % 2 == 0) continue; // skip evens
    System.out.println(i);      // 1 3 5 7 9
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Expect it to exitif bad: continue # loop keeps going
✓ Use break to exitif bad: break
Why: continue skips to the next iteration; it does not leave the loop.
❌ Skip the counterwhile i < n: if skip(i): continue i += 1
✓ Advance before continuewhile i < n: j = i; i += 1 if skip(j): continue
Why: in a while loop, continue can jump past the counter update and loop forever.
⑥ Remember
continue does not exit the loop — the loop keeps going. To exit entirely, use break. Mixing them up is a common beginner mistake.
FAQ
What does continue do?
Skips the rest of the current iteration and starts the next one.
continue vs break?
continue keeps looping; break stops the loop.
Is it dangerous in while?
Yes — if it skips the counter update, the loop never ends.
Should I use it often?
Sparingly — overuse can make loops hard to follow.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·07 BreakC·05 For Loop
cherry code › C·09
Ternary Operator
An inline if-else that evaluates to a value in one expression.
C·09operatorsconditionals
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·01 If Statement
① Definition
"The ternary operator is a compact if-else that returns one of two values based on a condition."
It takes three operands: a condition, a value if true, and a value if false. It is an expression (produces a value), not a statement, so it can be used inside assignments and function calls.
② Explanation

The ternary operator compresses a simple if/else into a single expression that produces a value. Because it is an expression rather than a statement, you can use its result directly — assign it to a variable, pass it as an argument, or embed it in a larger expression.

It shines for short, value-choosing decisions like picking one of two labels, but it becomes unreadable when nested or stuffed with logic. The rule of thumb is to reach for it when both branches are tiny and to fall back to a full if/else otherwise.

③ Where It Appears
Choosing a label like 'on' or 'off'
Setting a default when a value is missing
Picking singular vs plural wording
Inline class names in UI templates
④ How It Works
Java/JS syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse. Python syntax: valueIfTrue if condition else valueIfFalse. Use it for simple, readable two-branch choices. Avoid chaining ternaries — nested ternaries are very hard to read.
⑤ Code Example
age = 20
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
print(status)  # adult

grade = "pass" if 70 <= age else "fail"
const age = 20;
const status = age >= 18 ? "adult" : "minor";
console.log(status); // adult

// Use in JSX / template:
const label = age > 0 ? "positive" : "zero or neg";
int age = 20;
String status = age >= 18 ? "adult" : "minor";
System.out.println(status); // adult
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Missing elselabel = "ok" if passed
✓ Provide both brancheslabel = "ok" if passed else "fail"
Why: a Python ternary must supply both the true and false value.
❌ Deeply nested ternarya if x else (b if y else c)
✓ Use if/elifif x: r = a elif y: r = b else: r = c
Why: nested ternaries are hard to read. A normal if/elif chain is clearer.
⑥ Remember
Use the ternary only for simple, clear two-branch expressions. If your condition or values are complex, use a regular if/else — readability beats brevity.
FAQ
What is a ternary?
A compact if/else that evaluates to a value.
Python syntax?
value_if_true if condition else value_if_false.
JavaScript syntax?
condition ? a : b.
When should I avoid it?
When it grows long or nested — use a full if statement.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:O·09 Expression vs StatementC·01 If Statement
cherry code › C·10
Nested Loops
A loop inside another loop — for grids, tables, and combinations.
C·10loops2D data
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:C·05 For Loop
① Definition
"Nested loops place one loop inside another to iterate over multi-dimensional data or all combinations."
For each iteration of the outer loop, the inner loop runs to completion. They are used for grids, matrices, and generating all pairs from two collections.
② Explanation

Nested loops place one loop inside another so the inner loop runs completely for every single pass of the outer loop. This is how programs work across two dimensions — rows and columns, or every pairing between two lists.

The cost grows multiplicatively: an outer loop of n and an inner loop of m run n×m times, which is why deeply nested loops can become slow on large inputs. Keeping the inner work cheap and limiting nesting depth are key to keeping them efficient.

③ Where It Appears
Iterating over a 2D grid or matrix
Comparing every pair of items in a list
Rendering a calendar of weeks and days
Generating combinations like a seating chart
④ How It Works
If the outer loop runs m times and the inner loop runs n times, the body executes m × n times total. This is O(n²) complexity for equal-sized loops — something to watch for with large datasets. Keep nesting shallow; deeply nested loops are hard to read and slow.
⑤ Code Example
# Multiplication table 1-3
for i in range(1, 4):
    for j in range(1, 4):
        print(f"{i}x{j}={i*j}", end="  ")
    print()
# 1x1=1  1x2=2  1x3=3
# 2x1=2  2x2=4  2x3=6
# 3x1=3  3x2=6  3x3=9
for (let i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
  let row = "";
  for (let j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
    row += `${i}x${j}=${i*j}  `;
  }
  console.log(row);
}
for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for (int j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
        System.out.print(i + "x" + j + "=" + (i*j) + "  ");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Reuse the same variablefor i in rows: for i in cols: # clobbers outer i
✓ Distinct namesfor r in rows: for c in cols:
Why: sharing a loop variable across levels overwrites the outer one and corrupts the loop.
❌ Quadratic on big datafor a in big: for b in big: if a == b: ...
✓ Use a set lookupseen = set(big) for a in big: if a in seen: ...
Why: nested loops multiply work. A set membership check is far faster on large inputs.
⑥ Remember
Nested loops have O(n²) cost — fine for small data, but slow for large collections. If you find yourself writing three nested loops, look for a better algorithm.
FAQ
What are nested loops?
A loop placed inside another loop.
What about performance?
Iterations multiply — two loops over n items do roughly n×n work.
Does break exit both?
No — it only exits the inner loop.
Any alternatives?
Sometimes a single pass or a set/dictionary lookup avoids the nesting.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:C·05 For LoopC·07 Break
5
Block 5 · Functions
The Art of Reuse
Functions are the smallest unit of reusable logic. Once you can name a behavior, parameterize it, and return a value, you can compose entire programs from clean building blocks.
ParametersReturnScopeRecursionClosures
12 terms · N·01 → N·12 compose it
cherry code › N·01
Function
A reusable named block of code that performs a task.
N·01functionsreuse
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:F·02 CodeT·01 Variable
① Definition
"A function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task."
You define it once and call it as many times as you need. Functions take inputs (parameters), do work, and optionally return a result.
② Explanation

A function packages a chunk of logic behind a name so you can run it again and again just by calling that name. This is the single most important tool for fighting repetition: write the steps once, then reuse them everywhere instead of copying code.

Functions also let you think at a higher level. Once a task is wrapped in a well-named function, you can use it without remembering how it works inside, which keeps the rest of your program short and focused on what it does rather than how.

③ Where It Appears
Reusing a calculation in many places
Breaking a large program into named steps
Sharing logic across files via a library
Wrapping a complex API call behind one name
④ How It Works
Define a function with a name, parameters, and body. Call it by writing the name followed by parentheses with arguments. The function executes its body and may return a value back to the caller.
⑤ Code Example
def greet(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("Ada"))   # Hello, Ada!
function greet(name) {
  return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}
console.log(greet("Ada"));  // Hello, Ada!
static String greet(String name) {
    return "Hello, " + name + "!";
}
System.out.println(greet("Ada"));
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Define but never calldef greet(): print("hi") # nothing prints
✓ Call itdef greet(): print("hi") greet()
Why: defining a function only stores it. You must call it with () to run the body.
❌ Forget to returndef double(x): x * 2
✓ Return the resultdef double(x): return x * 2
Why: with no return, the function gives back None.
⑥ Remember
A good function does one thing. If you find yourself writing "and" in its name, split it in two.
FAQ
Why use functions?
They let you reuse code, name a behavior, and test it in isolation.
def vs calling?
def creates the function; () runs it.
One job per function?
Yes — small, single-purpose functions are easier to reuse and debug.
Can a function call another?
Yes — that is how programs are composed.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·02 ParameterN·04 Return Value
cherry code › N·02
Parameter
A named input variable in the function definition.
N·02functionsinput
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·01 Function
① Definition
"A parameter is a placeholder name listed in a function's definition for the values it will receive."
Parameters act as local variables inside the function, holding whatever arguments the caller passes in.
② Explanation

A parameter is a placeholder named in a function's definition, standing in for a value the caller will supply later. Parameters are what make a function general instead of hard-coded — the same function can act on whatever data you feed it.

Inside the function the parameter behaves like a local variable, initialized to whatever was passed in. Choosing clear parameter names and ordering them sensibly is part of designing a function that is pleasant to call.

③ Where It Appears
Defining a greet(name) that works for anyone
Configuring behavior with option parameters
Passing data into a calculation function
Accepting a callback to customize behavior
④ How It Works
When the function is called, each parameter is bound to the corresponding argument. Parameters exist only inside the function — they vanish when the function returns.
⑤ Code Example
# 'a' and 'b' are parameters
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

print(add(2, 3))   # 5
function add(a, b) {  // a, b are parameters
  return a + b;
}
console.log(add(2, 3));  // 5
static int add(int a, int b) {  // parameters
    return a + b;
}
System.out.println(add(2, 3));
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Confuse the two termsdef f(value): # value is the argument?
✓ Parameter in the definitiondef f(value): # value is the parameter
Why: a parameter is the placeholder in the definition; the argument is the value passed at the call.
❌ Rely on order silentlybox(2, 5, 1)
✓ Name thembox(width=2, height=5, depth=1)
Why: keyword arguments are self-documenting and immune to reordering mistakes.
⑥ Remember
Parameters are the recipe; arguments are the ingredients you actually pour in.
FAQ
Parameter vs argument?
Parameter is named in the definition; argument is the value supplied when calling.
Positional vs keyword?
Positional are matched by order; keyword by name.
How many is too many?
If a function has many parameters, group them into an object or dict.
What are type hints?
Optional annotations like def f(x: int) that document expected types.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·03 ArgumentN·09 Default Arguments
cherry code › N·03
Argument
The actual value passed in when calling a function.
N·03functionscall
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·02 Parameter
① Definition
"An argument is the concrete value supplied to a function when it is called."
Arguments fill the slots defined by parameters. They can be literals, variables, or even other function calls.
② Explanation

An argument is the concrete value you actually hand to a function at the moment you call it. Where a parameter is the named slot in the definition, the argument is the real thing that fills that slot for this particular call.

Languages match arguments to parameters either by position or by name, and getting the order or count wrong is a frequent bug. Understanding the parameter/argument distinction clears up a lot of confusion when reading function calls.

③ Where It Appears
Passing 3 and 5 to an add function
Supplying a filename to an open call
Sending user input into a validator
Providing config values to a setup routine
④ How It Works
Arguments are matched to parameters either by position (in order) or by name (keyword arguments in Python). Mismatch in count usually raises an error.
⑤ Code Example
def power(base, exp):
    return base ** exp

power(2, 8)              # positional
power(base=2, exp=8)      # keyword args
function power(base, exp) {
  return base ** exp;
}
power(2, 8);   // 2 and 8 are arguments
static int power(int base, int exp) {
    return (int) Math.pow(base, exp);
}
power(2, 8);  // arguments
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrong countdef add(a, b): ... add(1)
✓ Match the signatureadd(1, 2)
Why: passing too few or too many arguments raises a TypeError.
❌ Mutate a passed listdef push(lst): lst.append(1)
✓ Return a new listdef push(lst): return lst + [1]
Why: objects are passed by reference, so mutating an argument changes the caller's data.
⑥ Remember
Parameters are defined, arguments are passed. Two words for two sides of the same handshake.
FAQ
Argument vs parameter?
The argument is the actual value you pass in.
Positional or keyword?
Python supports both at the call site.
Pass by value or reference?
Python passes references to objects, so mutations are visible to the caller.
Can I unpack a list into arguments?
Yes — f(*mylist) spreads it.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·04 Return ValueN·02 Parameter
cherry code › N·04
Return Value
The value a function hands back to whoever called it.
N·04functionsreturn
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·01 Function
① Definition
"The return value is the result a function produces and sends back to the caller."
The return statement ends the function immediately and passes its expression back. If a function has no return, it implicitly returns nothing (None, undefined, or void).
② Explanation

A return value is the result a function sends back to its caller, ending the function's execution at that point. It is how a function communicates its answer, turning input into a usable output that the surrounding code can store or pass along.

A function without a return (or with a bare return) hands back nothing meaningful — it exists only for its side effects. Returning a value instead of printing it keeps functions composable, because the caller decides what to do with the result.

③ Where It Appears
A square(n) that hands back n*n
Returning whether a password is valid
Producing a formatted string to display later
Returning a list of search results
④ How It Works
When return executes, the function stops and its expression replaces the call site. You can capture the return value in a variable or feed it directly into another expression.
⑤ Code Example
def square(x):
    return x * x

result = square(5)
print(result)   # 25
function square(x) {
  return x * x;
}
const result = square(5);
console.log(result);  // 25
static int square(int x) {
    return x * x;
}
int result = square(5);  // 25
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ print instead of returndef total(a, b): print(a + b)
✓ return the valuedef total(a, b): return a + b
Why: print shows text but the caller cannot use it. return hands the value back.
❌ Code after returnreturn result log(result) # never runs
✓ Do work before returnlog(result) return result
Why: return exits immediately, so anything after it is unreachable.
⑥ Remember
return exits the function immediately. Any code after a triggered return is unreachable.
FAQ
What if there is no return?
The function returns None (Python) or undefined (JS).
Can there be multiple returns?
Yes — the first one reached ends the function.
Return multiple values?
Return a tuple: return a, b.
return vs print?
return gives a value to the caller; print only displays text.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·05 Pure FunctionN·01 Function
cherry code › N·05
Pure Function
Same inputs always produce the same outputs, with no side effects.
N·05functionsfunctional
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:O·10 Side EffectsN·04 Return Value
① Definition
"A pure function depends only on its inputs and produces no observable side effects."
It does not modify external state, write files, print, or alter its arguments. Given the same inputs, it always returns the same output.
② Explanation

A pure function depends only on its arguments and changes nothing outside itself: given the same inputs, it always returns the same output and leaves the rest of the program untouched. This predictability makes it the easiest kind of code to reason about and test.

Because pure functions have no hidden state, they can be cached, run in parallel, and reordered safely. Pushing as much logic as possible into pure functions and isolating the messy side-effect code is a core idea in functional programming.

③ Where It Appears
Math helpers like calculating a total
Data transformations in a pipeline
Formatting values for display
Easily unit-tested business rules
④ How It Works
Pure functions are easy to test, easy to cache, and safe to parallelize because they never reach outside themselves. Impure functions touch the world — they read clocks, mutate globals, or perform I/O.
⑤ Code Example
# Pure: depends only on inputs
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

# Impure: reads external state
counter = 0
def tick():
    global counter
    counter += 1
    return counter
// Pure
const add = (a, b) => a + b;

// Impure: mutates outside state
let counter = 0;
const tick = () => ++counter;
// Pure
static int add(int a, int b) {
    return a + b;
}

// Impure: depends on system time
static long now() {
    return System.currentTimeMillis();
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Touch global statedef tax(p): return p * rate # rate is global
✓ Take all inputsdef tax(p, rate): return p * rate
Why: a pure function depends only on its arguments, so it is predictable and testable.
❌ Hidden randomnessdef roll(): return random.randint(1, 6)
✓ Inject the sourcedef roll(rng): return rng.randint(1, 6)
Why: functions that read time or randomness are not pure. Pass those in to keep them deterministic.
⑥ Remember
Prefer pure functions for the core logic; isolate impure code at the edges of your app.
FAQ
What makes a function pure?
Same inputs always give the same output, and it causes no side effects.
Why prefer pure functions?
They are easy to test, cache, and reason about.
Can every function be pure?
No — I/O and randomness inherently are not.
What is memoization?
Caching a pure function's result for repeated inputs.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·06 RecursionO·10 Side Effects
cherry code › N·06
Recursion
A function that calls itself to solve a smaller version of the problem.
N·06functionsrecursion
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·01 Function
① Definition
"Recursion is when a function calls itself, breaking the problem into smaller subproblems until reaching a base case."
It needs two parts: a base case that stops the recursion, and a recursive case that moves closer to the base.
② Explanation

Recursion is when a function solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller piece of that same problem, combining the partial results as the calls unwind. It mirrors problems that are naturally self-similar, like exploring a folder full of folders.

Every recursion needs a base case — a condition that stops the calls — or it will run until the call stack overflows. Recursion can express tree- and divide-and-conquer algorithms far more cleanly than loops, at the cost of extra memory for the call stack.

③ Where It Appears
Walking a file system tree
Traversing tree and graph structures
Divide-and-conquer like merge sort
Computing factorials or Fibonacci numbers
④ How It Works
Each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack. When the base case returns, the stack unwinds, each frame combining its result. Forget the base case and you get a stack overflow.
⑤ Code Example
def factorial(n):
    if n <= 1:        # base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)

print(factorial(5))   # 120
function factorial(n) {
  if (n <= 1) return 1;   // base case
  return n * factorial(n - 1);
}
console.log(factorial(5));  // 120
static int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ No base casedef f(n): return f(n - 1)
✓ Stop at the basedef f(n): if n == 0: return 1 return n * f(n - 1)
Why: without a base case the calls never stop and the stack overflows.
❌ Never shrink the inputdef f(n): if n == 0: return 0 return f(n)
✓ Move toward basedef f(n): if n == 0: return 0 return f(n - 1)
Why: each call must get closer to the base case, or the recursion is infinite.
⑥ Remember
Always write the base case first. Without it, recursion is just an infinite loop with extra steps.
FAQ
What is recursion?
A function that calls itself to solve a smaller version of the problem.
Do I always need a base case?
Yes — it is what stops the recursion.
Recursion vs a loop?
Often interchangeable; loops avoid call-stack limits.
What is a stack overflow?
Recursing too deeply exhausts the call stack and crashes.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·07 ClosureC·05 For Loop
cherry code › N·07
Closure
A function that remembers variables from the scope where it was created.
N·07functionsscope
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·01 FunctionT·11 Scope
① Definition
"A closure is a function bundled with references to its surrounding state."
When a function is defined inside another function, it can keep using the outer function's variables even after the outer function has returned.
② Explanation

A closure is a function that captures and remembers variables from the scope where it was defined, even after that outer scope has finished running. The inner function carries its surrounding environment with it like a backpack.

Closures are the foundation of data privacy and stateful functions in many languages: the captured variables are accessible only through the closure, not from outside. This is how patterns like counters, callbacks, and module-private state are built.

③ Where It Appears
Creating a counter that keeps its own count
Building event handlers that remember context
Private variables in module patterns
Function factories that pre-fill arguments
④ How It Works
The inner function "closes over" the outer variables. Each call to the outer function creates a fresh closure with its own copy of the captured state — useful for factories, counters, and private data.
⑤ Code Example
def make_counter():
    count = 0
    def inc():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count
    return inc

c = make_counter()
print(c(), c(), c())   # 1 2 3
function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return () => ++count;
}
const c = makeCounter();
console.log(c(), c(), c());  // 1 2 3
// Java captures effectively-final vars in lambdas
Supplier<Integer> makeCounter() {
    int[] count = {0};
    return () -> ++count[0];
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Capture the loop variablefns = [lambda: i for i in range(3)] # all return 2
✓ Bind per iterationfns = [lambda i=i: i for i in range(3)]
Why: a closure captures the variable, not its value. By call time the loop has finished, so default-binding freezes each value.
⑥ Remember
A closure is a function plus its backpack — it carries its birthplace's variables wherever it goes.
FAQ
What is a closure?
A function that remembers variables from the scope where it was created.
Why are they useful?
Function factories, callbacks, and keeping private state.
What is the classic bug?
Capturing a loop variable, so every closure sees its final value.
Are they common in JavaScript?
Yes — callbacks and event handlers rely on closures constantly.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·08 Lambda / Arrow FunctionT·11 Scope
cherry code › N·08
Lambda / Arrow Function
A short, anonymous function — usually a one-liner.
N·08functionslambda
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·01 Function
① Definition
"A lambda (or arrow function) is a compact, anonymous function expression."
It has no name and is usually used inline as an argument to another function, especially for callbacks and quick transformations.
② Explanation

A lambda or arrow function is a compact, nameless function written inline, usually as a single expression. It exists for situations where defining a full named function would be overkill — you just need a small piece of behavior right where you use it.

These functions are most often passed as arguments to other functions, such as the test in a filter or the rule in a sort. Their brevity is a strength for short logic and a weakness when the body grows, at which point a named function reads better.

③ Where It Appears
The key function for sorting a list
Callbacks passed to map and filter
Short event handlers in UI code
One-line transforms in data pipelines
④ How It Works
Python uses the lambda keyword for single-expression functions. JavaScript uses arrow syntax (x) => x * 2. Java uses the same arrow syntax for functional interfaces.
⑤ Code Example
double = lambda x: x * 2
print(double(5))   # 10

nums = [1, 2, 3]
print(list(map(lambda x: x * 2, nums)))
const double = x => x * 2;
console.log(double(5));  // 10

[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2);  // [2, 4, 6]
Function<Integer, Integer> doubler = x -> x * 2;
System.out.println(doubler.apply(5));  // 10
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Multi-statement lambdaf = lambda x: print(x); return x
✓ Use def for logicdef f(x): print(x) return x
Why: a Python lambda holds one expression. Anything with statements needs a real def.
❌ Assume arrow has own thisbtn.onclick = () => this.count++
✓ Know arrows inherit this// arrow uses the surrounding this on purpose
Why: JS arrow functions do not bind their own this; they inherit it from the enclosing scope.
⑥ Remember
If a lambda grows past one line, give it a real name. Anonymous functions stop helping when they get complex.
FAQ
What is a lambda?
A small anonymous function written as a single expression.
lambda vs def?
Use def when it needs a name or more than one line.
What is an arrow function?
JavaScript's concise function syntax with lexical this.
When are they handy?
Short callbacks for map, filter, and sort.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·12 Higher-Order FunctionsN·01 Function
cherry code › N·09
Default Arguments
Parameter values used when the caller doesn't provide one.
N·09functionsdefaults
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·02 Parameter
① Definition
"A default argument is a fallback value assigned to a parameter when no argument is passed."
It makes parameters optional and removes the need for repetitive null checks in callers.
② Explanation

Default arguments let a parameter fall back to a preset value when the caller omits it, so a single function can serve both the common case and customized calls. This trims the number of overloads or wrapper functions you would otherwise need.

Defaults are evaluated according to language-specific rules — Python notably evaluates them once at definition time, which makes mutable defaults like an empty list a famous trap. Used carefully, they make function signatures cleaner and calls shorter.

③ Where It Appears
A connect(timeout=30) with a sensible default
Optional formatting options on a function
Config functions where most values rarely change
APIs that stay backward-compatible as they grow
④ How It Works
Python and JS let you write def f(x=10) or function f(x = 10). If the caller omits the argument, the default is used. Java has no built-in defaults — you overload methods instead.
⑤ Code Example
def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    return f"{greeting}, {name}!"

print(greet("Ada"))           # Hello, Ada!
print(greet("Ada", "Hi"))     # Hi, Ada!
function greet(name, greeting = "Hello") {
  return `${greeting}, ${name}!`;
}
greet("Ada");          // Hello, Ada!
greet("Ada", "Hi");    // Hi, Ada!
// Java uses overloading instead of defaults
static String greet(String name) {
    return greet(name, "Hello");
}
static String greet(String name, String g) {
    return g + ", " + name + "!";
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Mutable defaultdef add(x, acc=[]): acc.append(x)
✓ Default to Nonedef add(x, acc=None): acc = acc or [] acc.append(x)
Why: a default list is created once and shared by every call, so it keeps growing.
❌ Default before requireddef f(a=1, b):
✓ Defaults lastdef f(b, a=1):
Why: parameters with defaults must come after those without, or it is a syntax error.
⑥ Remember
In Python, never use mutable defaults like def f(x=[]). The list is shared across calls and causes infamous bugs.
FAQ
What is a default argument?
A fallback value used when the caller omits that argument.
Why avoid mutable defaults?
They are created once and shared across all calls.
What is the order rule?
Parameters with defaults must follow those without.
How do I override a default?
Pass the argument explicitly when calling.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·10 *args / **kwargsN·02 Parameter
cherry code › N·10
*args / **kwargs
Variable-length arguments — accept any number of inputs.
N·10functionsvariadic
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·09 Default Arguments
① Definition
"Variadic parameters let a function accept an arbitrary number of arguments."
Python uses *args for positional and **kwargs for keyword arguments. JavaScript uses rest parameters ...args. Java uses Type... args.
② Explanation

The *args and **kwargs syntax lets a function accept any number of positional or keyword arguments, gathering the extras into a tuple and a dictionary. This frees a function from committing to a fixed parameter count.

They are essential for writing wrappers and decorators that forward arbitrary arguments to another function without knowing them in advance. The same star/double-star can also unpack a collection back into individual arguments at the call site.

③ Where It Appears
A sum that accepts any count of numbers
Decorators forwarding arguments transparently
Flexible logging or formatting helpers
Building wrapper functions around other APIs
④ How It Works
Inside the function, args behaves like a tuple/array and kwargs like a dict/object. The caller can pass zero or more values; they are collected automatically.
⑤ Code Example
def total(*args, **kwargs):
    return sum(args) + sum(kwargs.values())

print(total(1, 2, 3, bonus=10))  # 16
function total(...nums) {
  return nums.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
}
console.log(total(1, 2, 3, 4));  // 10
static int total(int... nums) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int n : nums) sum += n;
    return sum;
}
total(1, 2, 3, 4);  // 10
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Forget to unpacknums = [1, 2, 3] total(nums) # passes one list
✓ Spread with *total(*nums) # passes 1, 2, 3
Why: without * you pass the list itself, not its elements as separate arguments.
❌ Wrong parameter orderdef f(**kw, *args):
✓ Correct orderdef f(a, *args, b=1, **kwargs):
Why: the order is positional, then *args, then keyword, then **kwargs.
⑥ Remember
The names args and kwargs are convention, not law — what matters are the stars * and **.
FAQ
What is *args?
It collects any extra positional arguments into a tuple.
What is **kwargs?
It collects any extra keyword arguments into a dict.
When should I use them?
For flexible APIs and wrapper functions that forward arguments.
Are the names required?
No — args/kwargs are convention; the * and ** do the work.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·11 Function ScopeN·09 Default Arguments
cherry code › N·11
Function Scope
Variables declared in a function exist only inside it.
N·11functionsscope
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·11 ScopeN·01 Function
① Definition
"Function scope means variables declared inside a function are visible only within that function."
Outside the function, those names don't exist. This keeps internal details from leaking and prevents accidental conflicts.
② Explanation

Function scope means variables created inside a function live only there — they are born when the function is called and vanish when it returns, invisible to the outside world. This isolation is what keeps functions from clobbering each other's data.

Scope is what makes functions safe to reuse: two functions can both have a variable named i without interfering. Understanding the boundary between local and outer scope explains why a value computed inside a function must be returned to be used elsewhere.

③ Where It Appears
Loop counters that stay local to a function
Temporary variables in a calculation
Avoiding name clashes between functions
Protecting helper state from outside code
④ How It Works
When a function is called, a new local namespace is created. When it returns, that namespace is discarded. Variables defined outside the function (global) are visible inside unless shadowed by a local with the same name.
⑤ Code Example
def demo():
    secret = 42           # local
    print(secret)

demo()
print(secret)              # NameError
function demo() {
  let secret = 42;       // local
  console.log(secret);
}
demo();
console.log(secret);       // ReferenceError
static void demo() {
    int secret = 42;       // local
    System.out.println(secret);
}
// secret not visible here
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Rebind global by accidentcount = 0 def inc(): count = count + 1 # UnboundLocalError
✓ Be explicitdef inc(count): return count + 1
Why: assigning to a name makes it local. Reading it first then assigning raises an error unless you declare global.
❌ Expect local outsidedef f(): msg = "hi" print(msg)
✓ Return itdef f(): return "hi" print(f())
Why: a local name does not exist once the function ends.
⑥ Remember
Locals beat globals. Reaching out to a global variable from inside a function is a sign your design needs work.
FAQ
What is function scope?
Names created inside a function are local and vanish when it returns.
What does global do?
It lets a function rebind a module-level variable.
What is nonlocal?
It rebinds a variable in an enclosing (but not global) function.
Why prefer locals?
Local state keeps functions predictable and easy to test.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·07 ClosureT·11 Scope
cherry code › N·12
Higher-Order Functions
Functions that take or return other functions.
N·12functionsfunctional
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·01 FunctionN·08 Lambda / Arrow Function
① Definition
"A higher-order function takes another function as input, returns a function, or both."
This treats functions like values — passing them around, storing them in lists, building them on the fly.
② Explanation

A higher-order function treats other functions as ordinary data: it either accepts a function as an argument, returns a function, or both. This turns behavior itself into something you can pass around and customize.

They are the backbone of expressive collection processing — map, filter, and reduce all take a function describing what to do with each element. Higher-order functions let you separate the structure of an operation from the specific logic plugged into it.

③ Where It Appears
map, filter, and reduce on collections
Sort that takes a custom comparator
Decorators that wrap another function
Event systems that accept handler functions
④ How It Works
Common examples are map, filter, and reduce, which take a function and apply it to every element of a collection. They let you express transformations as data flow rather than loops.
⑤ Code Example
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
squared = list(map(lambda x: x**2, nums))
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x%2==0, nums))
print(squared, evens)
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4];
const squared = nums.map(x => x ** 2);
const evens = nums.filter(x => x % 2 === 0);
console.log(squared, evens);
List<Integer> nums = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4);
nums.stream()
    .map(x -> x * x)
    .forEach(System.out::println);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Call instead of passmap(double(), nums)
✓ Pass the functionmap(double, nums)
Why: double passes the function; double() calls it and passes its result.
❌ Forget map is lazyresult = map(f, xs) print(result) # map object
✓ Materialize itresult = list(map(f, xs))
Why: in Python 3, map and filter are lazy. Wrap in list() to get values.
⑥ Remember
Functions are first-class citizens — anywhere a value can go, a function can go too.
FAQ
What is a higher-order function?
One that takes a function as an argument or returns one.
Examples?
map, filter, reduce, and sorted(key=...).
Why are they useful?
They let you compose behavior and cut repetitive boilerplate.
Pass vs call?
Pass the name without parentheses to defer the call.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·08 Lambda / Arrow FunctionN·05 Pure Function
6
Block 6 · Data Structures
Shapes of Data
Data structures decide how information is stored, found, and changed. Picking the right shape — list, dict, tree, graph — is half the battle in writing fast, clear code.
ArraysMapsStacksTreesBig O
12 terms · S·01 → S·12 structure it
cherry code › S·01
Array
A fixed-size, ordered sequence of values accessed by index.
S·01datasequence
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·08 Array / List
① Definition
"An array is a contiguous block of memory holding a fixed number of elements of the same type."
You access elements in constant time with a numeric index that starts at 0 in most languages.
② Explanation

An array stores a fixed number of elements of the same type in one contiguous block of memory, reachable instantly by their numeric index. That contiguous layout is what gives arrays their signature strength: constant-time access to any element.

The fixed size is the tradeoff — you must know the capacity up front, and growing means allocating a new, larger array and copying everything over. Arrays are the low-level building block underneath many higher-level structures like lists and hash tables.

③ Where It Appears
Storing pixel data for an image
Fixed-size lookup tables
Buffers for reading file or network data
The backing store inside dynamic lists
④ How It Works
Because elements are stored side by side in memory, the address of element i is just base + i * size. That's why index access is constant time. Insertion in the middle, however, requires shifting elements.
