⑤ Code Example
# Python uses 'list' for arrays
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]
print(arr[0])      # 10
print(len(arr))    # 4
const arr = [10, 20, 30, 40];
console.log(arr[0]);    // 10
console.log(arr.length); // 4
int[] arr = {10, 20, 30, 40};
System.out.println(arr[0]);     // 10
System.out.println(arr.length); // 4
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Index past the endarr[len(arr)]
✓ Last valid indexarr[len(arr) - 1]
Why: valid indices run 0 to length-1. Reading the length-th slot is out of bounds.
❌ Assume fixed size// Java: arr.length cannot change
✓ Pick the right type# Python lists grow; arrays are fixed
Why: a true array has a fixed length. Python's list is a dynamic array that resizes.
⑥ Remember
Indices start at 0 in almost every language. The last index is length - 1.
FAQ
Array vs list?
An array is fixed-size and contiguous; a list usually grows dynamically.
Zero-indexed?
Yes — the first element is at index 0.
Is random access fast?
Yes — reading by index is O(1).
Cost of inserting in the middle?
O(n), because later elements must shift.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·02 ListS·11 Hash Table
cherry code › S·02
List
A dynamic, resizable, ordered collection of items.
S·02datadynamic
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·01 Array
① Definition
"A list is a dynamic array — an ordered collection that can grow or shrink at runtime."
Lists support adding, removing, and inserting items at any position. They are the everyday workhorse data structure.
② Explanation

A list is a resizable, ordered collection that grows and shrinks as you add or remove items, freeing you from declaring a size in advance. It is the everyday workhorse container in most high-level languages.

Under the hood a list usually wraps a dynamic array that quietly reallocates as it fills, so appends are fast on average while inserting in the middle requires shifting elements. Its flexibility makes it the default choice when you are not sure how many items you will have.

③ Where It Appears
Collecting user inputs as they arrive
Building up search results dynamically
A to-do list that items are added to and removed from
Accumulating rows parsed from a file
④ How It Works
Under the hood a list is usually backed by an array that the runtime resizes as needed (doubling its capacity). Appending at the end is amortized O(1); inserting in the middle is O(n).
⑤ Code Example
items = ["a", "b"]
items.append("c")
items.insert(0, "z")
items.pop()
print(items)   # ['z', 'a', 'b']
const items = ["a", "b"];
items.push("c");
items.unshift("z");
items.pop();
console.log(items);  // ['z', 'a', 'b']
List<String> items = new ArrayList<>();
items.add("a");
items.add("b");
items.add(0, "z");
items.remove(items.size() - 1);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Copy by assignmentb = a b.append(9) # a changed too
✓ Make a real copyb = a[:] # or list(a)
Why: assignment copies the reference, not the list. Both names point to the same object.
❌ Remove while iteratingfor x in nums: if x < 0: nums.remove(x)
✓ Build a new listnums = [x for x in nums if x >= 0]
Why: removing during iteration skips elements. A comprehension builds a clean result.
⑥ Remember
Append at the end whenever you can. Inserting at index 0 in a long list is slow — it shifts everything.
FAQ
List vs array?
A Python list grows and shrinks; an array has a fixed length.
Cost of append?
Amortized O(1).
Shallow vs deep copy?
a[:] is shallow; nested objects are still shared.
Cost of searching by value?
O(n) — it scans element by element.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·03 TupleS·04 Dictionary / Map
cherry code › S·03
Tuple
An immutable, ordered group of values.
S·03dataimmutable
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·02 List
① Definition
"A tuple is an ordered, fixed-size sequence of values that cannot be changed after creation."
Tuples are often used to group related values, like coordinates or a function's multiple return values.
② Explanation

A tuple is an ordered, fixed group of values that cannot be changed after creation. Its immutability is a feature: once built, a tuple is a guaranteed-stable bundle that can be safely shared and used as a dictionary key or set member.

Tuples are ideal for grouping a small, fixed set of related values that belong together, such as coordinates or a function returning several results at once. They signal intent — "this collection has a fixed shape" — in a way a mutable list does not.

③ Where It Appears
Returning multiple values from a function
Representing (x, y) coordinate pairs
Fixed records like (name, age, city)
Keys in a dictionary that need composite values
④ How It Works
Python has a dedicated tuple type with (a, b) syntax. JavaScript and Java fake it with arrays or small records. Because tuples are immutable, they can be used as dictionary keys in Python.
⑤ Code Example
point = (3, 4)
x, y = point        # unpacking
print(x, y)         # 3 4
# point[0] = 9       # TypeError — immutable
// JS uses arrays + Object.freeze for tuples
const point = Object.freeze([3, 4]);
const [x, y] = point;
console.log(x, y);   // 3 4
// Records (Java 14+) act like tuples
record Point(int x, int y) {}
Point p = new Point(3, 4);
System.out.println(p.x() + "," + p.y());
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Try to modify itpoint = (1, 2) point[0] = 9
✓ Build a new tuplepoint = (9, point[1])
Why: tuples are immutable. To "change" one, create a new tuple.
❌ One-element confusiont = (5) # this is just 5
✓ Trailing commat = (5,) # a 1-tuple
Why: parentheses alone do not make a tuple; the comma does.
⑥ Remember
Tuples scream "this won't change". They're for fixed bundles of data, not for collections that grow.
FAQ
Tuple vs list?
A tuple is immutable; a list can be changed.
Why use tuples?
Fixed records, safety against accidental edits, and as dict keys.
Can a tuple be a dict key?
Yes, if all its elements are hashable.
How do I unpack one?
x, y = point.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·04 Dictionary / MapS·05 Set
cherry code › S·04
Dictionary / Map
A collection of key → value pairs with fast lookup.
S·04datamap
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:T·09 Dict / Object / Map
① Definition
"A dictionary (or map) stores values indexed by unique keys instead of integer positions."
You look up data by key name, getting O(1) average-time access for both reads and writes.
② Explanation

A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs, letting you look up a value by its key almost instantly instead of scanning through positions. It models any "this maps to that" relationship directly, the way a real dictionary maps words to definitions.

Keys must be unique and are typically hashed to locate values in roughly constant time, which is why dictionaries are the go-to structure whenever fast lookup by name or id matters more than order. The tradeoff is extra memory and, traditionally, no guaranteed ordering.

③ Where It Appears
Looking up a user by their id
Counting word frequencies in text
Caching results keyed by input
Storing configuration as name-value settings
④ How It Works
Internally, the dictionary computes a hash of the key to find its slot in a backing array. Two different keys can hash to the same slot (a collision), which the implementation resolves transparently.
⑤ Code Example
user = {"name": "Ada", "age": 30}
print(user["name"])     # Ada
user["city"] = "Rio"
for k, v in user.items():
    print(k, v)
const user = { name: "Ada", age: 30 };
console.log(user.name);     // Ada
user.city = "Rio";
for (const [k, v] of Object.entries(user)) {
  console.log(k, v);
}
Map<String, Object> user = new HashMap<>();
user.put("name", "Ada");
user.put("age", 30);
System.out.println(user.get("name"));
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Crash on missing keyd["missing"]
✓ Use getd.get("missing", 0)
Why: bracket access raises KeyError; get returns a default.
❌ Unhashable keyd[[1, 2]] = "x"
✓ Use a hashable keyd[(1, 2)] = "x"
Why: keys must be hashable. Lists are not; tuples are.
⑥ Remember
Keys must be hashable. In Python you can use strings, numbers, or tuples — but not lists.
FAQ
Lookup cost?
O(1) on average.
Is it ordered?
Yes — insertion order is preserved since Python 3.7.
What can be a key?
Any hashable, immutable value.
How do I iterate pairs?
for k, v in d.items():.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·05 SetS·11 Hash Table
cherry code › S·05
Set
An unordered collection of unique items.
S·05dataunique
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·04 Dictionary / Map
① Definition
"A set is a collection that holds each value at most once and has no defined order."
Sets are perfect for membership tests and removing duplicates from data.
② Explanation

A set is a collection that holds only unique items and answers membership questions — "is x in here?" — very quickly. Adding a duplicate simply does nothing, which makes sets a natural way to eliminate repeats.

Built on hashing like dictionaries, sets sacrifice ordering and indexed access in exchange for fast lookup, insertion, and mathematical operations like union and intersection. Reach for a set whenever uniqueness or fast membership testing is the point.

③ Where It Appears
Removing duplicates from a list
Tracking which items have been seen
Fast 'is this allowed?' membership checks
Finding common elements between two groups
④ How It Works
Sets are typically implemented with a hash table just like dictionaries, but storing only the keys. Adding a duplicate is a no-op. Set operations like union and intersection are built in.
⑤ Code Example
tags = {"a", "b", "a"}
print(tags)              # {'a', 'b'}
tags.add("c")
print("a" in tags)        # True
print(tags | {"d"})      # union
const tags = new Set(["a", "b", "a"]);
console.log(tags.size);       // 2
tags.add("c");
console.log(tags.has("a"));   // true
Set<String> tags = new HashSet<>();
tags.add("a"); tags.add("b"); tags.add("a");
System.out.println(tags.size());    // 2
System.out.println(tags.contains("a"));
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Expect orderings = {3, 1, 2} list(s)[0] # not always 3
✓ Sort when you need ordersorted(s)
Why: sets are unordered. Convert with sorted if order matters.
❌ Empty bracess = {} # this is a dict
✓ Use set()s = set()
Why: {} creates an empty dict. Use set() for an empty set.
⑥ Remember
To deduplicate a list, wrap it in a set and back to a list: list(set(items)).
FAQ
What is a set?
An unordered collection of unique values.
Membership cost?
O(1) on average — far faster than scanning a list.
What happens to duplicates?
They are removed automatically.
What operations are built in?
Union, intersection, and difference.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·11 Hash TableS·04 Dictionary / Map
cherry code › S·06
Stack
LIFO collection — last in, first out.
S·06dataLIFO
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·02 List
① Definition
"A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) collection: the most recently added item is the first one removed."
Think of a pile of plates — you take from the top. The two main operations are push (add) and pop (remove).
② Explanation

A stack is a last-in, first-out collection: you can only add (push) or remove (pop) at the top, like a stack of plates. The most recently added item is always the first one to come back out.

This restriction is exactly what you want for tracking nested or reversible activity — the program remembers where it has been and unwinds in reverse order. The runtime itself uses a stack to manage function calls, which is why deep recursion can overflow it.

③ Where It Appears
Undo/redo history in editors
The browser's back button
The call stack of running functions
Matching brackets and evaluating expressions
④ How It Works
Stacks power function calls (the call stack), undo history, and expression parsing. Both push and pop are O(1). You can implement one with an array and a top pointer.
⑤ Code Example
stack = []
stack.append(1)
stack.append(2)
stack.append(3)
print(stack.pop())   # 3
print(stack.pop())   # 2
const stack = [];
stack.push(1);
stack.push(2);
stack.push(3);
console.log(stack.pop());  // 3
console.log(stack.pop());  // 2
Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
stack.push(1);
stack.push(2);
stack.push(3);
System.out.println(stack.pop());  // 3
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Pop when emptystack.pop() # IndexError
✓ Check firstif stack: stack.pop()
Why: popping an empty stack raises an error. Always check it is non-empty.
❌ Mix up the order# treat it as first-in-first-out
✓ LIFO# last pushed is first popped
Why: a stack is LIFO — last in, first out. A queue is the FIFO one.
⑥ Remember
Every function call you make uses a stack — your code's history is literally a pile of frames.
FAQ
What is a stack?
A LIFO structure — the last item added is the first removed.
What are the operations?
push to add and pop to remove from the top.
Where are stacks used?
The call stack, undo history, and expression parsing.
How do I implement one in Python?
A list with append and pop.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·07 QueueS·08 Linked List
cherry code › S·07
Queue
FIFO collection — first in, first out.
S·07dataFIFO
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·06 Stack
① Definition
"A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) collection: items are removed in the same order they were added."
Like a line at a checkout — the first to arrive is the first served. Operations are enqueue and dequeue.
② Explanation

A queue is a first-in, first-out collection: items are added at the back (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue), exactly like a line of people waiting their turn. The earliest arrival is always served first.

This fairness makes queues the standard structure for buffering work that must be handled in arrival order. They underpin scheduling, message passing, and breadth-first traversal, where order of processing matters as much as the items themselves.

③ Where It Appears
Print jobs waiting for the printer
Task and job scheduling systems
Message queues between services
Breadth-first search over a graph
④ How It Works
Queues power task scheduling, message buses, and breadth-first search. Implementations use a linked list or a double-ended array (deque) to keep both ends O(1).
⑤ Code Example
from collections import deque
q = deque()
q.append("a")
q.append("b")
print(q.popleft())   # 'a'
print(q.popleft())   # 'b'
const q = [];
q.push("a");
q.push("b");
console.log(q.shift());  // 'a'
console.log(q.shift());  // 'b'
Queue<String> q = new ArrayDeque<>();
q.offer("a");
q.offer("b");
System.out.println(q.poll());  // 'a'
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Pop from front of a listqueue.pop(0) # O(n)
✓ Use a dequefrom collections import deque q = deque(); q.popleft()
Why: list.pop(0) shifts every element. deque pops from the front in O(1).
❌ Treat it like a stack# last in, first out
✓ FIFO# first in, first out
Why: a queue serves items in arrival order. LIFO is the stack.
⑥ Remember
Don't use list.pop(0) in Python — it's O(n). Use collections.deque for real queues.
FAQ
What is a queue?
A FIFO structure — first in, first out.
What are the operations?
enqueue to add at the back, dequeue to remove from the front.
Why use a deque?
It pops from both ends in O(1).
Where are queues used?
Task scheduling, breadth-first search, and buffers.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·06 StackS·10 Graph
cherry code › S·08
Linked List
A chain of nodes where each node points to the next.
S·08datanodes
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·02 List
① Definition
"A linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node contains a value and a pointer to the next node."
Unlike an array, nodes are scattered in memory. Insertion and removal at known positions are O(1), but lookup by index is O(n).
② Explanation

A linked list strings data together as a chain of nodes, where each node holds a value and a pointer to the next node. Instead of one contiguous block, the items can live anywhere in memory, connected only by these links.

This makes inserting or deleting in the middle cheap — you just rewire a couple of pointers — but accessing the nth item is slow because you must walk the chain from the start. It is the right structure when you do frequent insertions and removals and rarely need random access.

③ Where It Appears
Implementing stacks and queues
Playlists with next/previous links
Undo chains that grow and shrink
Hash table buckets that chain collisions
④ How It Works
A singly linked list has a single next pointer per node. A doubly linked list also has a prev pointer. To find element i, you must walk from the head, one node at a time.
⑤ Code Example
class Node:
    def __init__(self, value, nxt=None):
        self.value = value
        self.next = nxt

head = Node(1, Node(2, Node(3)))
print(head.next.value)   # 2
class Node {
  constructor(value, next = null) {
    this.value = value;
    this.next = next;
  }
}
const head = new Node(1, new Node(2, new Node(3)));
class Node {
    int value;
    Node next;
    Node(int v) { value = v; }
}
Node head = new Node(1);
head.next = new Node(2);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Lose the headhead = head.next # original head gone
✓ Keep a referencenode = head node = node.next
Why: if you overwrite the head pointer you lose access to the whole list. Walk with a separate variable.
❌ Expect O(1) indexinglist[500]
✓ Traverse to reach it# follow next 500 times: O(n)
Why: linked lists have no random access. Reaching an element means walking the chain.
⑥ Remember
Linked lists are famous in interviews, rare in real code. Most languages prefer dynamic arrays for everyday work.
FAQ
What is a linked list?
A chain of nodes, each holding a value and a pointer to the next node.
How does it compare to an array?
No contiguous memory; access is O(n) but insertion at a known node is O(1).
Singly vs doubly linked?
A doubly linked list also stores a pointer to the previous node.
When should I use one?
When you insert and remove at the ends frequently.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·09 TreeS·06 Stack
cherry code › S·09
Tree
A hierarchical structure of nodes with parent-child links.
S·09datahierarchy
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·08 Linked List
① Definition
"A tree is a hierarchical data structure with one root node and child nodes branching out below."
Each node has zero or more children and exactly one parent (except the root, which has none).
② Explanation

A tree is a hierarchical structure where each node has one parent and any number of children, branching out from a single root. It captures relationships that are naturally nested — anything that contains things which contain other things.

Trees enable fast, structured search and organization: balanced trees keep operations logarithmic, and the parent-child layout maps cleanly onto real hierarchies. Most variations differ in how many children a node may have and how the data is ordered.

③ Where It Appears
File systems with nested folders
The DOM behind every web page
Decision trees and game move trees
Database indexes for fast lookup
④ How It Works
Trees model hierarchies — file systems, the DOM, decision trees. A binary tree limits each node to at most two children. Traversals (in-order, pre-order, post-order) visit every node in a specific sequence.
⑤ Code Example
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, v):
        self.value = v
        self.left = self.right = None

root = TreeNode(1)
root.left = TreeNode(2)
root.right = TreeNode(3)
class TreeNode {
  constructor(v) {
    this.value = v;
    this.left = null;
    this.right = null;
  }
}
const root = new TreeNode(1);
root.left = new TreeNode(2);
class TreeNode {
    int value;
    TreeNode left, right;
    TreeNode(int v) { value = v; }
}
TreeNode root = new TreeNode(1);
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ No null-node casedef height(n): return 1 + max(height(n.left), height(n.right))
✓ Handle the empty nodedef height(n): if n is None: return 0 return 1 + max(height(n.left), height(n.right))
Why: recursion on a tree must stop at empty children or it crashes on a missing node.
❌ Assume balanced# expect O(log n) always
✓ Know the worst case# a skewed tree degrades to O(n)
Why: a tree that leans to one side behaves like a linked list.
⑥ Remember
Folders, family trees, the DOM, JSON — most hierarchies you meet are trees in disguise.
FAQ
What is a tree?
A hierarchy of nodes with a single root and no cycles.
What is a binary tree?
One where each node has at most two children.
What traversals exist?
In-order, pre-order, post-order, and breadth-first.
Where are trees used?
File systems, the HTML DOM, and database indexes.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·10 GraphS·11 Hash Table
cherry code › S·10
Graph
Nodes connected by edges — relationships in any shape.
S·10datagraph
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·09 Tree
① Definition
"A graph is a set of nodes (vertices) connected by edges, with no required hierarchy."
Graphs model networks: social connections, maps, dependencies. Edges can be directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted.
② Explanation

A graph models things and the connections between them as nodes joined by edges, with no restriction on shape — connections can form cycles, run in one or both directions, and carry weights. It is the most general way to represent relationships.

Because so many real systems are networks of related entities, graphs power routing, recommendation, and dependency analysis. The price of that generality is more complex algorithms for traversal, shortest paths, and cycle detection than simpler structures need.

③ Where It Appears
Social networks of friends and follows
Maps and GPS shortest-path routing
Dependency graphs between software modules
Recommendation engines linking items
④ How It Works
A common representation is an adjacency list: a dict mapping each node to its neighbors. Common algorithms include BFS (breadth-first) and DFS (depth-first) for traversal, and Dijkstra for shortest paths.
⑤ Code Example
graph = {
    "A": ["B", "C"],
    "B": ["D"],
    "C": ["D"],
    "D": []
}
print(graph["A"])   # ['B', 'C']
const graph = {
  A: ["B", "C"],
  B: ["D"],
  C: ["D"],
  D: []
};
console.log(graph.A);   // ['B', 'C']
Map<String, List<String>> graph = new HashMap<>();
graph.put("A", List.of("B", "C"));
graph.put("B", List.of("D"));
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Ignore cyclesdef dfs(n): for m in n.neighbors: dfs(m)
✓ Track visiteddef dfs(n, seen): if n in seen: return seen.add(n) for m in n.neighbors: dfs(m, seen)
Why: graphs can contain cycles. Without a visited set, traversal loops forever.
❌ Wrong edge model# add edge a->b only
✓ Match directedness# undirected: add a->b and b->a
Why: in an undirected graph an edge connects both ways; model it on both ends.
⑥ Remember
A tree is a graph with rules. A graph is what you get when you let trees have any shape.
FAQ
What is a graph?
A set of nodes connected by edges.
How is it different from a tree?
A tree is a graph with no cycles and a single root.
How are graphs represented?
As an adjacency list or an adjacency matrix.
What traversals are common?
Breadth-first (BFS) and depth-first (DFS).
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·07 QueueS·12 Big O Notation
cherry code › S·11
Hash Table
The engine behind fast key-value lookup.
S·11datahash
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·04 Dictionary / Map
① Definition
"A hash table stores key-value pairs in an array, using a hash function to map each key to an index."
It is the implementation behind Python dicts, JavaScript objects, and Java HashMaps.
② Explanation

A hash table is the machinery that makes key-value lookup fast: it runs each key through a hash function to compute an index into an underlying array, so the value can be found directly rather than by searching. This is what gives dictionaries and sets their near-constant-time speed.

When two keys hash to the same slot — a collision — the table resolves it by chaining entries or probing for an open slot. A good hash function and a sensible load factor keep collisions rare, which is why performance stays fast in practice.

③ Where It Appears
The engine inside dictionaries and sets
Database indexing for instant lookup
Caches keyed by request or url
Deduplication and fast membership tests
④ How It Works
The hash function turns a key into a number, then takes that number modulo the array size to pick a slot. Collisions are resolved with chaining (a list per slot) or open addressing (probe to the next slot).
⑤ Code Example
# dict is a hash table
print(hash("hello"))   # big integer
table = {"hello": 1, "world": 2}
print(table["hello"])   # O(1) average
// Object and Map are hash tables
const table = new Map();
table.set("hello", 1);
table.set("world", 2);
console.log(table.get("hello"));
HashMap<String, Integer> table = new HashMap<>();
table.put("hello", 1);
table.put("world", 2);
System.out.println(table.get("hello"));
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Mutable keytable[[1, 2]] = "x"
✓ Immutable keytable[(1, 2)] = "x"
Why: a key must be hashable. Lists can change, so they cannot be hashed; tuples can.
❌ Assume O(1) always# every lookup is constant time
✓ Account for collisions# many collisions degrade to O(n)
Why: hashing is O(1) on average, but heavy collisions can slow it to O(n).
⑥ Remember
Average O(1) is the dream. With a bad hash, worst case becomes O(n) — collisions matter.
FAQ
What is a hash table?
A structure that maps keys to slots using a hash function.
Average lookup cost?
O(1).
What is a collision?
When two keys hash to the same slot.
Is the Python dict a hash table?
Yes — it is built on one.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·04 Dictionary / MapS·12 Big O Notation
cherry code › S·12
Big O Notation
How algorithm time or space grows as input size grows.
S·12datacomplexity
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·01 ArrayC·10 Nested Loops
① Definition
"Big O describes how an algorithm's resource use scales with the size of the input."
It's the language for talking about efficiency. Common classes: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2ⁿ).
② Explanation

Big O notation describes how an algorithm's running time or memory grows as the input gets larger, ignoring constant factors and focusing on the dominant term. It lets you compare algorithms by their scaling behavior rather than by raw speed on one machine.

The point is to predict what happens at scale: an O(n) algorithm that doubles work when input doubles will crush an O(n²) one on big data, even if the slower one wins on tiny inputs. Knowing the common classes — O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²) — guides which data structure or approach to choose.

③ Where It Appears
Comparing two sorting algorithms
Choosing a data structure for scale
Spotting why code slows on large inputs
Technical interview complexity questions
④ How It Works
Big O ignores constants and lower-order terms — it shows the shape of growth. An O(n²) algorithm slows down quadratically, so doubling the input quadruples the work. O(log n) barely flinches.
⑤ Code Example
# O(1) — constant time
def first(arr): return arr[0]

# O(n) — linear time
def total(arr):
    s = 0
    for x in arr: s += x
    return s

# O(n²) — nested loops
def pairs(arr):
    for a in arr:
        for b in arr:
            print(a, b)
// O(1)
const first = arr => arr[0];

// O(n)
const total = arr => arr.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);

// O(n²)
const pairs = arr => {
  for (const a of arr)
    for (const b of arr) console.log(a, b);
};
// O(1)
int first(int[] arr) { return arr[0]; }

// O(n)
int total(int[] arr) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int x : arr) s += x;
    return s;
}
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Count exact steps# "it does 3n + 5 ops"
✓ Describe growth# O(n): grows linearly with n
Why: Big O drops constants and lower terms. It describes how cost scales, not an exact count.
❌ Two loops = O(2n)for a in xs: ... for b in xs: ...
✓ Still O(n)# sequential loops add: O(n) + O(n) = O(n)
Why: nested loops multiply (O(n×n)); sequential loops add and constants drop.
⑥ Remember
O(n²) inside a loop with a million items is a disaster. Always ask: how does this scale?
FAQ
What is Big O?
A way to describe how runtime or memory grows as the input grows.
What are the common classes?
O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), and O(n^2).
Best, worst, or average?
Big O usually describes the worst case.
Why does it matter?
It predicts how code behaves on large inputs.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·11 Hash TableC·10 Nested Loops
7
Block 7 · Object-Oriented Programming
Code as Objects
OOP lets you model the world as objects that bundle data with behavior. Classes describe the shape; instances breathe life into it. Master inheritance, polymorphism, and encapsulation and you'll think in systems, not scripts.
ClassObjectInheritancePolymorphismEncapsulation
12 terms · P·01 → P·12 model it
cherry code › P·01
Class
A blueprint that defines the shape and behavior of objects.
P·01oopblueprint
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A class is a template that describes the attributes and methods objects of that type will have."
It's like a cookie cutter: the class doesn't do anything by itself — you use it to create objects (instances) that follow its shape.
② Explanation

A class is the modeling unit of object-oriented programming. It bundles together the data an object will hold (its attributes) and the operations that act on that data (its methods) into a single named type, so related state and behavior travel together instead of being scattered across loose functions and variables.

Defining a class is an act of design: you decide what real-world or domain concept you are modeling, what properties matter, and what it should be able to do. Nothing happens until you instantiate it - the class itself is just the description, and every object you create from it shares that same structure while holding its own values.

③ Where It Appears
Modeling domain entities like User, Order, or Invoice
Defining UI components such as a Button or Modal
Wrapping a database table row as an object
Grouping configuration and its related logic
④ How It Works
A class declares fields (data) and methods (behavior). When you instantiate it, the runtime allocates memory and links the new object to the class so it can find its methods.
⑤ Code Example
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
    def bark(self):
        return f"{self.name} says woof!"
class Dog {
  constructor(name) { this.name = name; }
  bark() { return `${this.name} says woof!`; }
}
class Dog {
    String name;
    Dog(String name) { this.name = name; }
    String bark() { return name + " says woof!"; }
}
⑥ Remember
A class is a recipe. You can't eat a recipe — you eat the cake (the instance).
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·02
Object
A concrete instance built from a class, holding state and behavior.
P·02oopinstance
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"An object is a self-contained bundle of state (attributes) and behavior (methods)."
It is a living value produced from a class, with its own data and an identity separate from every other object.
② Explanation

An object is a live, concrete thing produced from a class. While the class is the abstract description, the object occupies real memory and carries its own specific values for every attribute, making it the unit you actually pass around, store, and call methods on.

Objects are how OOP represents the nouns of a program. Two objects of the same class are independent: changing one does not affect the other, even though both follow the same blueprint. This identity-plus-shared-behavior model is what lets you reason about a system as a collection of cooperating things.

③ Where It Appears
A logged-in user represented as a User object
Each item in a shopping cart as a product object
A network connection held as a socket object
Game entities like players and enemies
④ How It Works
Each object stores its own attribute values. Methods are usually shared through the class. Two objects can have identical data and still be different objects in memory.
⑤ Code Example
rex = Dog("Rex")         # rex is an object
buddy = Dog("Buddy")
print(rex.bark())          # Rex says woof!
const rex = new Dog("Rex");
const buddy = new Dog("Buddy");
console.log(rex.bark());
Dog rex = new Dog("Rex");
Dog buddy = new Dog("Buddy");
System.out.println(rex.bark());
⑥ Remember
An object is data + behavior together. That bundle is what makes OOP different from procedural code.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·03
Instance
A specific object created from a class.
P·03oopinstance
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"An instance is a single, specific object created from a class."
The word emphasizes the link between the object and its blueprint. Every object is an instance of some class.
② Explanation

An instance is a single concrete realization of a class - the words "object" and "instance" describe the same thing from slightly different angles, with "instance" emphasizing the relationship to the class it came from. Instantiation is the moment the runtime carves out memory and initializes the object.

Thinking in instances helps you separate the one definition from its many uses. A single Button class can produce thousands of instances on a screen, each with its own label and position, all sharing the behavior declared once in the class.

③ Where It Appears
Creating many widgets from one component class
Spawning enemy instances in a game loop
Instantiating a model per database record
Building multiple HTTP request objects
④ How It Works
Instantiation calls the constructor, which sets up the object's initial state. You can ask the language whether a value is an instance of a class with isinstance() in Python or instanceof in JS/Java.
⑤ Code Example
d = Dog("Rex")
print(isinstance(d, Dog))  # True
const d = new Dog("Rex");
console.log(d instanceof Dog);  // true
Dog d = new Dog("Rex");
System.out.println(d instanceof Dog);  // true
⑥ Remember
"Instance" and "object" are mostly synonyms — instance just stresses which class it came from.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·04
Attribute
A piece of data stored on an object.
P·04oopstate
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"An attribute (or field, property) is a named value belonging to an object."
Attributes hold the object's state — its current data. Each instance can have its own distinct attribute values.
② Explanation

An attribute is a named piece of data attached to an object, representing part of its state. Attributes are what make two instances of the same class distinct: same structure, different values stored in each.

Attributes can be set in the constructor, mutated by methods, or exposed through controlled accessors. Good design keeps attributes consistent with the object's invariants, which is why encapsulation often guards them behind methods rather than exposing them raw.

③ Where It Appears
A user's name, email, and age fields
A rectangle's width and height
An order's total and status
A car's speed and fuel level
④ How It Works
In Python and JavaScript, attributes are usually set inside the constructor with self.x = ... or this.x = .... Java declares fields at the class level with explicit types.
⑤ Code Example
class Car:
    def __init__(self, color):
        self.color = color   # attribute
        self.speed = 0

c = Car("red")
print(c.color)              # red
class Car {
  constructor(color) {
    this.color = color;
    this.speed = 0;
  }
}
const c = new Car("red");
console.log(c.color);
class Car {
    String color;
    int speed;
    Car(String color) {
        this.color = color;
        this.speed = 0;
    }
}
⑥ Remember
Attributes describe what an object is; methods describe what it can do.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·05
Method
A function that belongs to a class and operates on an instance.
P·05oopbehavior
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A method is a function defined inside a class and intended to act on the object's data."
Methods are how an object exposes its behavior — they read and modify the object's attributes through self or this.
② Explanation

A method is a function that lives inside a class and operates on a particular instance, with privileged access to that object's attributes. Methods are the verbs of OOP - they define what an object can do and how it can change its own state.

Calling a method always happens in the context of an object, which is why methods receive an implicit reference to that object (this or self). This binding of behavior to data is the core idea that distinguishes methods from plain standalone functions.

③ Where It Appears
A BankAccount's deposit and withdraw operations
A String's toUpperCase or trim methods
A list's add, remove, and sort methods
An API client's get and post methods
④ How It Works
When you call obj.method(args), the language automatically passes obj as the implicit first argument (self/this). Methods can be instance methods, class methods, or static methods.
⑤ Code Example
class Counter:
    def __init__(self):
        self.n = 0
    def inc(self):
        self.n += 1
        return self.n
class Counter {
  constructor() { this.n = 0; }
  inc() { return ++this.n; }
}
class Counter {
    int n = 0;
    int inc() { return ++n; }
}
⑥ Remember
A method is a function with a body to act on — calling it is like sending a message to the object.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·06
Constructor
A special method that runs when a new object is created.
P·06oopinit
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A constructor is the method invoked automatically when an object is instantiated."
It initializes the object's attributes so the instance is ready to use immediately after creation.
② Explanation

A constructor is the special method that runs automatically the moment an object is created, giving you a single place to put the object into a valid initial state. It typically assigns the starting values of attributes and may validate the arguments passed in.

Because the constructor runs exactly once at birth, it is the natural guardian of an object's invariants - if construction succeeds, the rest of the code can trust the object is well-formed. Many languages name it specially (__init__ in Python, the class name in Java, constructor in JS).

③ Where It Appears
Setting a new user's name and default role
Opening a file or connection on creation
Validating required fields before an object exists
Injecting dependencies into a service object
④ How It Works
In Python it's __init__; in JavaScript and Java it's named constructor (JS) or matches the class name (Java). It typically takes parameters and stores them on self/this.
⑤ Code Example
class Point:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x, self.y = x, y

p = Point(3, 4)
class Point {
  constructor(x, y) {
    this.x = x;
    this.y = y;
  }
}
const p = new Point(3, 4);
class Point {
    int x, y;
    Point(int x, int y) {
        this.x = x;
        this.y = y;
    }
}
⑥ Remember
Keep constructors simple. Heavy work or I/O inside a constructor makes objects hard to test.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·07
Inheritance
A class extending another, reusing its fields and methods.
P·07oopextends
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Inheritance lets a class (subclass) reuse and extend the attributes and methods of another class (superclass)."
It models "is-a" relationships: a Dog is an Animal, so Dog inherits from Animal.
② Explanation

Inheritance lets one class build on another, automatically reusing the parent's attributes and methods while adding or overriding behavior. It models an "is-a" relationship: a Dog is an Animal, so Dog inherits what Animal already defines.

Inheritance is powerful but easy to overuse - deep hierarchies become rigid and hard to change. Modern practice often prefers composition (building objects from smaller parts) for flexibility, reserving inheritance for genuine, stable is-a relationships.

③ Where It Appears
A SavingsAccount extending a base Account
UI widgets sharing a common Component base
Custom exceptions extending a base Error type
Framework controllers inheriting routing logic
④ How It Works
The subclass automatically gets everything from the superclass. It can override methods, add new ones, and call the parent version using super(). Use sparingly — composition is often a better choice.
⑤ Code Example
class Animal:
    def speak(self): return "..."

class Dog(Animal):
    def speak(self): return "Woof!"

print(Dog().speak())     # Woof!
class Animal {
  speak() { return "..."; }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
  speak() { return "Woof!"; }
}
console.log(new Dog().speak());
class Animal {
    String speak() { return "..."; }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    String speak() { return "Woof!"; }
}
⑥ Remember
"Favor composition over inheritance." Deep inheritance chains are fragile — they turn small changes into big bugs.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·08
Polymorphism
Same interface, different implementations behind it.
P·08oopoverride
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Polymorphism lets one name (method) behave differently depending on the object's actual type."
You write code against a general interface, and the runtime picks the right implementation for the concrete object.
② Explanation

Polymorphism means a single interface can stand for many underlying implementations, so calling code can treat different types uniformly. When you call draw() on a shape, the correct version runs whether it is a Circle or a Square, without the caller knowing which.

This is what makes OOP code extensible: you can add a new subclass that honors the same interface and existing code keeps working unchanged. Polymorphism is the mechanism behind plugin systems, strategy patterns, and most flexible architectures.

③ Where It Appears
A render loop drawing many shape types
Payment processors sharing a charge() interface
Different file formats behind one save() call
Sorting any object that implements compareTo
④ How It Works
When you call animal.speak(), the runtime checks the object's actual class and invokes that class's speak(). This lets you treat many subclasses uniformly without conditionals.
⑤ Code Example
class Cat:
    def speak(self): return "Meow"
class Dog:
    def speak(self): return "Woof"

for a in [Cat(), Dog()]:
    print(a.speak())   # Meow, Woof
class Cat { speak() { return "Meow"; } }
class Dog { speak() { return "Woof"; } }

for (const a of [new Cat(), new Dog()])
  console.log(a.speak());
interface Animal { String speak(); }
class Cat implements Animal {
    public String speak() { return "Meow"; }
}
class Dog implements Animal {
    public String speak() { return "Woof"; }
}
⑥ Remember
Polymorphism replaces sprawling if/else chains with a single, uniform call to a shared method name.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·09
Encapsulation
Hiding internal state behind a controlled interface.
P·09oopprivacy
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Encapsulation is the practice of bundling data with the methods that operate on it, and restricting outside access."
The object exposes a public API while hiding its internal details so they can change without breaking callers.
② Explanation

Encapsulation is the practice of hiding an object's internal state behind a controlled public interface, so outside code interacts only through methods rather than poking at raw fields. This keeps invariants safe and lets you change the internals without breaking callers.

By exposing what an object does while concealing how it stores data, encapsulation reduces coupling across a system. Access modifiers (private, protected, public) and getters/setters are the tools that enforce these boundaries.

③ Where It Appears
Hiding a balance field behind deposit/withdraw
A class exposing read-only properties
Wrapping a raw array behind add/remove methods
Keeping database details inside a repository
④ How It Works
Java uses access modifiers (private, public). JavaScript uses #field for true privacy. Python relies on convention: a leading underscore means "internal — please don't touch."
⑤ Code Example
class Account:
    def __init__(self):
        self._balance = 0     # by convention private
    def deposit(self, x):
        self._balance += x
class Account {
  #balance = 0;          // truly private
  deposit(x) { this.#balance += x; }
  getBalance() { return this.#balance; }
}
class Account {
    private int balance = 0;
    public void deposit(int x) { balance += x; }
    public int getBalance() { return balance; }
}
⑥ Remember
The fewer things outside code knows about an object, the freer you are to refactor its insides later.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·10
Interface
A contract: a list of methods a class promises to implement.
P·10oopcontract
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"An interface is a contract that defines what methods a class must provide, without saying how."
It lets unrelated classes be used interchangeably as long as they implement the same set of operations.
② Explanation

An interface is a contract that lists method signatures a class promises to implement, without providing the bodies. It defines a capability ("can be compared", "can be drawn") that any number of unrelated classes can declare they support.

Interfaces decouple what from who: code can depend on the interface and accept any object that fulfills it, enabling polymorphism and easy testing through mocks. They are central to dependency inversion and clean architecture.

③ Where It Appears
A Comparable interface for sortable types
A Repository interface backed by SQL or memory
Plugin contracts in an extensible app
Mocking an interface in unit tests
④ How It Works
Java has dedicated interface declarations. Python uses Abstract Base Classes or Protocol classes. JavaScript relies on duck typing — if it has the right methods, it counts.
⑤ Code Example
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Shape(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def area(self): pass

class Square(Shape):
    def __init__(self, s): self.s = s
    def area(self): return self.s ** 2
// JS uses duck typing
class Square {
  constructor(s) { this.s = s; }
  area() { return this.s ** 2; }
}
// Anything with .area() can be a Shape
interface Shape {
    double area();
}
class Square implements Shape {
    double s;
    Square(double s) { this.s = s; }
    public double area() { return s * s; }
}
⑥ Remember
Code to interfaces, not implementations. Your code shouldn't care which shape it has — only that it has an area().
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·11
Abstraction
Focus on what something does, not how it does it.
P·11oopdesign
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Abstraction is the act of exposing only the essential parts of a system and hiding the rest."
You define what an object can do, but the messy internal details stay invisible to callers.
② Explanation

Abstraction is the discipline of exposing only the essential behavior of something while hiding the messy details of how it works. It lets you use a feature - sending an email, querying a database - by its purpose, without understanding its internal machinery.

Abstraction operates at every level: a method abstracts a block of logic, a class abstracts a concept, a module abstracts a subsystem. Good abstractions give you simple, stable surfaces to build on, which is what keeps large systems comprehensible.

③ Where It Appears
Calling sendEmail() without knowing SMTP
A driver abstracting different databases
High-level functions hiding algorithm details
An API hiding network and serialization
④ How It Works
A car's pedal is abstraction in action — you press, the car moves. You don't need to know about fuel injection, transmission, or pistons. Abstract classes and interfaces are how OOP enforces this discipline.
⑤ Code Example
class Database:
    def save(self, item):
        # caller doesn't care HOW we save
        self._write_to_disk(item)
    def _write_to_disk(self, item):
        pass   # messy details hidden
class Database {
  save(item) {           // the abstraction
    this.#writeToDisk(item);
  }
  #writeToDisk(item) { /* hidden */ }
}
abstract class Database {
    public void save(Object item) {
        writeToDisk(item);
    }
    protected abstract void writeToDisk(Object item);
}
⑥ Remember
Encapsulation hides data; abstraction hides complexity. They work together to keep callers calm.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › P·12
this / self
A reference to the current object inside its own methods.
P·12oopthis
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"this (or self in Python) is the keyword referring to the current instance inside a method."
It's how a method reads and writes the attributes of the very object it was called on.
② Explanation

this (or self in Python) is the implicit reference every method receives that points to the specific object the method was called on. It is how a method reaches its own instance's attributes and other methods.

Because the same method code runs for every instance, this is what disambiguates which object's data to read or modify in any given call. Misunderstanding how this is bound - especially in JavaScript callbacks - is a frequent source of bugs.

③ Where It Appears
Reading self.name inside a method
Returning this to chain method calls
Binding this correctly in event handlers
Distinguishing a parameter from a field
④ How It Works
In Python, self is an explicit first parameter. In JavaScript, this depends on how the function is called and is one of the language's trickiest features. In Java, this is the implicit receiver inside an instance method.
⑤ Code Example
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
    def hello(self):
        return f"I am {self.name}"
class Person {
  constructor(name) { this.name = name; }
  hello() { return `I am ${this.name}`; }
}
class Person {
    String name;
    Person(String name) { this.name = name; }
    String hello() { return "I am " + this.name; }
}
⑥ Remember
In JavaScript, regular functions rebind this. Arrow functions inherit it from the enclosing scope — pick wisely.
My Notes
Auto-saved
8
Block 8 · Errors & Debugging
When Things Break
Bugs are inevitable; the skill is finding them fast. Knowing how exceptions propagate, how to read a stack trace, and how to step with a debugger turns mysterious crashes into solvable puzzles.
ExceptionsTry/CatchStack TraceDebuggerEdge Cases
10 terms · E·01 → E·10 debug it
cherry code › E·01
Exception
An unexpected error that disrupts the normal flow of execution.
E·01errorsexception
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"An exception is an object representing an error that interrupts the normal execution of a program."
When an exception is raised, the runtime unwinds the call stack looking for a handler. If none is found, the program crashes.
② Explanation

An exception is a structured signal that something went wrong at runtime, interrupting the normal top-to-bottom flow of execution. Instead of returning a value, the code raises an exception object that propagates up the call stack until something handles it.

Exceptions separate the error-detection point from the error-handling point, letting low-level code report a problem and higher-level code decide what to do. They carry information - a type, a message, often a stack trace - that helps you diagnose the failure.

③ Where It Appears
A file-not-found error opening a missing path
Dividing by zero or parsing bad input
A network request timing out
Accessing a null reference or missing key
④ How It Works
Common exceptions include TypeError, ValueError, ZeroDivisionError, and NullPointerException. Each carries a message and a stack trace that points to where it occurred.
⑤ Code Example
x = 10 / 0
# ZeroDivisionError: division by zero
const obj = null;
console.log(obj.name);
// TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of null
int[] arr = new int[3];
int x = arr[5];
// ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
⑥ Remember
Exceptions aren't bugs — they're signals. Read the message first, then look at the line number.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·02
Try / Catch
Wrap risky code so errors don't crash the program.
E·02errorshandling
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"try/catch (or try/except) lets a program attempt risky code and recover gracefully if it fails."
The try block holds the risky code; the catch/except block runs only if an exception occurred.
② Explanation

Try/catch wraps a block of risky code so that if it raises an exception, control jumps to a handler instead of crashing the program. The try block holds the code that might fail; the catch (or except) block decides how to recover or report.

Good error handling is selective: catch only what you can meaningfully handle, and let everything else propagate. A finally block, when present, runs regardless of success or failure - ideal for releasing resources like files or connections.

③ Where It Appears
Reading a file that might not exist
Parsing user input that could be malformed
Calling an external API that may fail
Cleaning up resources in a finally block
④ How It Works
If an exception is raised inside the try block, execution jumps to the matching catch. The optional finally block runs whether or not an exception occurred — useful for cleanup like closing files.
⑤ Code Example
try:
    result = 10 / 0
except ZeroDivisionError as e:
    print(f"Caught: {e}")
finally:
    print("Always runs")
try {
  JSON.parse("not json");
} catch (e) {
  console.log("Caught:", e.message);
} finally {
  console.log("Always runs");
}
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Caught: " + e);
} finally {
    System.out.println("Always runs");
}
⑥ Remember
Never catch a generic exception just to ignore it. Silent failures are the worst kind of bugs.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·03
Throw / Raise
Deliberately signal an error in your own code.
E·03errorsthrow
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"throw (or raise in Python) lets your code create and propagate an exception on purpose."
You raise an exception when something invalid happens that the current function can't reasonably continue past.
② Explanation

Throwing (or raising) is the deliberate act of signaling an error from your own code when it detects a condition it cannot or should not handle locally. It creates an exception and hands control to whatever handler is waiting up the stack.

Raising your own exceptions makes failures explicit and self-documenting - far better than returning a misleading default or silently continuing with bad data. Choosing the right exception type and a clear message helps callers respond correctly.

③ Where It Appears
Rejecting invalid arguments in a function
Guarding against an impossible program state
Re-throwing after logging an error
Signaling a failed business rule
④ How It Works
You can throw a built-in exception or define your own by subclassing the base error class. The thrown object travels up the call stack until something catches it.
⑤ Code Example
def divide(a, b):
    if b == 0:
        raise ValueError("b must not be zero")
    return a / b
function divide(a, b) {
  if (b === 0) throw new Error("b must not be zero");
  return a / b;
}
static int divide(int a, int b) {
    if (b == 0)
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("b must not be zero");
    return a / b;
}
⑥ Remember
Raise early, raise specific. Don't return magic -1 values when an exception would say more.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·04
Stack Trace
The map of function calls that led to an error.
E·04errorstrace
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A stack trace is the list of function calls active at the moment an error happened."
It shows the chain that led from your program's entry point to the line that exploded.
② Explanation

A stack trace is the recorded chain of function calls that were active at the moment an error occurred, printed from the failure point back down to where execution began. It is the single most useful artifact for locating where and why something broke.

Reading a stack trace top-down tells you the immediate site of the error, while reading further down reveals the path that led there. Learning to scan past framework noise to find your own files is a core debugging skill.

③ Where It Appears
Pinpointing the line that threw an exception
Following an error through nested calls
Reading crash logs from production
Diagnosing an error reported by a user
④ How It Works
Each entry corresponds to a stack frame: a function, the file, and the line. Read it bottom-up in Python or top-down in JavaScript — the closest frame to your code is usually where the bug lives.
⑤ Code Example
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "app.py", line 10, in <module>
#     main()
#   File "app.py", line 7, in main
#     divide(10, 0)
#   File "app.py", line 3, in divide
#     return a / b
# ZeroDivisionError: division by zero
// Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of null
//     at parse (app.js:5:14)
//     at main (app.js:10:3)
//     at app.js:15:1
// Exception in thread "main" NullPointerException
//   at Parser.parse(Parser.java:14)
//   at Main.main(Main.java:7)
⑥ Remember
The trace tells you the path, not just the place. The bug is often a few frames up from the crash line.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·05
Breakpoint
A marker telling the debugger to pause at a specific line.
E·05errorsdebugger
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A breakpoint is a marker that tells the debugger to pause execution at a specific line."
While paused, you can inspect variables, step through the code line by line, and watch what changes.
② Explanation

A breakpoint is a marker you place on a line of code that tells the debugger to pause execution right before that line runs, freezing the program so you can inspect its live state. It turns a running program into something you can examine step by step.

While paused, you can read variable values, evaluate expressions, and walk the call stack as it actually is - far more reliable than guessing from print statements. Conditional breakpoints pause only when a condition holds, which is invaluable for catching rare cases.

③ Where It Appears
Pausing inside a loop to inspect each iteration
Stopping when a variable hits a bad value
Investigating why a function returns wrong output
Examining state before a crash
④ How It Works
Most IDEs let you click in the gutter to set a breakpoint. Languages also support code-level breakpoints like breakpoint() in Python and debugger; in JavaScript.
⑤ Code Example
def process(data):
    breakpoint()          # pauses here
    return data * 2
function process(data) {
  debugger;              // pauses here
  return data * 2;
}
// Breakpoints set in IDE gutter
static int process(int data) {
    return data * 2;     // click here in IDE
}
⑥ Remember
Set a breakpoint where the bug becomes visible, then walk backward through the trace.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·06
print / console.log
The simplest debugging tool — show what a variable holds.
E·06errorslogging
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Printing intermediate values is the simplest, oldest, and still most-used debugging technique."
You sprinkle print or console.log calls to expose values at runtime — sometimes called "printf debugging."
② Explanation

Print debugging is the simplest possible diagnostic technique: insert statements that output a variable's value or a marker at key points, then read the output to infer what the program actually did. It needs no tools and works everywhere.

Despite its crudeness it remains widely used because it is fast, requires no setup, and shows the real sequence of events. The downside is clutter and the need to remove the statements afterward - which is why structured logging often replaces ad-hoc prints in serious code.

③ Where It Appears
Checking a value mid-function quickly
Confirming whether a code branch executed
Tracing the order of asynchronous events
Debugging where a real debugger is unavailable
④ How It Works
It works because it's instant and requires no tools. The downside: prints clutter your code and need to be removed. Prefer a real logger or debugger for serious problems.
⑤ Code Example
total = 0
for i in range(5):
    total += i
    print(f"i={i}, total={total}")
let total = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  total += i;
  console.log(`i=${i}, total=${total}`);
}
int total = 0;
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    total += i;
    System.out.println("i=" + i + " total=" + total);
}
⑥ Remember
Always log labels, not just values. print(x) tells you nothing; print("x=", x) tells the story.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·07
Linter
A tool that catches errors and style issues before you run the code.
E·07errorstools
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A linter is a static analysis tool that scans your code for errors, smells, and style violations without running it."
It catches typos, unused variables, undefined names, and inconsistent formatting before they reach production.
② Explanation

A linter is a static-analysis tool that scans your source code without running it, flagging likely bugs, suspicious patterns, and style inconsistencies. It catches a whole class of mistakes - unused variables, undeclared names, risky comparisons - before they ever reach execution.

Beyond catching errors, linters enforce a consistent style across a team, removing arguments about formatting and making code easier to read. Integrated into editors and CI pipelines, they shorten the feedback loop dramatically.

③ Where It Appears
Catching an undefined variable as you type
Enforcing a team's style guide automatically
Blocking bad code in a CI pipeline
Flagging unreachable or dead code
④ How It Works
The linter parses your source code and compares it against rules. Modern editors run linters live, underlining issues with red or yellow squiggles as you type.
⑤ Code Example
# flake8 / ruff would flag this:
import os         # F401 unused import
x = 10;             # E702 semicolon
def foo( ):
    pass
// ESLint would flag this:
let x;                  // no-unused-vars
if (y == 1) {            // eqeqeq
  console.log("hi")    // missing semicolon
}
// Checkstyle / SpotBugs flag this:
class badName {        // should be CamelCase
    int X = 5;          // non-conventional
}
⑥ Remember
Treat lint warnings as cheap bug reports. Fixing them now is faster than debugging later.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·08
Debugger
A tool to step through code one line at a time.
E·08errorsdebugger
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A debugger is an interactive tool that lets you pause, inspect, and step through running code."
You set breakpoints, run the program, and then move forward one line at a time, watching state evolve.
② Explanation

A debugger is a tool that lets you control a running program's execution - pausing it, stepping through it one line at a time, and inspecting every variable along the way. It replaces guesswork with direct observation of what the code is really doing.

With a debugger you can step into functions, step over them, watch expressions update, and modify state mid-run. Mastering it transforms debugging from sprinkling print statements into a precise, interactive investigation.

③ Where It Appears
Stepping line-by-line through a tricky function
Watching variables change in real time
Inspecting the call stack at a crash
Setting conditional breakpoints for rare bugs
④ How It Works
Common operations: step over (next line, skip into calls), step into (descend into a function), step out (finish the current function), continue (run until next breakpoint).
⑤ Code Example
# Run with: python -m pdb script.py
import pdb
pdb.set_trace()        # pre-3.7 way
# Commands: n (next), s (step), c (continue), p var
// Chrome DevTools / VS Code debugger
function buggy(arr) {
  debugger;
  return arr.map(x => x * 2);
}
// IntelliJ/Eclipse: click in gutter to set breakpoint,
// then Run -> Debug
static int buggy(int[] arr) {
    return arr[0] + arr[1];   // inspect arr here
}
⑥ Remember
Learning to use your debugger saves hours over print-based detective work.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·09
Edge Case
An unusual input that breaks "normal" code.
E·09errorstesting
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"An edge case is a rare or extreme input that exposes hidden bugs in code that handles the common cases fine."
Examples: empty input, the maximum possible value, negative numbers, null, unicode, or very long strings.
② Explanation

An edge case is an input or situation at the boundary of what your code expects - empty lists, zero, negative numbers, the maximum value, simultaneous events - that "normal" logic often forgets to handle. These corners are where most bugs hide.

Robust software is largely a matter of identifying and handling edge cases deliberately rather than discovering them in production. Thinking about boundaries up front, and writing tests for them, is what separates fragile code from reliable code.

③ Where It Appears
An empty input where one item was assumed
Off-by-one errors at the start or end of a range
Division by zero or null values
Concurrent requests hitting the same record
④ How It Works
Always ask: what happens with an empty list? With one element? With a million? With unicode? With a negative number? Defensive coders test the edges first, since that's where most bugs hide.
⑤ Code Example
def average(nums):
    return sum(nums) / len(nums)

average([])   # ZeroDivisionError! edge case
function first(arr) {
  return arr[0].toUpperCase();
}
first([]);        // TypeError
first([null]);    // TypeError
static int max(int[] arr) {
    int m = arr[0];   // crashes on empty
    for (int x : arr) if (x > m) m = x;
    return m;
}
⑥ Remember
The empty list. The number zero. The single character. Test the boundary first.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › E·10
Syntax vs Runtime Error
Errors caught before vs during execution.
E·10errorstypes
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A syntax error is bad grammar — the code can't even be parsed. A runtime error happens later, while the program is running."
Syntax errors stop your code before it starts; runtime errors crash it mid-flight.
② Explanation

Errors split into two broad families by when they surface. Syntax errors are caught before the program runs - the code is grammatically invalid and the parser or compiler rejects it. Runtime errors slip past that check and only blow up while the program is actually executing.

Syntax errors are usually quick to fix because the tool points right at them; runtime errors are trickier because they depend on actual data and program state, so they may only appear under specific conditions. Understanding which kind you are facing guides where to look.

③ Where It Appears
A missing bracket caught before running
A null reference that crashes mid-execution
A typo in a keyword flagged by the compiler
A bad API response triggering a runtime fault
④ How It Works
A missing colon or unmatched bracket is a syntax error. Dividing by zero, indexing past the end of an array, or calling a method on null are runtime errors. There's also a third kind — logical errors — that don't crash but produce wrong results.
⑤ Code Example
# Syntax error — missing colon
if x > 0
    print(x)

# Runtime error
int("not a number")   # ValueError
// Syntax error — unclosed string
const name = "Ada;

// Runtime error
null.trim();   // TypeError
// Compile error — missing semicolon
int x = 5

// Runtime error
String s = null;
s.length();        // NullPointerException
⑥ Remember
Syntax errors are easy — the parser tells you the line. Logical errors are the worst — nothing screams; the answer is just wrong.
My Notes
Auto-saved
9
Block 9 · Best Practices
Code that Lasts
Working code is only half the job. The other half is code that other people — including future you — can read, change, and trust. Conventions, testing, and review turn a script into a system.
NamingDRYRefactorTestsDocs
10 terms · B·01 → B·10 craft it
cherry code › B·01
Naming Conventions
Choose names that explain themselves at a glance.
B·01stylenaming
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Naming conventions are agreed-upon rules for how to name variables, functions, classes, and files."
Good names act as inline documentation — they describe intent, not implementation.
② Explanation

Good naming is choosing identifiers for variables, functions, and types that reveal their intent at a glance, so a reader understands the code without tracing through it. A name like daysUntilExpiry communicates instantly where d or temp forces a guess.

Names are the most-read part of code - you write them once and read them hundreds of times. Investing in clear, consistent, pronounceable names is one of the highest-leverage habits for keeping a codebase understandable as it grows.

③ Where It Appears
Naming a function calculateTax not doCalc
Avoiding single letters outside tiny loops
Using consistent conventions across a team
Renaming a variable when its purpose shifts
④ How It Works
Python uses snake_case for variables/functions and PascalCase for classes. JavaScript and Java prefer camelCase for variables. Constants are usually UPPER_CASE. Pick the style of your language and stick to it.
⑤ Code Example
# Bad
d = 86400
def do(x): return x * d

# Good
SECONDS_IN_DAY = 86400
def days_to_seconds(days):
    return days * SECONDS_IN_DAY
// Bad
const d = 86400;

// Good
const SECONDS_IN_DAY = 86400;
function daysToSeconds(days) {
  return days * SECONDS_IN_DAY;
}
static final int SECONDS_IN_DAY = 86400;
static int daysToSeconds(int days) {
    return days * SECONDS_IN_DAY;
}
⑥ Remember
If your reader needs to scroll to find out what a name means, the name is wrong.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·02
Indentation
The visual structure that shows how code is nested.
B·02styleformat
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Indentation is the leading whitespace that visually shows the nesting level of code blocks."
In Python, indentation is part of the grammar — wrong indentation is an error. In other languages, it's just convention but still essential for readability.
② Explanation

Indentation is the consistent use of leading whitespace to show how code blocks nest inside one another, making the program's structure visible at a glance. Each level of nesting - a loop, a condition, a function body - shifts further right.

In most languages indentation is a readability convention, but in some (notably Python) it is part of the syntax and changes meaning. Either way, consistent indentation is essential: misaligned blocks hide bugs and make code far harder to follow.

③ Where It Appears
Showing which lines belong to a loop body
Making nested conditionals readable
Python blocks defined purely by indentation
Auto-formatting on save to stay consistent
④ How It Works
Standard is 4 spaces in Python, 2 spaces in JavaScript, 4 spaces in Java. Mixing tabs and spaces causes invisible bugs. Use your editor's auto-format command — most projects ship with a formatter config.
⑤ Code Example
def classify(n):
    if n > 0:
        return "positive"
    elif n < 0:
        return "negative"
    return "zero"
function classify(n) {
  if (n > 0) return "positive";
  if (n < 0) return "negative";
  return "zero";
}
static String classify(int n) {
    if (n > 0) return "positive";
    if (n < 0) return "negative";
    return "zero";
}
⑥ Remember
Let a formatter handle indentation. Don't argue about tabs vs spaces — run black, prettier, or google-java-format and move on.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·03
Comments
Notes in source code — explain WHY, not WHAT.
B·03styledocs
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A comment is text inside the source file that is ignored by the compiler/interpreter, used to explain intent to humans."
The best comments explain WHY a piece of code exists — not WHAT it does, because the code itself already says that.
② Explanation

Comments are human-readable notes embedded in source code that the compiler ignores. The best comments explain WHY a piece of code exists or why a non-obvious choice was made - context the code itself cannot convey - rather than restating WHAT the code plainly does.

Comments that merely echo the code add noise and rot quickly as the code changes. Aim for self-explanatory code first, then reserve comments for intent, caveats, edge cases, and links to the reasoning behind a decision.

③ Where It Appears
Explaining why a workaround was necessary
Documenting a tricky algorithm's reasoning
Marking a known limitation or TODO
Linking to a ticket or external spec
④ How It Works
Python uses #. JavaScript and Java use // for single-line and /* */ for multi-line. Avoid restating what the code says — instead, document the reason behind tricky choices.
⑤ Code Example
# Bad: restates the code
i += 1   # add 1 to i

# Good: explains why
# Skip the header row because the spreadsheet
# exports column titles as data.
for row in rows[1:]:
    process(row)
// Bad
i++;  // increment i

// Good: explains the surprise
// Use setTimeout(..., 0) to defer until after
// the current synchronous block finishes.
setTimeout(refresh, 0);
/* TODO: this brute-force search is fine for n < 1000.
 * Switch to a hash map if dataset grows past that. */
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
    check(arr[i]);
}
⑥ Remember
Stale comments are worse than no comments. If you change the code, update the comment in the same commit.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·04
DRY Principle
Don't Repeat Yourself — every piece of knowledge lives in one place.
B·04styleDRY
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself — means every piece of knowledge in a system should have a single, authoritative source."
Duplicated logic is duplicated maintenance — fix a bug in one copy and the others still bite you.
② Explanation

DRY - Don't Repeat Yourself - is the principle that every piece of knowledge or logic should have a single, authoritative home in the codebase. When the same idea is duplicated, a change must be made in every copy, and the copies inevitably drift out of sync.

Applying DRY means extracting repeated logic into a function, constant, or module so there is one place to fix and update. But it must be balanced - forcing unrelated code together just because it looks similar (premature abstraction) can be worse than a little duplication.

③ Where It Appears
Extracting a repeated calculation into a function
Defining a constant for a value used everywhere
Sharing validation logic across endpoints
Centralizing a config value in one place
④ How It Works
When you find yourself copy-pasting code, extract it into a function, class, or constant. But avoid over-DRYing: if two pieces of code happen to look alike but represent different ideas, leave them alone.
⑤ Code Example
# Bad — repetition
print("Hi Ada!")
print("Hi Bob!")
print("Hi Eve!")

# Good — DRY
def greet(name):
    print(f"Hi {name}!")
for n in ["Ada", "Bob", "Eve"]:
    greet(n)
const greet = name => console.log(`Hi ${name}!`);
["Ada", "Bob", "Eve"].forEach(greet);
static void greet(String name) {
    System.out.println("Hi " + name + "!");
}
for (String n : List.of("Ada", "Bob", "Eve"))
    greet(n);
⑥ Remember
Wait for the third repetition before extracting. Two looks like a coincidence — three is a pattern.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·05
SOLID Principles
Five OOP design principles for maintainable code.
B·05styledesign
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"SOLID is an acronym for five OOP design principles that produce flexible, maintainable systems."
Single responsibility, Open-closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion.
② Explanation

SOLID is a set of five object-oriented design principles - Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion - that together steer code toward being flexible, testable, and easy to change.

Each principle targets a common source of rigidity: classes that do too much, code that must be edited to extend it, subclasses that break their parent's contract, fat interfaces, and tight coupling to concrete dependencies. Applied with judgment, they keep large systems maintainable.

③ Where It Appears
Splitting a class that has too many jobs
Extending behavior without editing existing code
Depending on interfaces instead of concrete classes
Designing small, focused interfaces
④ How It Works
S: a class has one reason to change. O: open to extension, closed to modification. L: subtypes must be usable as their base. I: small focused interfaces over big ones. D: depend on abstractions, not concretes.
⑤ Code Example
# S — one responsibility per class
class Invoice:
    def total(self): pass

class InvoicePrinter:
    def print(self, inv): pass

# Two reasons to change → two classes
// D — depend on abstractions
class Notifier {
  constructor(sender) { this.sender = sender; }
  send(msg) { this.sender.send(msg); }
}
// sender can be Email, SMS, Slack...
// I — small focused interfaces
interface Readable  { String read(); }
interface Writable  { void  write(String s); }
// Don't force one big interface on everyone
⑥ Remember
SOLID is a compass, not a checklist. Reach for it when a system feels rigid or fragile.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·06
Refactor
Improve the code's structure without changing what it does.
B·06stylerefactor
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Refactoring is changing the internal structure of code while keeping its observable behavior the same."
You rename, extract, simplify, and reorganize — without adding features or fixing bugs in the same step.
② Explanation

Refactoring is the disciplined process of improving the internal structure of code - its clarity, organization, and design - without changing its external behavior. The program does exactly what it did before; it is just easier to understand and extend afterward.

Because behavior must stay identical, refactoring leans heavily on a test suite to prove nothing broke. Done continuously in small steps, it keeps a codebase healthy; postponed indefinitely, it lets technical debt accumulate until change becomes painful.

③ Where It Appears
Renaming variables for clarity
Extracting a long function into smaller ones
Removing duplication after a deadline rush
Restructuring code to make a new feature easy
④ How It Works
Refactoring needs a safety net — usually unit tests — so you can confirm behavior didn't change. Common moves: extract function, rename variable, inline a method, replace a magic number with a constant.
⑤ Code Example
# Before
def price(items):
    t = 0
    for i in items: t += i["p"] * i["q"]
    return t * 1.1

# After — extracted intent
TAX = 1.1
def subtotal(items):
    return sum(i["p"] * i["q"] for i in items)
def price(items):
    return subtotal(items) * TAX
// Before
function f(x) { return x.a * 0.07; }

// After
const TAX_RATE = 0.07;
function tax(order) {
  return order.amount * TAX_RATE;
}
// Before
if (u.role.equals("admin") && u.active) { ... }

// After — extracted clarity
if (isActiveAdmin(u)) { ... }
boolean isActiveAdmin(User u) {
    return u.active && "admin".equals(u.role);
}
⑥ Remember
Refactor and fix bugs in separate commits. Mixing them makes review and reverting much harder.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·07
Code Review
Peers reading each other's code before it merges.
B·07stylereview
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Code review is the practice of having other developers read your code and suggest improvements before it's merged."
It catches bugs, spreads knowledge across the team, and keeps style consistent.
② Explanation
