Code review is the practice of having other developers read a change before it merges into the shared codebase. It catches bugs, spreads knowledge, enforces standards, and gives the author feedback while the change is still fresh and easy to adjust.

Beyond finding defects, review is a cultural tool: it keeps more than one person familiar with each part of the system and surfaces design discussions early. Effective reviews are timely, focused, and kind - critiquing the code, not the coder.

③ Where It Appears
Approving a pull request before merge
Catching a security flaw a peer missed
Onboarding a new developer through feedback
Discussing a design choice in review comments
④ How It Works
You open a pull request, your teammates leave comments, you address them, and eventually they approve and merge. Good reviewers focus on correctness, clarity, and risk — not personal style.
⑤ Code Example
# A reviewer might suggest:

# Before
def check(u):
    if u.age >= 18: return True
    return False

# Suggested
def is_adult(user):
    return user.age >= 18
// Reviewer: "Use === instead of ==,
// and consider early return."
function isAdmin(user) {
  if (!user) return false;
  return user.role === "admin";
}
// Reviewer: "Use Optional<String> instead
// of returning null."
Optional<String> findName(int id) {
    return Optional.ofNullable(lookup(id));
}
⑥ Remember
Review the code, not the person. "This function" is feedback. "You always" is an argument.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·08
Version Control
A system that tracks every change to your code over time.
B·08stylegit
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Version control records the history of changes to your codebase so you can review, revert, branch, and collaborate safely."
Git is by far the most common system, often used through hosts like GitHub or GitLab.
② Explanation

Version control is a system that records every change made to a codebase over time, letting you see history, compare versions, revert mistakes, and work on parallel lines of development through branches. Git is the dominant tool today.

It is the backbone of modern collaboration: many people can work on the same project simultaneously, then merge their work with conflicts surfaced and resolved deliberately. Each commit is a labeled snapshot you can always return to, making experimentation safe.

③ Where It Appears
Reverting to a working version after a bad change
Branching to develop a feature in isolation
Merging multiple contributors' work
Reviewing the history of who changed what
④ How It Works
You make changes, stage them, and create a commit — a snapshot with a message. You can branch to work on features in isolation, then merge them back. The remote (origin) keeps the team in sync.
⑤ Code Example
# Same git commands work for any language
$ git init
$ git add app.py
$ git commit -m "feat: add login flow"
$ git checkout -b feature/profile
$ git push origin feature/profile
$ git status
$ git diff
$ git add src/
$ git commit -m "fix: handle null response"
$ git pull --rebase
$ git push
$ git log --oneline
$ git branch
$ git merge main
$ git revert <sha>
⑥ Remember
Commit messages are letters to your future self. "fix stuff" is a betrayal of the next debugger.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·09
Testing
Automated checks that prove your code does what you say.
B·09styletests
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"A test is code that runs your code with known inputs and verifies the outputs match expectations."
Tests are a safety net: they let you change things confidently because a failure surfaces immediately.
② Explanation

Testing is writing automated checks that exercise your code and assert it produces the expected results, so correctness is verified by machine rather than by hope. Tests range from tiny unit tests of single functions to broad end-to-end tests of whole workflows.

A good test suite is a safety net: it lets you refactor and add features confidently because any regression is caught immediately. Tests also document intended behavior and force you to design code that is easy to call in isolation.

③ Where It Appears
Unit tests verifying a function's output
Catching a regression before it ships
Enabling fearless refactoring
Documenting expected behavior as runnable specs
④ How It Works
Tests assert that a condition is true. Unit tests focus on a single function; integration tests check several pieces together. Test runners discover and run them: pytest, Jest, JUnit.
⑤ Code Example
# pytest example
def add(a, b): return a + b

def test_add():
    assert add(2, 3) == 5
    assert add(-1, 1) == 0
// Jest example
function add(a, b) { return a + b; }

test("adds numbers", () => {
  expect(add(2, 3)).toBe(5);
  expect(add(-1, 1)).toBe(0);
});
// JUnit example
@Test
void addsNumbers() {
    assertEquals(5, add(2, 3));
    assertEquals(0, add(-1, 1));
}
⑥ Remember
The first time tests catch a regression, you'll never write code without them again.
My Notes
Auto-saved
cherry code › B·10
Documentation
Writing meant for the next person who reads the code.
B·10styledocs
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
① Definition
"Documentation is everything written to help others (and future you) understand and use the code: READMEs, docstrings, API references, and architecture notes."
Good docs make the difference between a project people adopt and one they abandon.
② Explanation

Documentation is writing aimed at the next person who has to understand or use the code - which is often your future self. It spans README files, API references, inline doc comments, and architecture notes that explain how the pieces fit together.

Code shows how something works; documentation explains how to use it and why it is designed that way. Kept close to the code and updated alongside it, good docs dramatically lower the cost of onboarding, integrating, and maintaining a system.

③ Where It Appears
A README explaining how to run the project
API reference for a public library
Architecture notes for a complex subsystem
Doc comments generating reference pages
④ How It Works
Python uses triple-quoted docstrings. JavaScript uses JSDoc comments. Java uses Javadoc with /** */. Tools generate browsable HTML docs from these structured comments.
⑤ Code Example
def discount(price, percent):
    """Return the price after applying a percent discount.

    Args:
        price: positive number, the original price.
        percent: 0-100, the discount to apply.

    Returns:
        The discounted price.
    """
    return price * (1 - percent / 100)
/**
 * Apply a percent discount to a price.
 * @param {number} price  positive original price
 * @param {number} percent 0..100 discount
 * @returns {number} discounted price
 */
function discount(price, percent) {
  return price * (1 - percent / 100);
}
/**
 * Apply a percent discount to a price.
 *
 * @param price   positive original price
 * @param percent 0..100 discount
 * @return discounted price
 */
static double discount(double price, double percent) {
    return price * (1 - percent / 100);
}
⑥ Remember
The README is the front door. Write it for someone who's never seen the project — including you in six months.
My Notes
Auto-saved

Block 1 · Fundamentals
Concepts, tools, and meta-ideas that underlie every language.
Block 1 · Fundamentals
├─ Core Concepts
│ ├─ Programming — writing instructions
│ ├─ Code — the text itself
│ ├─ Bug — unintended behavior
│ └─ Debug — find & fix
├─ Tooling
│ ├─ IDE — editor + tools
│ ├─ Terminal — text command line
│ └─ Compiler vs Interpreter — translate vs execute
└─ Meta
├─ Syntax — language rules
├─ Runtime — while running
└─ Paradigm — style of programming
cherry code › mind map › block 2
Block 2 · Types & Variables
Data, kinds of data, and the names that hold them.
Block 2 · Types & Variables
├─ Primitives
│ ├─ String — text
│ ├─ Integer — whole numbers
│ ├─ Float — decimals
│ ├─ Boolean — true / false
│ └─ Null / None — absence of value
├─ Containers
│ ├─ Array / List — ordered sequence
│ └─ Dict / Object — key→value pairs
└─ Concepts
├─ Variable — a named box
├─ Data Type — kind of value
├─ Constant — never changes
├─ Scope — where it lives
└─ Type Casting — convert types
cherry code › mind map › block 3
Block 3 · Operators
The verbs that act on values.
Block 3 · Operators
├─ Math
│ ├─ Arithmetic — + - * /
│ ├─ Modulo — remainder
│ └─ Increment / Decrement — ++ / --
├─ Logic
│ ├─ Comparison — == < >
│ └─ Logical — and / or / not
└─ Other
├─ Assignment — =
├─ String Concatenation — joining text
├─ Precedence — order of evaluation
├─ Expression vs Statement — value vs action
└─ Side Effects — changes beyond return
cherry code › mind map › block 4
Block 4 · Control Flow
How execution branches, loops, and skips.
Block 4 · Control Flow
├─ Branching
│ ├─ If — conditional run
│ ├─ Else / Elif — alternatives
│ ├─ Switch / Case — multi-branch
│ └─ Ternary — inline if-else
├─ Loops
│ ├─ While — repeat while true
│ ├─ For — counted loop
│ ├─ For-Each — iterate collection
│ └─ Nested Loops — loops in loops
└─ Control
├─ Break — exit loop
└─ Continue — skip iteration

Reusable named behavior.
Block 5 · Functions
├─ Basics
│ ├─ Function — reusable behavior
│ ├─ Parameter — input slot
│ ├─ Argument — value passed
│ └─ Return Value — result
├─ Concepts
│ ├─ Pure Function — no side effects
│ ├─ Recursion — calls itself
│ ├─ Closure — captures scope
│ ├─ Function Scope — local variables
│ └─ Higher-Order — function-of-functions
└─ Special
├─ Lambda / Arrow — anonymous fn
├─ Default Arguments — optional values
└─ *args / **kwargs — variadic params

Shapes for storing and finding data.
Block 6 · Data Structures
├─ Linear
│ ├─ Array — fixed contiguous
│ ├─ List — dynamic sequence
│ ├─ Tuple — immutable sequence
│ ├─ Stack — LIFO
│ ├─ Queue — FIFO
│ └─ Linked List — node chain
├─ Map-based
│ ├─ Dictionary / Map — key→value
│ ├─ Set — unique elements
│ └─ Hash Table — fast lookup
├─ Hierarchical
│ ├─ Tree — parent & children
│ └─ Graph — nodes & edges
└─ Analysis
└─ Big O — cost of operations

Modeling the world as objects.
Block 7 · OOP
├─ Building Blocks
│ ├─ Class — blueprint

│ ├─ Instance — one created object
│ ├─ Attribute — stored data
│ └─ Method — function on object
├─ Lifecycle
│ ├─ Constructor — setup at birth
│ └─ this / self — current instance
└─ Principles
├─ Inheritance — extend a class
├─ Polymorphism — one name, many forms
├─ Encapsulation — hide internals
├─ Abstraction — show the what

cherry code › cheatsheets › python
Python Cheatsheet
The most-used Python snippets, grouped by topic.
String methods
upper() / lower()
Convert string case.
"hi".upper()  # 'HI'
"HI".lower()  # 'hi'
split() / join()
Split into list, join list with separator.
"a,b,c".split(",")  # ['a','b','c']
"-".join(["a","b"])  # 'a-b'
replace()
Replace all occurrences.
"hello".replace("l", "L")  # 'heLLo'
strip()
Remove leading/trailing whitespace.
"  hi  ".strip()  # 'hi'
find() / count()
Find index (-1 if missing) / count occurrences.
"hello".find("l")   # 2
"hello".count("l")  # 2
f-strings / format()
Template strings with variables.
name = "Ana"
print(f"Hi {name}")
"Hi {}".format(name)
startswith() / endswith()
Prefix/suffix check returning bool.
"file.py".endswith(".py")  # True
"hello".startswith("he") # True
List methods
append() / extend()
Add single / multiple items.
a = [1,2]
a.append(3)      # [1,2,3]
a.extend([4,5])  # [1,2,3,4,5]
pop() / remove()
Remove by index (returns value) / by value.
a = [1,2,3]
a.pop()        # 3, a=[1,2]
a.remove(1)   # a=[2]
insert()
Insert at index.
a = [1,3]
a.insert(1, 2)  # [1,2,3]
sort() / reverse()
In-place sort / reverse.
a = [3,1,2]
a.sort()      # [1,2,3]
a.reverse()   # [3,2,1]
index() / count()
Find first index / total count.
[1,2,2].index(2)  # 1
[1,2,2].count(2)  # 2
List comprehension
Build a list inline from any iterable.
[x*x for x in range(5)]
# [0,1,4,9,16]
[x for x in data if x > 0]
Dictionary methods
keys() / values() / items()
Iterate keys, values, or both.
d = {"a":1, "b":2}
for k, v in d.items():
    print(k, v)
get()
Safe lookup with default.
d.get("x", 0)  # 0 if 'x' missing
update() / pop()
Merge / remove by key.
d.update({"c":3})
d.pop("a")
Dict comprehension
Build a dict inline.
{x: x*x for x in range(4)}
# {0:0, 1:1, 2:4, 3:9}
File I/O patterns
Read whole file
Open with auto-close.
with open("f.txt") as f:
    text = f.read()
Read lines
Iterate line by line (memory-friendly).
with open("f.txt") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.rstrip())
Write
Open in write mode (overwrites).
with open("out.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("hello\n")
JSON
Read/write JSON.
import json
data = json.load(open("a.json"))
json.dump(data, open("b.json","w"))
Exception handling
try / except / else / finally
Full structure.
try:
    x = 1/0
except ZeroDivisionError as e:
    print(e)
else:
    print("no error")
finally:
    print("cleanup")
raise
Signal an error.
if n < 0:
    raise ValueError("negative")
Custom exception
Subclass Exception.
class MyError(Exception): pass
raise MyError("oops")
Useful built-ins
len / range
Length and number sequences.
len([1,2,3])         # 3
list(range(3))         # [0,1,2]
enumerate / zip
Index+value / pair sequences.
for i, v in enumerate(items): ...
for a, b in zip(xs, ys): ...
map / filter
Apply a function / keep matches.
list(map(lambda x: x*2, xs))
list(filter(lambda x: x>0, xs))
sorted / reversed
Return new sorted/reversed sequence.
sorted([3,1,2])      # [1,2,3]
list(reversed([1,2,3]))
sum / any / all
Aggregate values / boolean checks.
sum([1,2,3])    # 6
any([False,True])  # True
all([True,True])   # True
cherry code › cheatsheets › javascript
JavaScript Cheatsheet
The most-used JavaScript snippets, grouped by topic.
Array methods
map / filter / reduce
Transform, keep matches, fold to a value.
[1,2,3].map(x => x*2)        // [2,4,6]
[1,2,3].filter(x => x>1)     // [2,3]
[1,2,3].reduce((a,b)=>a+b)  // 6
find / forEach
First match / side-effect iteration.
arr.find(x => x.id===3)
arr.forEach(x => console.log(x))
some / every
Any match / all match.
[1,2].some(x => x>1)   // true
[1,2].every(x => x>0)  // true
slice / splice
Non-destructive slice / destructive splice.
[1,2,3,4].slice(1,3)   // [2,3]
arr.splice(1, 1)         // removes 1 at idx 1
concat / flat
Combine / flatten nested arrays.
[1].concat([2,3])      // [1,2,3]
[[1],[2,[3]]].flat(2)   // [1,2,3]
Object operations
Spread / rest
Expand / collect properties.
const a = {x:1};
const b = {...a, y:2};  // {x:1,y:2}
const {x, ...rest} = b;
Object.keys/values/entries
Iterate keys, values, or pairs.
Object.keys(obj)
Object.values(obj)
Object.entries(obj).forEach(([k,v])=>{})
Destructuring
Unpack values into variables.
const {name, age} = user;
const [first, ...others] = arr;
String methods
split / join
String to array, array to string.
"a,b,c".split(",")  // ['a','b','c']
["a","b"].join("-")  // 'a-b'
replace / replaceAll
First / all occurrences.
"a-b-c".replace("-",".")     // 'a.b-c'
"a-b-c".replaceAll("-",".")  // 'a.b.c'
Template literals
Backticks for interpolation.
const name = "Ana";
console.log(`Hi ${name}`);
trim / padStart
Strip spaces / pad to width.
"  hi ".trim()              // 'hi'
"7".padStart(3,"0")        // '007'
Async
Promise
Represents a future value.
new Promise((res, rej) => {
  setTimeout(() => res(42), 100);
});
async / await
Sequential async code.
async function load(){
  const r = await fetch(url);
  return r.json();
}
fetch + then/catch
Chain-based async.
fetch(url)
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data))
  .catch(e => console.error(e));
Promise.all
Wait for all in parallel.
const [a, b] = await Promise.all([
  fetch("/a"), fetch("/b")
]);
DOM
querySelector
Find one element by CSS selector.
const el = document.querySelector(".btn");
document.querySelectorAll("li");
addEventListener
Hook an event handler.
btn.addEventListener("click", e => {
  console.log("clicked");
});
classList
Add/remove/toggle CSS classes.
el.classList.add("on");
el.classList.remove("on");
el.classList.toggle("on");
dataset
Read data-* attributes.
// <div data-id="42">
el.dataset.id  // "42"
Common patterns
Ternary
Inline if/else expression.
const msg = age >= 18 ? "adult" : "minor";
Optional chaining ?.
Safe property access; returns undefined if null.
user?.address?.city
Nullish ??
Default only if null/undefined (not falsy).
const n = value ?? 0;
Default args
Function parameter defaults.
function greet(name = "world"){
  return `Hi ${name}`;
}
cherry code › cheatsheets › java
Java Cheatsheet
The most-used Java snippets, grouped by topic.
String methods
length() / charAt()
Length and character at index.
String s = "hello";
s.length();      // 5
s.charAt(1);    // 'e'
substring
Slice from index (end exclusive).
s.substring(1, 4);  // "ell"
indexOf / split
Find index / split by separator.
s.indexOf("l");     // 2
"a,b,c".split(",");  // {"a","b","c"}
toUpperCase / equals
Compare strings with equals, NOT ==.
s.toUpperCase();
if (a.equals(b)) { ... }
String.format
printf-style formatting.
String.format("x=%d, y=%s", 5, "hi");
ArrayList methods
add / get / set
Add, read by index, replace by index.
List<Integer> a = new ArrayList<>();
a.add(1);
a.get(0);
a.set(0, 9);
remove / size / clear
Remove by index, length, empty.
a.remove(0);
a.size();
a.clear();
contains
Membership test.
a.contains(5);  // true/false
HashMap methods
put / get / containsKey
Set, read, and check existence.
Map<String,Integer> m = new HashMap<>();
m.put("a", 1);
m.get("a");
m.containsKey("a");
remove / keySet / values
Remove and iterate.
m.remove("a");
for (String k : m.keySet()) { ... }
for (Integer v : m.values()) { ... }
entrySet
Iterate pairs.
for (Map.Entry<String,Integer> e : m.entrySet()){
  e.getKey(); e.getValue();
}
Streams
stream + filter + collect
Filter to a new list.
List<Integer> pos = nums.stream()
  .filter(n -> n > 0)
  .collect(Collectors.toList());
map
Transform each element.
List<Integer> squared = nums.stream()
  .map(n -> n*n)
  .collect(Collectors.toList());
count
Count matches.
long c = nums.stream()
  .filter(n -> n > 0)
  .count();
try-with-resources
Auto-close
Resources closed automatically.
try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(
        new FileReader("f.txt"))) {
    String line;
    while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
        System.out.println(line);
    }
}
Common patterns
Enhanced for loop
For-each over any iterable.
for (String s : list) {
    System.out.println(s);
}
var (Java 10+)
Local type inference.
var list = new ArrayList<String>();
var count = 42;
record (Java 14+)
Immutable data class.
public record Point(int x, int y) {}
var p = new Point(1, 2);
p.x(); // 1
cherry code › references › comparison
Languages Comparison
Same concepts across Python, JavaScript and Java — side by side.
ConceptPythonJavaScriptJava
Printprint(x)console.log(x)System.out.println(x)
Comment line# ...// ...// ...
Comment block""" ... """/* ... *//* ... */
Variablex = 5let x = 5int x = 5;
ConstantX = 5 (convention)const x = 5final int X = 5;
Ifif x:if (x) {}if (x) {}
For loopfor i in range(10):for (let i=0;i<10;i++)for (int i=0;i<10;i++)
For-eachfor x in lst:for (let x of arr)for (Type x : arr)
Functiondef f(x):function f(x){} or (x)=>{}static T f(T x){}
Array[1,2,3][1,2,3]int[] a = {1,2,3};
Lengthlen(x)x.lengthx.length (arr) / x.length() (str)
String concat'a' + 'b''a' + 'b'"a" + "b"
String formatf"{x}"`${x}`String.format("%s", x)
Boolean trueTruetruetrue
NullNonenull / undefinednull
Classclass Foo:class Foo {}class Foo {}
Methoddef m(self):m() {}void m() {}
InstanceFoo()new Foo()new Foo()
Try/catchtry: ... except E:try {} catch (e) {}try {} catch (E e) {}
cherry code › references › anti-patterns
Anti-patterns
Red flags to avoid — each shows BAD vs GOOD.
God Class
A single class that does everything — mixed responsibilities, impossible to maintain.
BADclass App { loadUser() {}; saveDB() {}; sendEmail() {}; renderUI() {}; computeTax() {}; logIO() {}; }
GOODclass User {...} class Mailer {...} class TaxCalc {...} // each has one reason to change
Magic Numbers
Unexplained literals scattered through the code. Future-you won't remember what they mean.
BADif (elapsed > 86400) { expire(); }
GOODconst SECONDS_PER_DAY = 86400; if (elapsed > SECONDS_PER_DAY) { expire(); }
Copy-Paste Programming
Duplicating blocks instead of extracting a function. A bug fix now needs N edits.
BADtotal1 = price1 * 1.08; total2 = price2 * 1.08; total3 = price3 * 1.08;
GOODdef with_tax(p): return p * 1.08 totals = [with_tax(p) for p in prices]
No Tests
Shipping code without verification. Every change is a gamble.
BAD// "I checked it manually, // looks good, push to prod"
GOODdef test_add(): assert add(2,3) == 5 assert add(-1,1) == 0
Premature Optimization
Micro-optimizing before measuring. You sacrifice clarity for imaginary speed.
BAD// hand-rolled bit tricks // before profiling shows // they're a bottleneck
GOOD// write clearly first // profile, then optimize // the actual hot path
Cryptic Names
Single letters and abbreviations everywhere. Code becomes a puzzle.
BADdef calc(x, y, tmp): d = x - y mgr = d * tmp return mgr
GOODdef discount(price, base, rate): diff = price - base return diff * rate
Long Functions
200+ lines doing many things. Hard to read, test, or reuse.
BADdef process(): # 250 lines of mixed # validation + parsing # + saving + emailing ...
GOODdef process(): data = validate(input) rec = parse(data) save(rec) notify(rec)
Deeply Nested Code
5+ indentation levels. Use early returns or extract helpers.
BADif a: if b: if c: if d: run()
GOODif not a: return if not b: return if not c: return if not d: return run()
Catching All Exceptions
A bare except swallows real bugs — you lose the signal.
BADtry: risky() except: pass # silent fail!
GOODtry: risky() except NetworkError as e: log.warning(e) retry()
Global Mutable State
Shared globals modified everywhere. Bugs become impossible to trace.
BADcounter = 0 def add(): global counter counter += 1 # called from 30 places
GOODclass Counter: def __init__(self): self.n = 0 def add(self): self.n += 1
Comment Repeats Code
Comments that restate the obvious add noise instead of context.
BADi = i + 1 # increment i x = [] # empty list
GOOD# Skip the header row i = i + 1 # Collected matches go here x = []
Boolean Trap
Several positional booleans — impossible to read at the call site.
BADsetVisible(true, false, true); // what do these mean?
GOODsetVisible({ visible: true, animate: false, focus: true });
Stringly-Typed
Passing flags as raw strings instead of enums/constants. Typos compile fine.
BADorder.status = "PENDIGN" // silently wrong forever
GOODclass Status(Enum): PENDING = "pending" DONE = "done" order.status = Status.PENDING
Tight Coupling
Module A reaches into B's internals. Changing B breaks A.
BAD// reach into private field total = order._items[0].priv;
GOOD// use the public API total = order.getTotal();
Ignoring Warnings
Compiler/linter warnings exist for a reason. Each ignored one is a future bug.
BAD// 247 warnings, who cares // just push and ship
GOOD// CI fails on new warnings // every warning is read // and either fixed or // explicitly suppressed
cherry code › references › method
Study Method
An 8-step method to actually learn programming — not just read it.
01
Read the concept first (1 min)
Understand WHAT something is before HOW to write it. Read the term's definition slowly, in your own words.
02
Predict the code (2 min)
Before opening the example, guess what the snippet will look like. Right or wrong, this primes your memory far more than passive reading.
03
Read the code examples (3 min)
Now compare your prediction with reality. Surprises are the most valuable signal — pay attention to them.
04
Type it yourself (5 min)
Open a REPL or editor and type the snippet character by character. Muscle memory matters. Copy-paste does not stick.
05
Modify, break, fix (5 min)
Change values. Remove a line. See what breaks and read the error. Reading errors teaches the language better than reading working code.
06
Compare languages (3 min)
Use the language tabs to look at the same concept in Python, JavaScript and Java. Spotting the shared idea behind the syntax is what makes you a polyglot.
07
Tag the term (1 min)
Mark each term as Studied, Review, Hard, or Favorite. Future-you needs this to know what to revisit.
08
Move on — come back later
Don't perfect it now. Spaced repetition beats one-shot mastery. Hit Review Mode tomorrow and the day after.
cherry code › goals
Goals & Progress
Define your learning objectives. Track what matters. Celebrate small wins.
① Counter
0 / 0 goals completed
② Add a goal
③ Suggested goals
④ Your goals
cherry code › statistics
Statistics Dashboard
A snapshot of everything you have tagged, studied, and quizzed.
① Overview
② Quizzes
③ Blocks
Progress Rings
cherry code › progress
Manage Progress
Export your progress to a file, import it on another device, or wipe everything and start fresh.
① Export

Download all your tags, notes, goals and quiz scores as a single .cherry.json file.

② Import

Restore progress from a previously exported file. You will be asked to confirm before anything is written.

③ Reset

Danger zone. This will permanently delete all your tags, notes, goals, and quiz scores from this browser.

cherry code › visuals › execution
How Code Runs
From the text you type to the instructions your CPU actually executes.
The Execution Pipeline
Source Code

The human-readable text you write.

→
Lexer / Tokenizer

Splits text into tokens (keywords, names, symbols).

→
Parser (AST)

Builds a tree showing the structure & grammar.

→
Compiler / Interpreter

Translates the tree into lower-level instructions.

→
Bytecode / Machine Code

Compact instructions a machine can read.

→
CPU Executes

Hardware runs the instructions, step by step.

Compiled vs Interpreted

Compiled

Java · C · C++ · Rust · Go

The whole program is translated ahead of time into machine code (or bytecode). You compile once, then run the result many times. Errors surface at compile time; execution is fast.

Interpreted

Python · JavaScript · Ruby

An interpreter reads and runs the program line by line at runtime — no separate build step. Faster to iterate, but slower per run, and many errors only appear when that line executes.

Reading It

The line between the two blurs in practice: Python compiles to bytecode before interpreting it, and JavaScript engines use JIT compilation to turn hot code into machine code on the fly. What matters is when the translation happens — before you run (compiled) or while you run (interpreted).

cherry code › visuals › call stack
The Call Stack
How a program keeps track of which function is running and where to return.
A Small Example
main() → calls greet() → calls format()
format()
locals: text · return → greet()
greet()
locals: name · return → main()
main()
locals: user · return → runtime
↓ stack base · frames pushed on top ↑
How It Works

Every time a function is called, a new stack frame is pushed on top. Each frame holds that call's local variables and a return address — the spot to jump back to when it finishes.

When a function returns, its frame is popped off and control unwinds to the caller below it. The stack shrinks back down — last in, first out.

Stack overflow happens when recursion goes too deep (or never stops): frames keep stacking until the stack runs out of memory and the program crashes. That's why recursive functions need a base case.

cherry code › visuals › data structures
Data Structures Map
The everyday containers, grouped by shape — and when to reach for each.
Linear
Array

Fixed slots, instant index access.

Linked List

Cheap inserts/removes, no random access.

List

Growable, ordered, general purpose.

Stack

LIFO — undo, call frames, backtracking.

Queue

FIFO — tasks, buffers, scheduling.

Map-based
Dict / Map

Key → value lookups, fast by key.

Set

Unique items, fast membership tests.

Hash Table

Engine behind dicts/sets — O(1) average.

Hierarchical
Tree

Hierarchies — files, parsing, search trees.

Graph

Networks — maps, social, dependencies.

Reading It

Pick by the operation you do most. Need fast lookup by key? A dict. Order matters and you append a lot? A list. Need uniqueness? A set. Most data structures are trade-offs: speed for one operation usually costs speed in another.

cherry code › visuals › big-o
Big-O Complexity
How an algorithm's cost grows as the input gets bigger.
O(1) constant O(log n) O(n) O(n log n) O(n²) O(2ⁿ)
Common Operations
OperationComplexityCost
Array access by indexO(1)great
Hash / dict lookup (avg)O(1)great
Binary search (sorted)O(log n)great
Linear searchO(n)ok
Merge / quick sort (avg)O(n log n)ok
Bubble / insertion sortO(n²)slow
Nested loop over pairsO(n²)slow
Naive recursive subsetsO(2ⁿ)slow
Reading It

Big-O describes the shape of growth, not exact timing. We drop constants — O(2n) is just O(n). What matters is how cost scales: doubling the input barely touches an O(log n) algorithm but quadruples an O(n²) one. At large n, the curve is everything.

cherry code › visuals › oop
OOP Relationships
The four pillars, drawn out — class, inheritance, encapsulation, polymorphism.

Class → Objects

A class is a blueprint; an object is a concrete instance built from it. One class, many objects — each with its own data.

Inheritance

Dog and Cat inherit shared behaviour from Animal (eat, sleep) and add their own. Reuse without copy-paste.

Encapsulation

Private data is hidden behind public methods. Outside code can call deposit() but can't touch balance directly — the object guards its own state.

Polymorphism

The same method call behaves differently depending on the object's type. Call .speak() on any animal and each one answers in its own way.

Reading It

These four pillars work together: encapsulation hides the messy details, inheritance shares the common ones, polymorphism lets you treat different types uniformly, and classes tie it all into reusable blueprints. Good OOP is about modelling your problem, not adding boxes for their own sake.

cherry code › resources › learning
Learning Resources
Where to actually practice — picked platforms, ranked by friendliness.
Recommended order for beginners ✦  CS50 → freeCodeCamp → The Odin Project → LeetCode (easy). Build the fundamentals, then a real project path, then drill problem-solving.
Foundations & Curricula
CS50 (Harvard)Beginnercs50.harvard.edu ↗
Harvard's legendary intro to computer science. Free, lectures + problem sets covering C, Python, SQL, algorithms and how computers actually work.
✦ Best for building real CS fundamentals from scratch.
freeCodeCampBeginnerfreecodecamp.org ↗
Free, full curriculum with thousands of hands-on exercises and certifications — web dev, data, Python, and more. Browser-based, no install.
✦ Best for a structured, project-driven first path.
The Odin ProjectBeginnertheodinproject.com ↗
Free, project-based full-stack web development path (HTML/CSS, JS, Ruby/Node). Teaches you to use real tools and build a portfolio.
✦ Best for going from basics to employable web dev.
Practice & Challenges
LeetCodeEasy → Hardleetcode.com ↗
The standard for algorithm & data-structure practice and coding-interview prep. Thousands of problems graded by difficulty, with discussions.
✦ Best for interview prep — start with the Easy set.
HackerRankPracticehackerrank.com ↗
Practice tracks and challenges across languages, plus skill certifications used by employers. Gentler ramp than LeetCode for many topics.
✦ Best for warming up and broad language drills.
ExercismMentoredexercism.org ↗
Free exercises across 70+ languages with optional human mentoring and code feedback. Focuses on idiomatic, clean solutions.
✦ Best for learning a new language the right way.
CodewarsGamifiedcodewars.com ↗
Bite-sized "kata" challenges with belts and ranks. Compare your solution against others after solving — great for sharpening fluency.
✦ Best for daily, gamified short practice.
Reference & Docs
MDN Web DocsReferencedeveloper.mozilla.org ↗
The authoritative reference for JavaScript, HTML, CSS and web APIs. Accurate, example-rich, and the first place to check how something works.
✦ Best for looking up anything web/JS.
Python.org docs / Real PythonReferencedocs.python.org · realpython.com ↗
The official Python docs for precise language/library detail, plus Real Python's tutorials for clear, practical walkthroughs of Python topics.
✦ Best for Python reference and deeper how-tos.
